restrictions.name: McHenry County
restrictions.title: Crystal Lake — Chicago NW Exurb and ComEd Territory
restrictions.description: McHenry County (Crystal Lake, Woodstock) is Chicago's northwestern exurban frontier where ComEd's transmission grid has supported large-load industrial users. Lower industrial land costs than DuPage or Lake counties, I-90 corridor connectivity, Wisconsin border proximity, and Illinois' data center incentive program make it an emerging location for hyperscale-adjacent data center development.

restrictions.name: Kankakee County
restrictions.title: Kankakee — Chicago Southern Exurb and ComEd/Ameren Boundary
restrictions.description: Kankakee County sits at the southern edge of the Chicago metro along I-57. ComEd service (overlapping with Ameren boundary), lower industrial land costs than Will or Grundy County while retaining metro Chicago fiber access, I-57 corridor connectivity, and available industrial land along the Kankakee River industrial corridor make it a potential data center site for Chicago-market operators seeking lower-cost options.

sources.label: Illinois Department of Commerce — High Impact Business

restrictions.name: LaSalle County
restrictions.title: Ottawa-Peru — I-80 Backbone and Illinois Valley Nuclear Generation
restrictions.description: LaSalle County (Ottawa, Peru, Streator) anchors the Illinois Valley corridor along I-80 between Chicago and the Quad Cities. The county hosts the LaSalle County Generating Station (two Exelon nuclear units), providing proof of large-scale power infrastructure. ComEd service, Illinois' High Impact Business data center incentive, I-80/I-39 interchange, and Exelon's nuclear-rich grid make it a candidate for power-intensive data center deployments.

restrictions.name: Tompkins County
restrictions.title: Ithaca — Cornell University AI Research and NYSEG Service
restrictions.description: Tompkins County (Ithaca) hosts Cornell University, one of the leading AI and computing research universities globally, including the Cornell Ann S. Bowers College of Computing and Information Science and the Cornell AI for Science initiative. NYSEG (Avangrid) service with access to New York's Excelsior Jobs Program, Cornell's growing data center infrastructure, and the county's concentration of spin-out AI companies make it a significant research computing market.

sources.label: Cornell University Computing and Information Science
New York State Excelsior Jobs Program

restrictions.name: Dutchess County
restrictions.title: Poughkeepsie — IBM Global Headquarters and Hudson Valley Tech Corridor
restrictions.description: Dutchess County (Poughkeepsie, Fishkill) hosts IBM's world headquarters and the IBM Research facility at Yorktown Heights (Westchester/Dutchess border). IBM's semiconductor fabrication in East Fishkill and its extensive data center infrastructure along the Route 9/Taconic corridor have established Dutchess County as one of the most significant enterprise computing locations in the US. Central Hudson Gas & Electric service and NY's Excelsior program support the county.

sources.label: New York State Excelsior Jobs Program

restrictions.name: Rockland County
restrictions.title: Rockland County — NYC Northern Near Suburb and Orange & Rockland Service
restrictions.description: Rockland County (New City, Nanuet, Orangeburg) sits on the west bank of the Hudson River just north of the Tappan Zee Bridge, giving it direct NYC metro access with more industrial land availability. Orange & Rockland Utilities (Con Edison subsidiary) service, growing technology employer base moving from Manhattan, Route 287 connectivity, and New York's data center tax incentive program make it an established secondary market in the NYC metro.

sources.label: New York State Excelsior Jobs Program

restrictions.name: Chautauqua County
restrictions.title: Dunkirk-Jamestown — Lake Erie Industrial and NYPA Niagara Proximity
restrictions.description: Chautauqua County (Jamestown, Dunkirk, Fredonia) anchors southwestern New York on Lake Erie. National Grid service with access to NYPA's economic development power programs drawing on cheap Niagara Falls hydroelectric generation, former NRG Dunkirk power plant site industrial infrastructure, and the county's lower-cost industrial real estate compared to Erie County make it a niche but viable market for power-intensive data center workloads.

restrictions.name: Passaic County
restrictions.title: Paterson-Wayne — NJ Industrial Corridor and PSEG Service
restrictions.description: Passaic County (Paterson, Wayne, Clifton, Passaic) is one of New Jersey's most densely populated and industrially active counties along the I-80 corridor. PSEG New Jersey service with the state's high-reliability transmission infrastructure, Route 46 and I-80 fiber crossings, growing tech employer base in Wayne Township, and New Jersey's ERG grant program make it an active secondary data center market.

sources.label: Passaic County Department of Planning and Economic Development

restrictions.name: Gloucester County
restrictions.title: Woodbury-Washington Township — Philadelphia South NJ and PSEG Service
restrictions.description: Gloucester County (Woodbury, Washington Township, Deptford) anchors the southern New Jersey corridor between Camden and Salem along I-295. PSEG New Jersey service with high reliability, growing pharmaceutical and healthcare IT demand, Route 55 industrial corridor, lower land costs than Camden County, and New Jersey's data center incentive programs make it a growing location for Philadelphia-metro data center deployment.

restrictions.name: Solano County
restrictions.title: Fairfield-Vacaville — I-80 Corridor and Travis AFB Digital Infrastructure
restrictions.description: Solano County (Fairfield, Vacaville, Vallejo, Benicia) straddles the I-80 Bay Area to Sacramento corridor and hosts Travis Air Force Base. PG&E service, industrial sites along I-80 with direct Bay Area and Sacramento fiber rings, California's data center sales tax exemption (Rev. & Tax. Code § 6377.1), and the county's strategic military and logistics infrastructure make it an established secondary Bay Area data center market.

sources.label: California Governor's Office of Business and Economic Development — Data Centers

restrictions.name: Sonoma County
restrictions.title: Santa Rosa — North Bay Tech and PG&E Service Territory
restrictions.description: Sonoma County (Santa Rosa, Petaluma, Rohnert Park) hosts a growing tech and biotech corridor in the North Bay, anchored by Keysight Technologies and Agilent legacy employers. PG&E service with California's data center sales tax incentive, Route 101 fiber connectivity north of Marin, and a professional workforce drawing from Bay Area spillover make it a secondary Northern California data center market.

sources.label: California Governor's Office of Business and Economic Development

restrictions.name: Butte County
restrictions.title: Chico — CSU Chico Research Computing and PG&E North Valley Grid
restrictions.description: Butte County (Chico, Oroville) hosts California State University, Chico, with growing research computing and data science programs. PG&E service, available industrial land at substantially lower costs than Bay Area or Sacramento markets, California Highway 99 connectivity, and the county's rebuilding economy following the 2018 Camp Fire create data center development opportunities in the Northern Sacramento Valley.

sources.label: California Governor's Office of Business and Economic Development

restrictions.name: Midland County
restrictions.title: Midland — Dow Chemical Global HQ and Consumers Energy Midland Nuclear Site
restrictions.description: Midland County hosts Dow Chemical's global headquarters and was home to the Midland Nuclear Power Plant site (now Midland Cogeneration Venture, one of the largest natural gas cogeneration facilities in the US). Consumers Energy service with large industrial power history, Dow's massive enterprise IT infrastructure, and Michigan's data center incentive program (MCL § 207.803) make Midland a niche but significant data center market for chemical and industrial AI workloads.

restrictions.name: Calhoun County
restrictions.title: Battle Creek — Kellogg's, Post Foods, and Consumers Energy Service
restrictions.description: Calhoun County (Battle Creek, Albion, Marshall) hosts Kellogg's and Post Consumer Brands' production facilities, a significant US Army Garrison at Fort Custer, and the Battle Creek Executive Airport. Consumers Energy service with competitive industrial rates, I-94 corridor connectivity between Detroit and Chicago, Michigan's data center incentive program, and available industrial land at low cost make it a regional enterprise data center market.

restrictions.name: Eaton County
restrictions.title: Charlotte-Lansing Suburb — Michigan State Capital Western Ring and Consumers Energy
restrictions.description: Eaton County (Charlotte, Delta Township, Grand Ledge) forms the western suburban ring of the Lansing MSA. Consumers Energy service, proximity to Michigan state government IT infrastructure in adjacent Ingham County, I-96 connectivity to Grand Rapids and Detroit, and lower industrial land costs than Ingham County make it a secondary market for Lansing-area data center deployment.

restrictions.name: Grayson County
restrictions.title: Sherman-Denison — Texas Semiconductor Hub and ONCOR/TDU Territory
restrictions.description: Grayson County (Sherman, Denison) emerged as one of the most significant US semiconductor and advanced manufacturing destinations following announcements from Texas Instruments (300mm fab) and GlobalFoundries expansion. High-power industrial infrastructure, Oncor Electric Delivery competitive rates, US 75 and US 82 connectivity to Dallas metro 60 miles south, Texas Enterprise Zone Program, and no-income-tax advantage position Grayson County as a rapidly growing data center and chip manufacturing-adjacent market.

restrictions.name: Craven County
restrictions.title: New Bern-Cherry Point — MCAS Cherry Point and Eastern NC Industrial
restrictions.description: Craven County (New Bern, Havelock) hosts Marine Corps Air Station Cherry Point, a major USMC aviation and Fleet Readiness Center, and the UNC Health Vidant facility serving Eastern NC. Duke Energy Progress service with reliable coastal grid connectivity, North Carolina's Data Center Act (NCGS § 105-164.13(55)) sales tax exemption on equipment, and the county's industrial base supporting military and healthcare IT make it a secondary Eastern NC data center market.

sources.label: North Carolina Department of Commerce — Data Centers

restrictions.name: Nueces County
restrictions.title: Corpus Christi — Port Industrial Hub and AEP Texas Territory
restrictions.description: Nueces County (Corpus Christi) anchors the Texas Coastal Bend with the Port of Corpus Christi, a major LNG and petrochemical export hub. AEP Texas (West) service territory with large industrial transmission infrastructure, significant data center demand from oil/gas analytics and refinery automation, Texas Enterprise Zone Program, no-income-tax advantage, and the county's growing LNG tech corridor create a secondary Texas data center market.

sources.label: Corpus Christi Regional Economic Development Corporation

restrictions.name: Hidalgo County
restrictions.title: McAllen-Edinburg — Rio Grande Valley's Fastest-Growing Metro
restrictions.description: Hidalgo County (McAllen, Edinburg, Pharr) is the largest county in the Rio Grande Valley and one of the fastest-growing metros in the United States. AEP Texas (Central) service, Texas' no-income-tax and data center equipment tax exemption, University of Texas Rio Grande Valley creating a tech workforce, and the county's cross-border trade infrastructure with Mexico driving enterprise IT demand make it an emerging Texas data center market.

restrictions.name: Potter County
restrictions.title: Amarillo — Xcel Energy Wind Hub and Texas Panhandle Industrial
restrictions.description: Potter County (Amarillo) anchors the Texas Panhandle and sits in one of the richest wind energy corridors in the US. Southwestern Public Service (Xcel Energy) service with among the lowest commercial electricity rates in Texas driven by massive wind generation, I-40 connectivity, the Pantex nuclear weapons plant proximity creating defense data infrastructure demand, and Texas' data center tax exemption make Amarillo a compelling data center destination for power-intensive workloads.

restrictions.name: Jefferson County
restrictions.title: Beaumont-Port Arthur — Southeast Texas Petrochemical Hub and Entergy Texas
restrictions.description: Jefferson County (Beaumont, Port Arthur, Nederland) anchors the Southeast Texas petrochemical corridor with the largest refinery concentration in the US east of Houston. Entergy Texas service with competitive industrial rates and large-load history, Texas' data center tax exemption, I-10 connectivity to Houston, and the county's established industrial power infrastructure supporting refinery automation and AI analytics create a niche but significant data center market.

restrictions.name: Wichita County
restrictions.title: Wichita Falls — North Texas Hub, Sheppard AFB, and Oncor Territory
restrictions.description: Wichita County (Wichita Falls) is the primary commercial center for North Texas near the Oklahoma border. Oncor Electric Delivery service, Sheppard Air Force Base (USAF pilot training center) driving military data infrastructure demand, Midwestern State University, Texas' data center tax exemption, and the county's role as a regional healthcare IT hub for North Texas make it a secondary data center market.

restrictions.name: Citrus County
restrictions.title: Crystal River — Duke Energy Florida Nuclear Site and High-Power Infrastructure
restrictions.description: Citrus County (Crystal River, Homosassa) hosts the Crystal River Nuclear Plant (recently decommissioned Duke Energy Florida facility) and the Crystal River Energy Complex (coal to gas conversion), establishing a county with exceptional industrial power transmission infrastructure. Duke Energy Florida service, Florida's data center equipment sales tax exemption (F.S. § 212.08(5)(f)), and available industrial sites near large-capacity transmission substations make it a distinctive large-load data center candidate.

restrictions.name: Flagler County
restrictions.title: Palm Coast — Northeast Florida Growth Corridor and FPL/Duke Service
restrictions.description: Flagler County (Palm Coast, Bunnell) is one of Florida's fastest-growing counties between St. Johns and Volusia. Florida Power & Light and Duke Energy Florida service, Florida's data center equipment sales tax exemption, I-95 fiber connectivity, and rapidly expanding commercial and industrial development at Palm Coast make it an emerging secondary data center market as the Northeast Florida/Jacksonville corridor continues to grow.

restrictions.name: Clay County
restrictions.title: Orange Park — Jacksonville SW Suburb and JEA/Duke Energy Territory
restrictions.description: Clay County (Orange Park, Fleming Island, Middleburg) anchors the southwestern Jacksonville metro and is one of Florida's fastest-growing suburban counties. JEA (Jacksonville Electric Authority) and Duke Energy Florida service, Florida's data center equipment sales tax exemption, I-295 and US 17 corridor connectivity to Jacksonville's downtown fiber hub, and the county's rapidly expanding commercial real estate market make it a growing data center location.

restrictions.name: Whitfield County
restrictions.title: Dalton — Carpet Capital of the World and TVA/MEAG Power Hub
restrictions.description: Whitfield County (Dalton) is the global center of carpet and flooring manufacturing, hosting Shaw Industries, Mohawk Industries, and many major manufacturing facilities with exceptionally large power loads. TVA power (via Municipal Electric Authority of Georgia), some of the lowest industrial electricity rates in Georgia, I-75 corridor fiber connectivity, and Georgia's data center equipment sales tax exemption (O.C.G.A. § 48-8-3.2) make it a distinctive market for manufacturing-adjacent data center deployment.

restrictions.name: Lowndes County
restrictions.title: Valdosta — South Georgia Regional Hub and Georgia Power Service
restrictions.description: Lowndes County (Valdosta) is the commercial and medical hub for South Georgia and anchors a region stretching from the Florida border to Albany. Georgia Power service, Moody Air Force Base driving military IT demand, Valdosta State University, Georgia's data center sales tax exemption, I-75 corridor connectivity, and the county's position as a regional healthcare and logistics hub create data center demand.

restrictions.name: Walton County
restrictions.title: Monroe — Atlanta Eastern Exurb and Walton EMC Territory
restrictions.description: Walton County (Monroe) sits on Atlanta's rapidly expanding eastern exurban frontier along US 78, adjacent to Newton County (Covington). Walton EMC (electric membership cooperative) service, lower industrial land costs than the closer-in Atlanta suburban counties, Georgia's data center equipment sales tax exemption, and the county's explosive residential and commercial growth driven by Atlanta metro expansion make it a data center development candidate.

restrictions.name: Litchfield County
restrictions.title: Torrington — NW Connecticut Rural Broadband and Eversource Territory
restrictions.description: Litchfield County (Torrington, Winsted, Litchfield) completes Connecticut's 8-county coverage as the state's northwestern rural county. Eversource Energy service, Connecticut's data center tax exemption (CGS § 12-412(99)), Route 8 industrial corridor, and the county's role as a destination for Hartford and NYC metro residents creating residential tech demand contribute to limited but present data center consideration in this primarily rural CT market.

sources.label: Connecticut Department of Economic and Community Development

restrictions.name: Grafton County
restrictions.title: Hanover-Lebanon — Dartmouth College and Upper Valley Tech Hub
restrictions.description: Grafton County (Hanover, Lebanon, Plymouth) hosts Dartmouth College, one of the leading AI and computing research institutions in New England. Eversource Energy service, New Hampshire's no-sales-tax and no-income-tax advantage, the Upper Valley biotech and tech cluster along I-89/I-91, and Dartmouth's expanding AI and machine learning research programs (Dartmouth AI) create data center and research computing demand in the Upper Connecticut River Valley.

sources.label: Dartmouth College — Department of Computer Science
New Hampshire Division of Economic Development

restrictions.name: Androscoggin County
restrictions.title: Lewiston-Auburn — Maine's Second Metro and Central Maine Power Service
restrictions.description: Androscoggin County (Lewiston, Auburn) is Maine's second-largest metropolitan area and a historic textile and manufacturing center undergoing economic revitalization. Central Maine Power (Avangrid) service, Maine's data center property tax exemption, the Lewiston-Auburn metro's improving fiber connectivity, and the county's available industrial mill buildings being repurposed for technology uses create a data center opportunity in Maine's secondary market.

sources.label: Maine Department of Economic and Community Development

restrictions.name: York County
restrictions.title: Biddeford-Saco-Kittery — Southern Maine and Boston Metro Spillover
restrictions.description: York County (Biddeford, Saco, Sanford, Kittery, York) anchors Southern Maine and is the state's fastest-growing county, driven by Boston metro spillover across the Piscataqua River. Eversource Energy service, Maine's data center property tax incentive, the US 1/I-95 corridor connecting Portland to Boston, University of New England's healthcare tech programs, and rapidly rising commercial development in the Biddeford-Saco corridor create growing data center demand.

sources.label: Maine Department of Economic and Community Development

restrictions.name: Medina County
restrictions.title: Medina — Rapidly Growing Cleveland-Akron Exurb and FirstEnergy Territory
restrictions.description: Medina County (Medina, Brunswick) is one of Ohio's fastest-growing suburban counties, positioned between Cleveland and Akron on I-71. FirstEnergy (Ohio Edison) service, Ohio's data center sales tax exemption (R.C. § 5739.02(B)), lower industrial land costs than Summit or Cuyahoga, and strong I-71 and US 42 fiber corridor access make it an emerging data center market for the Cleveland-Akron metro.

restrictions.name: Wood County
restrictions.title: Bowling Green-Perrysburg — Toledo South Suburb and AEP Ohio Territory
restrictions.description: Wood County (Bowling Green, Perrysburg, Rossford) anchors the southwestern Toledo MSA and hosts Bowling Green State University. AEP Ohio service with competitive industrial rates, Ohio's data center equipment sales tax exemption, I-75 corridor connectivity between Toledo and Columbus, and the Davis-Besse Nuclear Power Station in neighboring Ottawa County providing large-scale power infrastructure proof make it a viable secondary data center market.

restrictions.name: Trumbull County
restrictions.title: Warren — Northeast Ohio Industrial and FirstEnergy/AEP Territory
restrictions.description: Trumbull County (Warren, Niles, Cortland) anchors the northeastern Ohio industrial corridor north of Mahoning County (Youngstown). FirstEnergy (Ohio Edison) and AEP Ohio service with large industrial transmission infrastructure, Ohio's data center equipment sales tax exemption, I-80/I-11 connectivity, and the county's legacy steel manufacturing sites providing available large-footprint industrial land make it a secondary data center option for the Youngstown-Warren market.

restrictions.name: Cole County
restrictions.title: Jefferson City — Missouri State Capital Government IT Hub
restrictions.description: Cole County (Jefferson City) is the seat of Missouri state government and hosts significant state government IT infrastructure including the Missouri Office of Administration's Division of Information Technology. AmerenUE service, Missouri's data center sales tax exemption, I-70/US 54 connectivity, and the concentration of state agencies and regulatory bodies driving enterprise IT demand make it a secondary Missouri data center market for government cloud and compliance workloads.

sources.label: Jefferson City Area Chamber of Commerce
Missouri Department of Economic Development — Data Centers

restrictions.name: Phelps County
restrictions.title: Rolla — Missouri S&T University and Defense Research Computing
restrictions.description: Phelps County (Rolla) hosts Missouri University of Science and Technology (Missouri S&T), one of the leading STEM universities in the Midwest with significant cybersecurity, AI, and geospatial computing programs. Empire District Electric (Liberty Utilities) service, US 63/I-44 corridor connectivity, proximity to Fort Leonard Wood, and Missouri's data center incentive program create research computing and defense-adjacent IT demand.

sources.label: Missouri University of Science and Technology

restrictions.name: Montgomery County
restrictions.title: Blacksburg — Virginia Tech, ICTAS, and Appalachian Power Service
restrictions.description: Montgomery County (Blacksburg, Radford) hosts Virginia Tech, one of the nation's leading cybersecurity and engineering universities and home to the Virginia Tech Transportation Institute, the Hume Center for National Security and Technology, and the Institute for Critical Technology and Applied Science (ICTAS). Appalachian Power (AEP) service, Virginia's Data Center Investment Grant (DCIG) program, I-81 corridor connectivity, and Virginia Tech's growing AI and HPC research programs create significant data center and research computing demand.

sources.label: Virginia Economic Development Partnership — Data Centers

restrictions.name: King George County
restrictions.title: Dahlgren — Naval Surface Warfare Center and Dominion Transmission Corridor
restrictions.description: King George County (Dahlgren) hosts the Naval Surface Warfare Center Dahlgren Division (NSWCDD), the Navy's primary site for weapon systems engineering, combat systems development, and advanced cyber research. Dominion Energy Virginia service, the county's proximity to NOVA data center clusters just 60 miles away via US 301, Virginia's Data Center Investment Grant program, and NSWCDD's extensive classified computing infrastructure create significant federal data center demand.

sources.label: Naval Surface Warfare Center Dahlgren Division
Virginia Economic Development Partnership — Data Centers

restrictions.name: Roanoke County
restrictions.title: Roanoke County — Suburban Roanoke and Appalachian Power Service
restrictions.description: Roanoke County (Salem, Vinton, Cave Spring) surrounds Roanoke city and is distinct from it under Virginia's independent city structure. Appalachian Power (AEP) service, lower industrial land costs than the city, I-81 corridor connectivity, Carilion health system's regional IT demand, Virginia's Data Center Investment Grant program, and available industrial parks (Valley View, Explore Park) make it a secondary western Virginia data center market.

restrictions.name: Prince George County
restrictions.title: Hopewell-Prince George — Fort Gregg-Adams and Army Logistics
restrictions.description: Prince George County (Hopewell) hosts Fort Gregg-Adams, the Army's Quartermaster Center and School and a major Army logistics command. Dominion Energy Virginia service with a major Hopewell transmission hub, I-95 corridor connectivity 25 miles south of Richmond, Virginia's Data Center Investment Grant program, and the county's industrial base supporting military IT and logistics enterprise computing create data center demand in this Richmond-Petersburg corridor.

sources.label: Virginia Economic Development Partnership — Data Centers

restrictions.name: Harford County
restrictions.title: Aberdeen — Aberdeen Proving Ground and Army Research Laboratory
restrictions.description: Harford County (Bel Air, Aberdeen, Havre de Grace) hosts Aberdeen Proving Ground, one of the US Army's most significant testing and research installations and home to the Army Research Laboratory (ARL), the Army's largest corporate laboratory. BGE (Exelon) service, Maryland's data center sales tax exemption, I-95 corridor connectivity between Baltimore and Philadelphia, and ARL's massive AI and cybersecurity research programs create substantial classified and enterprise data center demand.

sources.label: US Army Research Laboratory — Aberdeen Proving Ground

restrictions.name: Frederick County
restrictions.title: Frederick — Fort Detrick, NIST, and I-270 Technology Corridor
restrictions.description: Frederick County (Frederick) hosts Fort Detrick (US Army Medical Research), the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) Gaithersburg campus (just south in Montgomery), and anchors the I-270 Technology Corridor between DC and Hagerstown. Potomac Edison (FirstEnergy)/BGE service, Maryland's data center sales tax exemption, growing commercial development north of Montgomery County, and the county's concentration of federal bio/tech agencies create significant enterprise and classified data center demand.

sources.label: Frederick County Office of Economic Development
Maryland Department of Commerce — Data Centers

restrictions.name: Washington County
restrictions.title: Hagerstown — I-70/I-81 Junction and Dominion/Potomac Edison Service
restrictions.description: Washington County (Hagerstown) sits at the critical I-70/I-81 junction, one of the most strategically positioned logistics crossroads in the eastern US. Potomac Edison (FirstEnergy) service, Maryland's data center sales tax exemption, lower industrial land costs than the Montgomery-Frederick corridor, and the county's growing distribution and e-commerce facility investment driven by its crossroads location make it an emerging secondary Maryland data center market.

restrictions.name: Bibb County
restrictions.title: Macon — Middle Georgia Hub and MEAG/Georgia Power Service
restrictions.description: Bibb County (Macon) is the commercial, medical, and cultural center for Middle Georgia. Georgia Power/MEAG service, Mercer University and Middle Georgia State University creating tech workforce, I-75/I-16 interchange at the geographic center of Georgia, Georgia's data center equipment sales tax exemption (O.C.G.A. § 48-8-3.2), and the county's position as a regional logistics and healthcare IT hub make it a secondary data center market for central Georgia.

sources.label: Middle Georgia Regional Commission — Economic Development

restrictions.name: Hall County
restrictions.title: Gainesville — NE Georgia Hub, Poultry Tech, and Hall EMC Service
restrictions.description: Hall County (Gainesville) is the commercial center for Northeast Georgia and the global center of poultry processing, hosting Pilgrim's Pride and Wayne Farms. Hall EMC and Georgia Power service, Georgia's data center sales tax exemption, US 129 and SR 53 corridor connectivity, the growing tech-adjacent industries around Lake Lanier, and the University of North Georgia creating a regional STEM workforce make it an emerging Northeast Georgia data center market.

restrictions.name: Dougherty County
restrictions.title: Albany — Marine Corps Logistics Base and SW Georgia Industrial Hub
restrictions.description: Dougherty County (Albany) hosts Marine Corps Logistics Base Albany (MCLB Albany), the Marine Corps' largest logistics installation responsible for worldwide equipment maintenance and supply. Georgia Power service, Georgia's data center sales tax exemption, the Flint River Industrial Complex, and MCLB Albany's significant IT and supply chain data infrastructure create defense and enterprise data center demand in southwest Georgia.

restrictions.name: Monterey County
restrictions.title: Salinas-Monterey — Ag-Tech AI Hub and MBARI Ocean Data Computing
restrictions.description: Monterey County (Salinas, Monterey, Seaside) is the center of California's Central Coast agriculture and hosts the Monterey Bay Aquarium Research Institute (MBARI), a leading ocean data and AI research center. PG&E service, California's data center sales tax incentive (Rev. & Tax. Code § 6377.1), the presidio area's defense data infrastructure (Defense Language Institute, Naval Postgraduate School), and the growing Salinas Valley ag-tech AI sector create data center demand in this dual defense/research market.

sources.label: California Governor's Office of Business and Economic Development

restrictions.name: Santa Cruz County
restrictions.title: Santa Cruz — UC Santa Cruz and Bay Area Southern Overflow
restrictions.description: Santa Cruz County hosts University of California Santa Cruz, home to the Jack Baskin School of Engineering with growing AI and data science programs. PG&E service, California's data center sales tax incentive, SR 17 fiber connectivity to San Jose/Silicon Valley, and the county's growing tech employer base drawing from Santa Clara County spillover create data center and research computing demand in this Bay Area adjacent market.

sources.label: UC Santa Cruz Baskin School of Engineering
Economic Development Department of Santa Cruz County
California Governor's Office of Business and Economic Development

restrictions.name: Sherburne County
restrictions.title: Monticello — Xcel Energy Monticello Nuclear Plant and Twin Cities NW Exurb
restrictions.description: Sherburne County (Monticello, Elk River, Big Lake) hosts the Monticello Nuclear Generating Plant, operated by Xcel Energy, providing large-scale carbon-free baseload power in the Twin Cities exurb. Xcel Energy service with nuclear and growing wind generation, Minnesota's Enterprise Zone data center program, I-94 corridor connectivity 35 miles northwest of Minneapolis, and the county's rapid residential and commercial growth make it a compelling large-load data center location with proven nuclear power infrastructure.

sources.label: Minnesota Department of Employment and Economic Development

restrictions.name: Wright County
restrictions.title: Buffalo-Monticello Area — Twin Cities NW Exurb and Xcel Energy Service
restrictions.description: Wright County (Buffalo, Monticello, St. Michael) anchors one of the fastest-growing counties in Minnesota as Twin Cities metro expansion moves northwest along I-94 and US 12. Xcel Energy service with nuclear baseload and renewable portfolio, lower industrial land costs than Hennepin or Anoka counties, and Minnesota's Enterprise Zone data center incentive program create a growing data center market adjacent to the Sherburne County nuclear corridor.

sources.label: Minnesota Department of Employment and Economic Development

restrictions.name: Lyon County
restrictions.title: Marshall — Southwest MN Wind Energy Hub and Southwest Power Pool
restrictions.description: Lyon County (Marshall) anchors southwestern Minnesota's wind energy corridor and hosts Southwest Minnesota State University. The Southwest Minnesota Power Cooperative (SMMPA) territory with exceptional wind resources and some of the lowest power rates in Minnesota, the county's position in the Southwest Power Pool (SPP) with renewable energy certificate access, and Minnesota's Enterprise Zone data center incentive program make it a niche market for renewable energy-driven data center development.

sources.label: Minnesota Department of Employment and Economic Development

restrictions.name: Indian River County
restrictions.title: Vero Beach — Treasure Coast Growth and FPL/Indian River County Utilities
restrictions.description: Indian River County (Vero Beach, Sebastian) anchors the northern Treasure Coast between Palm Beach and Brevard and is one of Florida's fastest-growing counties. Florida Power & Light service, Florida's data center equipment sales tax exemption (F.S. § 212.08(5)(f)), US 1 and I-95 corridor connectivity, and growing affluent demographic driving financial services IT demand make it an emerging secondary data center market on Florida's Atlantic Coast.

sources.label: Indian River County Economic Development Office

restrictions.name: Charlotte County
restrictions.title: Port Charlotte-Punta Gorda — SW Florida Growth and FPL Service
restrictions.description: Charlotte County (Port Charlotte, Punta Gorda, Englewood) is one of Florida's fastest-growing counties along the Gulf Coast, squeezed between fast-growing Lee County (Fort Myers) and Sarasota County. Florida Power & Light service, Florida's data center equipment sales tax exemption, US 41 and I-75 corridor connectivity, and the county's surging residential and commercial development driving enterprise IT demand create a growing secondary Southwest Florida data center market.

restrictions.name: Moore County
restrictions.title: Pinehurst-Southern Pines — SAS Institute Proximity and Duke Energy Progress
restrictions.description: Moore County (Pinehurst, Southern Pines, Aberdeen) sits in the North Carolina Sandhills, 60 miles southwest of Research Triangle Park and proximate to SAS Institute's campus in Cary. Duke Energy Progress service with North Carolina's Data Center Act (NCGS § 105-164.13(55)) sales tax exemption on equipment, Fort Liberty (formerly Fort Bragg) proximity driving military IT demand spillover, and the county's growing commercial corridor along US 15-501 create data center relevance.

sources.label: North Carolina Department of Commerce — Data Centers

restrictions.name: Randolph County
restrictions.title: Asheboro — NC Triad Manufacturing Hub and Duke Energy Progress Territory
restrictions.description: Randolph County (Asheboro) is a significant manufacturing county in the central North Carolina Piedmont between Greensboro and Charlotte. Duke Energy Progress service, North Carolina's data center equipment sales tax exemption, available industrial land at competitive rates, the NC Zoo driving tourism IT demand, and Randolph Community College's workforce programs make it a secondary data center consideration in the Triad manufacturing corridor.

sources.label: North Carolina Department of Commerce — Data Centers

restrictions.name: Vance County
restrictions.title: Henderson — I-85 Corridor Hub Between Raleigh and Richmond
restrictions.description: Vance County (Henderson) sits on I-85 exactly between the Research Triangle Park metro (50 miles south) and Richmond, Virginia (60 miles north). Duke Energy Progress service, North Carolina's data center equipment sales tax exemption, US 1 fiber route connectivity, Kerr Lake industrial development, and the county's position as a logistics gateway on the Southeast corridor make it an emerging secondary market for data center operators seeking lower costs proximate to the RTP and Richmond markets.

sources.label: North Carolina Department of Commerce — Data Centers

restrictions.name: Cameron County
restrictions.title: Cameron County TX — Brownsville/SpaceX Starbase Boca Chica
restrictions.description: Cameron County anchors the Rio Grande Valley's southernmost tip, home to SpaceX's Starbase launch facility (formerly Boca Chica), Brownsville/South Padre Island International Airport, and AEP Texas's Lower Rio Grande Valley transmission system. The county hosts one of the highest-profile aerospace and advanced manufacturing investments in the US, driving demand for industrial power and high-connectivity infrastructure on the Texas-Mexico border.

sources.label: AEP Texas — Lower Rio Grande Valley

restrictions.name: McLennan County
restrictions.title: McLennan County TX — Waco/Baylor University/I-35 corridor
restrictions.description: McLennan County anchors central Texas along the I-35 corridor between Dallas-Fort Worth and Austin, served by Oncor Electric Delivery. Waco hosts Baylor University (one of Texas's largest private universities) and is a growing logistics and distribution hub. The midpoint position on the I-35 digital spine makes McLennan County relevant for connectivity and fiber route infrastructure serving both major Texas metros.

restrictions.name: Gregg County
restrictions.title: Gregg County TX — Longview/East Texas oil & gas energy hub
restrictions.description: Gregg County (Longview) is the commercial center of East Texas, heavily served by ArkLaTex natural gas and oil infrastructure. Longview's position at the junction of US-80 and US-259, combined with SWEPCO/AEP's industrial electrical capacity serving the Sabine River industrial corridor, creates competitive industrial energy pricing. LeTourneau University and the county's long industrial history make it a secondary East Texas data center consideration.

sources.label: Gregg County — City of Longview TX

restrictions.name: Bartholomew County
restrictions.title: Bartholomew County IN — Columbus/Cummins HQ/Toyota Manufacturing
restrictions.description: Bartholomew County (Columbus) is home to Cummins Inc.'s global headquarters, a Toyota Motor Manufacturing plant, and a nationally recognized design and architecture heritage. Columbus's industrial base drives significant electrical infrastructure through Duke Energy Indiana, while the county's advanced manufacturing concentration supports strong fiber connectivity and industrial power delivery. The Cummins campus's own IT and power systems represent a high-quality industrial data infrastructure baseline.

sources.label: Bartholomew County — City of Columbus IN

restrictions.name: Howard County
restrictions.title: Howard County IN — Kokomo/Stellantis/auto manufacturing
restrictions.description: Howard County (Kokomo) is one of the most automotive-intensive counties in the US, home to Stellantis (formerly Chrysler/Fiat Chrysler) manufacturing plants that produce transmission components and Jeep models. The industrial power infrastructure serving major auto plants through Duke Energy Indiana provides high-capacity electrical access at competitive industrial rates. Indiana Nextgen Networks and high-density auto supplier connectivity serve the county's manufacturing base.

sources.label: Howard County — City of Kokomo IN

restrictions.name: Delaware County
restrictions.title: Delaware County IN — Muncie/Ball State University
restrictions.description: Delaware County (Muncie) is home to Ball State University, one of Indiana's major public universities with significant research computing programs. The county's position on AEP Indiana's grid and proximity to the Indianapolis digital corridor makes it a secondary Indiana data center consideration. Ball State's own high-performance computing infrastructure and the county's cost-competitive industrial electricity represent real data center fundamentals for university-adjacent deployments.

sources.label: Delaware County — City of Muncie IN

restrictions.name: Christian County
restrictions.title: Christian County KY — Hopkinsville/Fort Campbell/101st Airborne
restrictions.description: Christian County (Hopkinsville) hosts Fort Campbell, the home of the Army's 101st Airborne Division (Air Assault) — one of the largest military installations in the US by population. Fort Campbell straddles the Kentucky-Tennessee border and drives significant federal IT, communications, and logistics infrastructure demand. Kenergy Corporation serves the area with industrial-grade power from the TVA system, providing competitive energy pricing for the significant government computing workloads in the county.

sources.label: Kenergy Corp (TVA member cooperative) — Christian County

restrictions.name: Madison County
restrictions.title: Madison County KY — Richmond/Eastern Kentucky University
restrictions.description: Madison County (Richmond) anchors the Bluegrass region's eastern edge, home to Eastern Kentucky University and positioned between Lexington and the Appalachian transition zone on I-75. EKU's computing programs and the county's access to Kentucky Utilities (LG&E and KU Energy) provide solid infrastructure fundamentals. Richmond's growing logistics and industrial base along I-75 between Lexington and Knoxville positions it as a secondary data center consideration in the Bluegrass corridor.

sources.label: Madison County — City of Richmond KY

restrictions.name: Anderson County
restrictions.title: Anderson County SC — Upstate SC tech cluster/Clemson University adjacent
restrictions.description: Anderson County is part of Upstate South Carolina's burgeoning advanced manufacturing and technology cluster, adjacent to Clemson University and benefiting from the BMW manufacturing corridor running from Greenville through Spartanburg. Duke Energy Carolinas serves the county with industrial rates competitive within the Southeast, while Clemson's research computing programs and the county's proximity to I-85 create fiber access. The Upstate SC region has attracted significant manufacturing and logistics investment that supports data center co-location.

restrictions.name: Dorchester County
restrictions.title: Dorchester County SC — Summerville/Boeing Charleston suburb
restrictions.description: Dorchester County (Summerville) is one of the fastest-growing counties in South Carolina, a northern suburb of Charleston directly adjacent to Boeing's commercial aircraft manufacturing operations at Charleston International Airport. Dominion Energy South Carolina serves the county, and the Charleston metro's robust fiber infrastructure extends into Dorchester. The county's I-26 and US-17A positioning combined with significant aviation and aerospace employment drive enterprise IT and connectivity demand.

restrictions.name: Beaufort County
restrictions.title: Beaufort County SC — Parris Island MCRD/MCAS Beaufort/Hilton Head
restrictions.description: Beaufort County combines significant military presence with one of the Southeast's premier resort economies. Marine Corps Recruit Depot Parris Island (one of two USMC recruit training bases in the US) and Marine Corps Air Station Beaufort (F-35 training) drive substantial federal IT and communications infrastructure. Dominion Energy South Carolina serves the county. The Hilton Head side brings significant corporate and hospitality IT infrastructure to a coastal county with growing broadband backbone connectivity.

sources.label: MCRD Parris Island — Beaufort County SC
MCAS Beaufort — Marine Corps Air Station

restrictions.name: Broomfield County
restrictions.title: Broomfield County CO — Denver-Boulder tech corridor/Lumen Technologies HQ
restrictions.description: Broomfield County — Colorado's smallest county by area, created in 2001 as a standalone city-county — sits at the geographic midpoint of the Denver-Boulder technology corridor on US-36. Lumen Technologies (formerly CenturyLink) maintains its corporate headquarters in Broomfield, making it a literal internet infrastructure hub. Ball Aerospace, Oracle, and numerous telecom and cloud companies operate facilities here. Xcel Energy serves the corridor with highly competitive commercial rates backed by wind-heavy generation.

sources.label: Broomfield County — City and County of Broomfield CO

restrictions.name: Pueblo County
restrictions.title: Pueblo County CO — Pueblo/steel industry/Rocky Mountain internet exchange
restrictions.description: Pueblo County anchors southern Colorado with a legacy in steel manufacturing (EVRAZ Rocky Mountain Steel) and substantial industrial power infrastructure. Black Hills Energy and Xcel Energy both serve the county, with competitive industrial rates supported by the Arkansas River Basin's hydropower resources. Pueblo's position on I-25 and its fiber connectivity as a Southern Colorado internet hub (connecting Denver to New Mexico) gives it backbone relevance. Colorado State University-Pueblo and the county's cost-competitive land add secondary data center appeal.

restrictions.name: Ascension Parish
restrictions.title: Ascension Parish LA — Gonzales/Industrial Chemical Corridor/Entergy
restrictions.description: Ascension Parish (Gonzales) sits in the heart of Louisiana's Industrial Chemical Corridor between Baton Rouge and New Orleans, home to Dow Chemical, Shell Chemical, and BASF petrochemical plants. The industrial electrical infrastructure serving these massive chemical complexes through Entergy Louisiana provides exceptional high-load power delivery capacity. The parish's Baton Rouge Metro adjacency and I-10 positioning give fiber and connectivity access, while the industrial substation density creates unusually strong power infrastructure for large-load data center users.

restrictions.name: Livingston Parish
restrictions.title: Livingston Parish LA — Denham Springs/LIGO Observatory/Baton Rouge east
restrictions.description: Livingston Parish (Denham Springs) is the eastern Baton Rouge suburb and home to the LIGO Livingston Observatory — one of two sites of the Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory, which detected gravitational waves for the first time. LIGO Livingston's high-performance computing requirements have driven advanced fiber connectivity to the site. The parish's rapid residential and commercial growth east of Baton Rouge along I-12, served by Entergy Louisiana and Louisiana Electric Cooperative members, provides a growing data infrastructure base.

sources.label: LIGO Livingston Observatory — Livingston Parish LA

restrictions.name: Lincoln Parish
restrictions.title: Lincoln Parish LA — Ruston/Louisiana Tech University/I-20
restrictions.description: Lincoln Parish (Ruston) anchors north-central Louisiana as the home of Louisiana Tech University, one of Louisiana's flagship research universities with nationally recognized programs in engineering and computer science. The university's computing infrastructure and I-20 corridor positioning between Shreveport and Monroe give Lincoln Parish connectivity and academic computing significance. Entergy Louisiana and the North Louisiana Electric Cooperative serve the parish with industrial rates competitive in the Deep South.

restrictions.name: Tooele County
restrictions.title: Tooele County UT — Tooele Army Depot/Salt Lake exurb/data center campus
restrictions.description: Tooele County sits directly west of Salt Lake County, served by Rocky Mountain Power (PacifiCorp) with some of the lowest utility rates in Utah. The county hosts the Tooele Army Depot (chemical weapons demilitarization and Army logistics) and has attracted data center development taking advantage of low land costs, cold desert climate for free-air cooling, competitive power rates, and Salt Lake City's fiber networks extending westward. The Tooele Valley's flat terrain and I-80 corridor access make it an active secondary data center zone.

restrictions.name: Washington County
restrictions.title: Washington County UT — St. George/Dixie State/fastest-growing Utah city
restrictions.description: Washington County (St. George) has been the fastest-growing metropolitan area in the US by percentage multiple years running, driven by migration from California and Nevada. Dixie State University (now Utah Tech University) provides regional higher education computing needs. Rocky Mountain Power serves the county with competitive rates, while Utah's favorable tax environment and St. George's proximity to Las Vegas (130 miles) and the I-15 fiber corridor create secondary data center potential. The mild southern Utah climate and rapidly growing enterprise market make Washington County an emerging consideration.

sources.label: Rocky Mountain Power — Washington County UT

restrictions.name: Douglas County
restrictions.title: Douglas County NV — Carson Valley/Reno-Carson data corridor
restrictions.description: Douglas County sits at the southern end of the Reno-Tahoe data center corridor in Carson Valley, between Reno (Washoe County) and Carson City. NV Energy serves the county with Nevada's competitive commercial electricity rates, and the valley's position on US-395 and SR-88 fiber routes gives it connectivity to both the Reno and Sacramento metro areas. The county's low land costs, rural character, and absence of state income tax add to Nevada's well-known data center appeal. Several industrial and logistics operations in Gardnerville and Minden demonstrate the county's suitability for large footprint users.

restrictions.name: Cecil County
restrictions.title: Cecil County MD — Elkton/I-95 DE border/Northeast corridor fiber
restrictions.description: Cecil County (Elkton) sits at the Maryland-Delaware border astride I-95 — the most heavily-fiber-cabled interstate corridor in the Northeast US. The county's position at the I-95/US-40 junction between Baltimore and Wilmington DE puts it directly on the Northeast fiber spine. Delmarva Power (an Exelon/Constellation company) serves the county, with grid access to PJM's highly liquid wholesale electricity market. The county's low land costs relative to neighboring Delaware and northern Maryland, combined with I-95 fiber diversity for data routing, create solid secondary data center fundamentals.

restrictions.name: Madison County
restrictions.title: Madison County ID — Rexburg/BYU-Idaho/upper Snake River Plain
restrictions.description: Madison County (Rexburg) is home to BYU-Idaho (Brigham Young University–Idaho), one of the largest private universities in the Pacific Northwest by enrollment. The university's significant IT and data infrastructure, combined with Rexburg's position on the upper Snake River Plain east of Idaho Falls and Rocky Mountain Power's competitive rates, gives the county secondary data center relevance. The region's cold climate enables substantial free-air cooling hours, and the county's proximity to Idaho National Laboratory creates a research-adjacent computing environment.

restrictions.name: Payne County
restrictions.title: Payne County OK — Stillwater/Oklahoma State University
restrictions.description: Payne County (Stillwater) hosts Oklahoma State University — one of Oklahoma's two flagship research universities and a significant research computing site. OSU's computing and engineering programs drive institutional IT demand, while the county's position on US-412 and proximity to the Oklahoma City metro (80 miles) provides I-35 fiber access. Oklahoma Gas and Electric (OG&E) serves the county with Oklahoma's competitive commercial electricity rates. The combination of university research and moderate cost of living makes Payne County a secondary Oklahoma data center consideration.

restrictions.name: Canadian County
restrictions.title: Canadian County OK — Yukon/Mustang/Oklahoma City west metro growth
restrictions.description: Canadian County (Yukon, Mustang, El Reno) is the rapidly expanding western suburb of Oklahoma City, one of the fastest-growing OKC Metro counties. The county's position on I-40 west of OKC, served by Oklahoma Gas and Electric and the OKC metropolitan fiber network, makes it an edge data center candidate for OKC latency-sensitive workloads. Yukon's lower commercial real estate costs relative to OKC proper, combined with proximity to the Will Rogers World Airport logistics cluster, attract distribution and logistics operators driving secondary data infrastructure.

sources.label: Oklahoma Gas and Electric (OG&E) — OKC Metro

restrictions.name: Faulkner County
restrictions.title: Faulkner County AR — Conway/University of Central Arkansas/Hendrix College
restrictions.description: Faulkner County (Conway) hosts two four-year universities — the University of Central Arkansas (UCA) and Hendrix College — and is part of the Arkansas River Valley's growing tech and logistics corridor 30 miles north of Little Rock on I-40. Entergy Arkansas serves the county. Conway's rapid growth as a Little Rock bedroom community and its concentration of higher education institutions drive IT and connectivity demand. The county's 'City of Colleges' reputation and I-40/I-440 access give it connectivity relevance in the Arkansas data center landscape.

sources.label: University of Central Arkansas — Conway AR
Faulkner County AR — City of Conway

restrictions.name: Lowndes County
restrictions.title: Lowndes County MS — Columbus/USAF Columbus AFB/Golden Triangle industrial
restrictions.description: Lowndes County (Columbus) hosts Columbus Air Force Base — one of the US Air Force's primary undergraduate pilot training bases, flying T-38 Talons. The Golden Triangle region (Columbus, Starkville, West Point) is northeast Mississippi's primary industrial corridor. Mississippi Power (Southern Company) serves the county with Mississippi's competitive industrial rates. The region's PACCAR manufacturing plant, steel mill, and aerospace training drive industrial electrical infrastructure. Columbus AFB's aviation fuel and communications systems represent substantial federal IT and power load.

sources.label: Columbus Air Force Base — Lowndes County MS

restrictions.name: Buffalo County
restrictions.title: Buffalo County NE — Kearney/I-80 midpoint/University of Nebraska-Kearney
restrictions.description: Buffalo County (Kearney) sits at the geographic midpoint of I-80 between Omaha and Denver — a strategic position on the US east-west fiber backbone. University of Nebraska-Kearney provides academic computing and IT demand, while the county's agricultural processing and distribution economy (driven by Nebraska Public Power District's low-cost hydroelectric and nuclear power) supports industrial-grade electricity access. Kearney's I-80 position and NPPD pricing make it a legitimate consideration for I-80 corridor data center infrastructure.

sources.label: Buffalo County NE — City of Kearney

restrictions.name: Stark County
restrictions.title: Stark County ND — Dickinson/Bakken oil services/Basin Electric
restrictions.description: Stark County (Dickinson) is the commercial center of the Western Slope and the principal service hub for the Bakken oil patch. Dickinson State University and the county's position on I-94 between Bismarck and Montana make it a regional hub with growing connectivity. Basin Electric Power Cooperative serves the county. The Bakken oil economy's digital transformation (IoT, SCADA, field data systems) drives IT infrastructure demand from energy companies maintaining operations centers in Dickinson.

sources.label: Stark County ND — City of Dickinson

restrictions.name: Butler County
restrictions.title: Butler County KS — El Dorado/Wichita east exurb/Koch Industries adjacent
restrictions.description: Butler County (El Dorado) is the eastern Wichita suburb and home to El Dorado Refinery (HollyFrontier/HF Sinclair), one of Kansas's largest oil refineries. Koch Industries' Wichita headquarters complex (in neighboring Sedgwick County) extends its supply chain and industrial services into Butler County. Evergy (formerly Westar Energy) serves the county. The county's refinery-driven industrial electrical infrastructure and proximity to Wichita's aviation and advanced manufacturing cluster give it real data center adjacent fundamentals.

restrictions.name: Leavenworth County
restrictions.title: Leavenworth County KS — Fort Leavenworth/Army Combined Arms Center/federal IT
restrictions.description: Leavenworth County hosts Fort Leavenworth — the Army's intellectual center, home to the Command and General Staff College (CGSC) and the Combined Arms Center. Fort Leavenworth is a major Army education and training installation with significant IT, communications, and intelligence infrastructure. The adjacent city of Leavenworth provides federal civilian employment and services. Evergy serves the county, with Kansas City Metro's fiber network providing robust connectivity adjacent to Fort Leavenworth.

restrictions.name: Muskogee County
restrictions.title: Muskogee County OK — Muskogee/Oklahoma's fourth-largest city/Port of Muskogee
restrictions.description: Muskogee County (Muskogee) is Oklahoma's fourth-largest city and home to the McClellan-Kerr Arkansas River Navigation System's Port of Muskogee — Oklahoma's only navigable port. The county's industrial heritage (steel, glass, and chemical manufacturing) has driven substantial electrical infrastructure through Public Service Oklahoma (AEP). Northeastern State University (in nearby Tahlequah, Cherokke County) serves the broader region. Muskogee's industrial grid capacity, US-69/US-64 connectivity, and AEP's industrial rate structure give it genuine secondary data center potential in eastern Oklahoma.

sources.label: Muskogee County OK — City of Muskogee

restrictions.name: Lincoln County
restrictions.title: Lincoln County NE — North Platte/Union Pacific Bailey Yard/world's largest rail classification yard
restrictions.description: Lincoln County (North Platte) is home to Union Pacific Railroad's Bailey Yard — the world's largest railroad classification yard, handling over 10,000 cars daily. The enormous logistics and communications infrastructure at Bailey Yard, combined with Nebraska Public Power District's low-cost electricity and North Platte's I-80/US-83 position, creates an unusual IT infrastructure base in a mid-sized Nebraska city. The rail yard's digital transformation (remote sensing, AI-enabled scheduling, fiber connectivity for locomotive management) makes Lincoln County a distinctive secondary data center market.

sources.label: Union Pacific Bailey Yard — North Platte NE

restrictions.name: Williams County
restrictions.title: Williams County ND — Williston/Bakken oil play/SCADA and field digitalization
restrictions.description: Williams County (Williston) is the epicenter of the Williston Basin/Bakken oil play, one of the most significant oil production zones in the US. The digitalization of Bakken oil field operations — IoT sensors, SCADA systems, AI-driven production optimization, remote monitoring — drives significant real-time data and computing demand. Basin Electric Power Cooperative and Montana-Dakota Utilities serve the county. Williston's boom-bust economy has left substantial industrial power infrastructure and emerging fiber connectivity that supports regional data center operations.

sources.label: Williams County ND — City of Williston
Basin Electric Power Cooperative — Western ND

restrictions.name: Silver Bow County
restrictions.title: Silver Bow County MT — Butte/Anaconda copper mining power infrastructure
restrictions.description: Silver Bow County (Butte) hosts one of the most distinctive legacy industrial power infrastructures in the West. The Anaconda copper mining era left behind massive high-voltage electrical transmission infrastructure originally designed to power copper smelters and mines. NorthWestern Energy now serves the county with hydropower-backed low-cost electricity. Butte's mountain valley location, cold climate (exceptional free-air cooling), and the historical substation network create unusual power delivery capacity for a small market. Montana Tech (University of Montana-Butte) provides academic computing presence.

sources.label: Silver Bow County MT — City of Butte
NorthWestern Energy — Silver Bow County MT

restrictions.name: Bannock County
restrictions.title: Bannock County ID — Pocatello/Idaho State University/I-15 gateway
restrictions.description: Bannock County (Pocatello) is Idaho's second-largest city and home to Idaho State University (ISU), a research university with significant pharmacy, health sciences, and engineering programs. Rocky Mountain Power (PacifiCorp) serves the county with Idaho's competitive rates. Pocatello's position at the I-15/I-86 junction — the principal north-south and east-west corridor in southeastern Idaho — gives it strategic connectivity to Salt Lake City (175 miles) and Boise (230 miles). The county's proximity to Idaho National Laboratory (Bonneville County) adds federal research-adjacent computing context.

restrictions.name: Brookings County
restrictions.title: Brookings County SD — Brookings/South Dakota State University/ag tech research
restrictions.description: Brookings County (Brookings) is home to South Dakota State University (SDSU) — the state's land-grant university and the largest university in South Dakota, with nationally ranked agricultural, engineering, and technology programs. SDSU's Center for Scalable Computing and USDA research programs drive significant academic and government computing infrastructure. Otter Tail Power and NorthWestern Energy serve the area. South Dakota's zero state corporate income tax and Brookings' I-29 corridor access to the Minneapolis-Sioux Falls fiber backbone add data center appeal.

sources.label: South Dakota State University — Brookings SD

restrictions.name: Garland County
restrictions.title: Garland County AR — Hot Springs/Oaklawn/south-central Arkansas regional hub
restrictions.description: Garland County (Hot Springs) is Arkansas's tourist and hospitality capital, home to Oaklawn Racing Casino Resort and Hot Springs National Park. The county's service economy drives significant IT infrastructure through Entergy Arkansas. Hot Springs' position on US-70 between Little Rock (55 miles) and the Ouachita Mountains, combined with the county's long history as a regional healthcare and financial services hub, creates enterprise IT demand. The county's moderate climate, proximity to Little Rock fiber networks, and growing retirement/remote-worker population add secondary data center relevance.

sources.label: Garland County AR — City of Hot Springs

restrictions.name: Saline County
restrictions.title: Saline County AR — Benton/Little Rock SW exurb/fastest-growing AR county
restrictions.description: Saline County (Benton, Bryant) is one of the fastest-growing counties in Arkansas, a southwestern suburb of Little Rock directly accessed by I-30. The county's rapid residential and commercial growth on Little Rock's southern edge, combined with Entergy Arkansas service and LR Metro fiber extension, makes it a natural edge data center market for the Little Rock metro. Saline County's lower land costs relative to Pulaski County (Little Rock proper) and its I-30/US-67 positioning attract logistics and distribution operations that generate secondary IT demand.

sources.label: Saline County AR — City of Benton

restrictions.name: Scott County
restrictions.title: Scott County IA — Davenport/Quad Cities/Rock Island Arsenal
restrictions.description: Scott County (Davenport) anchors the Iowa side of the Quad Cities metro, the nation's fourth-largest bi-state metro area spanning Iowa and Illinois along the Mississippi River. Rock Island Arsenal (on Arsenal Island in the Mississippi River, adjacent to Scott County) is the Army's largest government-owned weapons manufacturing arsenal. MidAmerican Energy serves the county with Iowa's competitive rates. The Quad Cities' position at the I-80/I-74/US-61 junction gives Scott County strong fiber access, and the bi-state industrial economy drives significant enterprise IT demand.

sources.label: Scott County IA — City of Davenport

restrictions.name: Dodge County
restrictions.title: Dodge County NE — Fremont/Omaha northwest exurb/Costco chicken campus
restrictions.description: Dodge County (Fremont) is a growing Omaha western suburb on US-30 and US-77, served by OPPD (Omaha Public Power District) and Nebraska Public Power District. Fremont hosts Costco's large poultry processing campus, representing significant industrial electrical load on the Omaha metro grid. Midland University adds an academic presence. Dodge County's proximity to Omaha's data center corridor (Sarpy/Douglas counties), combined with OPPD's competitive commercial rates and Fremont's industrial zoning, makes it a legitimate edge consideration for Omaha metro overflow data center capacity.

sources.label: Dodge County NE — City of Fremont

restrictions.name: Madison County
restrictions.title: Madison County NE — Norfolk/NE northeast hub/Northeast Nebraska Medical Center
restrictions.description: Madison County (Norfolk) is the commercial and healthcare hub of northeast Nebraska, home to Northeast Nebraska Medical Center and Norfolk Catholic High School. Nebraska Public Power District (NPPD) serves the county with Nebraska's low-cost power mix. Norfolk's position on US-81 (the principal north-south corridor through eastern Nebraska) and US-275 gives it regional connectivity to the Sioux City and Omaha fiber networks. The county's role as a regional agricultural, healthcare, and distribution center for a large rural catchment drives modest but steady enterprise IT demand.

sources.label: Madison County NE — City of Norfolk

restrictions.name: Woodbury County
restrictions.title: Woodbury County IA — Sioux City/MidAmerican Energy/Tri-State livestock hub
restrictions.description: Woodbury County (Sioux City) is Iowa's third-largest city and the commercial hub of Siouxland — the tri-state Iowa-Nebraska-South Dakota market. MidAmerican Energy's low commercial rates and Sioux City's position on I-29 (the principal north-south corridor in western Iowa) give the county solid data center fundamentals. The county's agricultural processing economy (Tyson, Hy-Vee distribution) drives substantial industrial electrical load. Morningside University and Sioux City's role as a regional financial and healthcare center create enterprise IT demand.

sources.label: Woodbury County IA — City of Sioux City

restrictions.name: Cerro Gordo County
restrictions.title: Cerro Gordo County IA — Mason City/US-18 & US-65 junction/North Iowa hub
restrictions.description: Cerro Gordo County (Mason City) is the commercial center of north-central Iowa, situated at the US-18 and US-65 junction. North Iowa Area Community College and Mason City's role as a regional healthcare, agricultural, and manufacturing hub drive institutional IT demand. Alliant Energy (Interstate Power and Light) serves the county. The county's Lime Creek and Shell Rock River corridor industrial heritage, combined with its fiber connections on the Iowa Communications Network's north trunk, gives it solid secondary data infrastructure context.

sources.label: Cerro Gordo County IA — City of Mason City

restrictions.name: Webster County
restrictions.title: Webster County IA — Fort Dodge/Gypsum Belt/Iowa Central Community College
restrictions.description: Webster County (Fort Dodge) is north-central Iowa's commercial hub, historically defined by gypsum mining and manufacturing (USG Corporation, National Gypsum). Iowa Central Community College provides regional education computing. MidAmerican Energy serves the county. Fort Dodge's position on US-169 and US-20 gives it connectivity to both Des Moines (90 miles south) and Mason City, while the county's heavy manufacturing history has driven industrial-grade electrical infrastructure from MidAmerican's service territory.

sources.label: Webster County IA — City of Fort Dodge

restrictions.name: Clay County
restrictions.title: Clay County SD — Vermillion/University of South Dakota/I-29 corridor
restrictions.description: Clay County (Vermillion) is the home of the University of South Dakota (USD) — the state's flagship liberal arts and professional university, hosting the only law school in South Dakota. USD's computing infrastructure, combined with Vermillion's position on I-29 (30 miles north of Sioux City) and Otter Tail Power's competitive rates, give the county secondary data center relevance. South Dakota's zero state income tax and USD's growing research programs add to the county's data infrastructure appeal.

sources.label: University of South Dakota — Vermillion SD

restrictions.name: Davison County
restrictions.title: Davison County SD — Mitchell/I-90 crossroads/South Dakota School of Mines adjacent
restrictions.description: Davison County (Mitchell) sits at the I-90 corridor midpoint in south-central South Dakota, a key junction for east-west interstate traffic. Mitchell's role as the commercial hub of the region, combined with NorthWestern Energy's competitive rates and South Dakota's zero corporate income tax, creates solid secondary data center fundamentals. The county's agricultural service economy and I-90 fiber corridor positioning give it connectivity access to both Sioux Falls (70 miles east) and Rapid City (250 miles west).

sources.label: Davison County SD — City of Mitchell

restrictions.name: Lawrence County
restrictions.title: Lawrence County SD — Lead/Deadwood/Sanford Underground Research Facility (SURF)
restrictions.description: Lawrence County (Lead, Deadwood) is home to the Sanford Underground Research Facility (SURF) — the deepest underground laboratory in the United States, located in the former Homestake Gold Mine. SURF hosts major physics experiments including the LUX-ZEPLIN dark matter detector and the DUNE/LBNF neutrino program. The research computing requirements of SURF and its DOE connections drive significant scientific computing infrastructure to the Black Hills. Black Hills Energy serves the county with South Dakota's favorable commercial rates.

sources.label: Sanford Underground Research Facility (SURF) — Lead SD
Black Hills Energy — Lawrence County SD

restrictions.name: Saline County
restrictions.title: Saline County KS — Salina/I-70 & I-135 junction/Kansas geographic center hub
restrictions.description: Saline County (Salina) occupies the precise geographic crossroads of Kansas, where I-70 (east-west) and I-135 (north-south, connecting Wichita to I-70) intersect. This junction makes Salina a critical fiber route diversification point for network infrastructure crossing Kansas. Evergy and Salina's municipal utilities provide competitive commercial rates. Salina's aviation industry (Smoky Hill Air National Guard, Great Plains Aviation) and agricultural processing economy (Cargill, Westar Grain) drive industrial-grade electrical demand. Kansas Wesleyan University provides a modest academic computing presence.

sources.label: Saline County KS — City of Salina

restrictions.name: Ellis County
restrictions.title: Ellis County KS — Hays/Fort Hays State University/I-70 western Kansas hub
restrictions.description: Ellis County (Hays) is the commercial center of western Kansas, home to Fort Hays State University — the fastest-growing university in Kansas and a nationally recognized online education provider. FHSU's online programs drive significant computing infrastructure at scale, and the university's data center operations are among the largest in western Kansas. Evergy serves the county. Hays' I-70 position gives it fiber corridor access, and the county's wind energy surroundings (Kansas wind belt) provide future renewable power context for data operations.

sources.label: Fort Hays State University — Hays KS
Ellis County KS — City of Hays

restrictions.name: Forrest County
restrictions.title: Forrest County MS — Hattiesburg/University of Southern Mississippi
restrictions.description: Forrest County (Hattiesburg) is home to the University of Southern Mississippi (USM) — one of Mississippi's two Carnegie R2 research universities — and William Carey University. USM's research computing programs, combined with Hattiesburg's position at the I-59/US-98 junction (connecting the Gulf Coast to Metro Jackson), give the county solid secondary data center fundamentals. Mississippi Power (Southern Company) serves the county with Mississippi's competitive industrial rates. The Hattiesburg metro's healthcare and education economy drives steady enterprise IT demand.

sources.label: University of Southern Mississippi — Hattiesburg MS

restrictions.name: Lee County
restrictions.title: Lee County MS — Tupelo/Toyota Motor Manufacturing/NE Mississippi hub
restrictions.description: Lee County (Tupelo) is the commercial center of northeast Mississippi and home to Toyota Motor Manufacturing Mississippi — one of the largest Toyota plants in North America, producing Corolla and Highlander models. The Toyota plant's industrial electrical infrastructure through Tennessee Valley Authority (TVA) power, combined with Tupelo's role as a regional financial and healthcare hub, creates solid industrial-grade power delivery in a mid-sized Mississippi city. North Mississippi Medical Center (the largest rural hospital in the US by volume) drives substantial healthcare IT demand.

sources.label: Toyota Motor Manufacturing Mississippi — Lee County

restrictions.name: Lauderdale County
restrictions.title: Lauderdale County MS — Meridian/Naval Air Station Meridian/Key Field
restrictions.description: Lauderdale County (Meridian) hosts Naval Air Station Meridian — the Navy's primary jet pilot training base, operating T-45 Goshawks. Meridian's position at the I-20/US-45 junction makes it the most significant I-20 hub in Mississippi outside Jackson. Mississippi Power (Southern Company) and TVA serve the county. The NAS Meridian's aviation fuel, communications, and IT infrastructure, combined with East Mississippi Community College and Meridian Community College, drive institutional computing demand in a county larger than several northeastern US states.

sources.label: Naval Air Station Meridian — Lauderdale County MS

restrictions.name: Marion County
restrictions.title: Marion County WV — Fairmont/WVU adjacent/National Energy Technology Lab (NETL)
restrictions.description: Marion County (Fairmont) sits immediately south of Monongalia County (Morgantown/WVU) and is effectively part of the WVU research corridor. NETL's Morgantown campus (which has a Marion County-adjacent presence) is the Department of Energy's leading research lab for fossil fuel and energy transition technology — a major federal computing customer. Fairmont State University provides local academic computing. FirstEnergy (Mon Power) serves the county. Marion County's I-79 connectivity and proximity to WVU's tech transfer ecosystem give it secondary data center relevance.

sources.label: Marion County WV — City of Fairmont

restrictions.name: Putnam County
restrictions.title: Putnam County WV — Teays Valley/Hurricane/Charleston west suburb
restrictions.description: Putnam County (Hurricane, Teays Valley, Winfield) is consistently the fastest-growing county in West Virginia, a Charleston western suburb accessible via I-64 and WV-34. The county's residential and commercial growth west of Charleston has attracted regional distribution and logistics operations. Appalachian Power (AEP) serves the county with West Virginia's competitive rates. Putnam County's position on I-64 between Charleston and Huntington — two of WV's largest cities — gives it linear corridor connectivity for fiber routing and edge data center deployment.

restrictions.name: Franklin County
restrictions.title: Franklin County KY — Frankfort/Kentucky state capital/state government IT
restrictions.description: Franklin County (Frankfort) is the Kentucky state capital and home to the Commonwealth Office of Technology — the state's central IT and cybersecurity agency. State government computing infrastructure, data centers, and telecommunications systems are concentrated in Frankfort. Kentucky Utilities (LG&E and KU Energy) serves the county. Kentucky State University provides a Historically Black University computing presence. The capital's government IT infrastructure, bourbon distilling operations (Buffalo Trace), and proximity to the Lexington fiber hub (25 miles) give Franklin County data center context unique to state capital markets.

sources.label: Kentucky Commonwealth Office of Technology — Frankfort
Franklin County KY — City of Frankfort

restrictions.name: Bullitt County
restrictions.title: Bullitt County KY — Shepherdsville/Louisville south/Amazon fulfillment/I-65
restrictions.description: Bullitt County (Shepherdsville, Mount Washington) is the rapidly growing southern suburb of Louisville, accessed directly by I-65 and I-265 (the Gene Snyder Freeway). Amazon's Kentucky Fulfillment Center and multiple Louisville Metro logistics campuses have expanded into Bullitt County, driving significant electrical demand through Louisville Gas and Electric. The county's lower commercial real estate cost relative to Jefferson County, combined with LG&E's competitive rates and Louisville's robust fiber network extending southward, make it a natural edge data center zone for the Louisville market.

sources.label: Bullitt County KY — City of Shepherdsville
Louisville Gas and Electric (LG&E) — Bullitt County

restrictions.name: Pulaski County
restrictions.title: Pulaski County KY — Somerset/Lake Cumberland/south-central KY hub
restrictions.description: Pulaski County (Somerset) is the commercial hub of south-central Kentucky and the Lake Cumberland region — one of the largest man-made lakes east of the Mississippi River. The county's role as a regional healthcare, retail, and logistics center drives institutional IT demand. Kentucky Utilities (LG&E and KU Energy) serves the county with competitive rates backed by Kentucky's diversified generation mix. Somerset Community College and the county's manufacturing base (auto parts, plastics) support enterprise IT demand in a region often overlooked in data center geography.

sources.label: Pulaski County KY — City of Somerset

restrictions.name: Webb County
restrictions.title: Webb County TX — Laredo/international border trade/AEP Texas
restrictions.description: Webb County (Laredo) is home to the busiest land port of entry in the Western Hemisphere — Laredo handles more international trade than any other US land crossing. The volume of logistics, customs, supply chain management, and financial transaction processing through Laredo drives significant enterprise IT demand. AEP Texas serves the county.
