As NEARSHORING accelerates manufacturing relocation from Asia to Mexico, Webb County's position as the principal gateway for US-Mexico cross-border trade makes it an increasingly important secondary data center market.

sources.label: Webb County TX — City of Laredo

restrictions.name: Tom Green County
restrictions.title: Tom Green County TX — San Angelo/wind energy hub/Concho Valley
restrictions.description: Tom Green County (San Angelo) anchors the Concho Valley region of West Texas and sits in the heart of one of the highest wind energy generation zones in the US. Oncor Electric Delivery serves the county, with massive wind generation from surrounding counties flowing through Tom Green's transmission infrastructure. Angelo State University provides academic computing. San Angelo's military connection (Goodfellow AFB — Air Force intelligence training) drives federal IT demand. The county's combination of renewable energy access, low land costs, and mild Concho Valley climate creates real data center potential.

sources.label: Tom Green County TX — City of San Angelo
Goodfellow AFB — Tom Green County TX

restrictions.name: Nacogdoches County
restrictions.title: Nacogdoches County TX — Nacogdoches/SFA University/East Texas timber & energy
restrictions.description: Nacogdoches County (Nacogdoches) is home to Stephen F. Austin State University (SFA) and is the commercial hub of deep East Texas timber country. SWEPCO (AEP) serves the county. The county's proximity to the Sabine River energy corridor, combined with SFA's research computing programs and Nacogdoches' natural gas gathering infrastructure, creates a secondary East Texas data center context. The county's fiber connectivity via US-59 (the East Texas energy corridor) and relatively low cost of land and electricity add incremental data center appeal.

sources.label: Stephen F. Austin State University — Nacogdoches TX

restrictions.name: Victoria County
restrictions.title: Victoria County TX — Victoria/Formosa Plastics/petrochemical coastal hub
restrictions.description: Victoria County (Victoria) is a Gulf Coast petrochemical hub 30 miles inland from the shipping channels, home to Formosa Plastics' Point Comfort Plant — one of the largest petrochemical complexes in North America. AEP Texas serves the county with the industrial-grade electrical infrastructure required by major plastics and chemical manufacturing operations. Victoria College and the University of Houston-Victoria provide local academic computing. The county's proximity to Corpus Christi (80 miles south) and Houston (130 miles east) via US-77 and US-59 gives it connectivity to two major Texas fiber markets.

sources.label: Victoria County TX — City of Victoria

restrictions.name: Macon County
restrictions.title: Macon County IL — Decatur/ADM (Archer Daniels Midland) HQ/agricultural processing power
restrictions.description: Macon County (Decatur) is home to Archer Daniels Midland (ADM) — one of the world's largest agricultural commodity processors, with its headquarters and largest grain processing campus in Decatur. ADM's ethanol, soybean crushing, and corn wet milling operations are among the most electrically intensive industrial processes in the US, driving massive substation buildout through Ameren Illinois. Millikin University and the county's role as central Illinois's primary agricultural processing hub contribute to significant institutional IT and industrial electrical infrastructure.

restrictions.name: St. Clair County
restrictions.title: St. Clair County IL — Belleville/Scott AFB/US Transportation Command (TRANSCOM)
restrictions.description: St. Clair County (Belleville, O'Fallon) hosts Scott Air Force Base — headquarters of US Transportation Command (TRANSCOM), the DOD's global logistics command coordinating all military transport and supply chain operations worldwide. TRANSCOM's computing and communications infrastructure for coordinating global military logistics is among the most extensive federal IT footprints in the Midwest. Ameren Illinois serves the county, part of the St. Louis Metro East. Scott AFB also hosts the Air Mobility Command (AMC) and is one of the largest DoD logistics computing sites in the US.

sources.label: Scott Air Force Base — US Transportation Command

restrictions.name: Madison County
restrictions.title: Madison County IL — Alton/Granite City/St. Louis Metro East industrial
restrictions.description: Madison County (Alton, Granite City, Edwardsville) is the largest county in Illinois's St. Louis Metro East, home to US Steel's Granite City Works (a major integrated steel mill), Shell Roxana Refinery, and significant chemical manufacturing on the Mississippi River corridor. Ameren Illinois serves the county. Southern Illinois University-Edwardsville (SIUE) provides academic computing, while the industrial corridor's heavy electrical load infrastructure supports large-scale power users. The county's Mississippi River position gives it connectivity to the broader St. Louis fiber market.

restrictions.name: Dawson County
restrictions.title: Dawson County NE — Lexington/JBS beef processing/massive NPPD industrial load
restrictions.description: Dawson County (Lexington) hosts JBS USA's Lexington Nebraska beef processing plant — one of the largest beef processing facilities in the world, employing over 5,000 workers and processing thousands of cattle daily. The industrial electrical load from this and adjacent processing operations is among the highest per-capita of any Nebraska county, creating large-capacity substation infrastructure through Nebraska Public Power District (NPPD). The plant's digital transformation (automated carcass sorting, animal tracing, IoT monitoring) and NPPD's low-cost power make Dawson County a real secondary data center site.

restrictions.name: Adams County
restrictions.title: Adams County NE — Hastings/mid-Nebraska hub/Hastings College
restrictions.description: Adams County (Hastings) is the commercial hub of south-central Nebraska, home to Hastings College and positioned on US-6 and US-281 between Lincoln (100 miles east) and Kearney (50 miles west). Nebraska Public Power District's low-cost hydro and nuclear power serves the county. Hastings' agricultural service economy, regional healthcare hub (Mary Lanning Healthcare), and Hastings College computing programs drive modest but steady institutional IT demand across a wide south-central Nebraska catchment.

sources.label: Adams County NE — City of Hastings

restrictions.name: York County
restrictions.title: York County NE — York/I-80 hub between Lincoln and Grand Island
restrictions.description: York County (York) sits on I-80 roughly midway between Lincoln and Grand Island — a position that makes it a natural fiber route waypoint on the US east-west backbone. Nebraska Public Power District serves the county. York College, a small liberal arts institution, and the county's agricultural service role anchor modest IT demand. The county's I-80 positioning, flat topography, and NPPD's consistently low commercial rates give it secondary data center potential as a route-diversity site between Omaha and the central Nebraska market.

sources.label: York County NE — City of York

restrictions.name: Scotts Bluff County
restrictions.title: Scotts Bluff County NE — Scottsbluff-Gering/Western NE panhandle hub/North Platte River
restrictions.description: Scotts Bluff County (Scottsbluff, Gering) is the commercial center of the Nebraska Panhandle and the western Wyoming-Nebraska border region. Western Nebraska Community College serves the area. Nebraska Public Power District provides low-cost power. The county's North Platte River irrigated agriculture drives significant water management IT, and its position on US-26 and US-71 makes it a rural hub serving a large geographic catchment. The county's NPPD power access and low land costs represent standard secondary Nebraska data center fundamentals.

sources.label: Scotts Bluff County NE — City of Scottsbluff

restrictions.name: Finney County
restrictions.title: Finney County KS — Garden City/Tyson Fresh Meats/massive beef processing power
restrictions.description: Finney County (Garden City) hosts Tyson Fresh Meats' massive Garden City beef processing plant — one of the largest in the world, employing over 5,000 workers. The industrial electrical load from this facility through Evergy is among the highest per-county in western Kansas. Garden City's position in the Arkansas River Valley with access to Ogallala Aquifer irrigation also drives significant agricultural processing IT. The county's industrial power infrastructure and Evergy's Kansas wind energy integration give it structural data center power advantages.

sources.label: Tyson Fresh Meats — Garden City KS

restrictions.name: Geary County
restrictions.title: Geary County KS — Junction City/Fort Riley/1st Infantry Division
restrictions.description: Geary County (Junction City) adjoins Fort Riley — home of the US Army's 1st Infantry Division (Big Red One), one of the most storied Army combat divisions. Fort Riley straddles the Riley-Geary county line but Junction City is the principal off-post community. The base's computing, communications, and intelligence infrastructure drives significant IT demand. Evergy serves the county. Junction City's position at the I-70/US-40 junction midway across Kansas gives it connectivity importance on the east-west fiber corridor.

sources.label: Geary County KS — City of Junction City

restrictions.name: Harvey County
restrictions.title: Harvey County KS — Newton/Wichita north suburb/BNSF railway hub
restrictions.description: Harvey County (Newton) is the northern Wichita suburb and home to one of the most significant BNSF Railway classification yards in Kansas, serving as a key junction for the transcontinental railroad network. Bethel College (one of the country's oldest Mennonite colleges) provides an academic computing presence. Evergy serves the county as part of the greater Wichita grid. Newton's BNSF yard drives telecommunications and digital operations infrastructure, and the county's proximity to Wichita's fiber network extends metro connectivity northward.

sources.label: Harvey County KS — City of Newton

restrictions.name: Washington County
restrictions.title: Washington County OK — Bartlesville/Phillips 66 global headquarters/Philbrook
restrictions.description: Washington County (Bartlesville) is home to Phillips 66's global headquarters — one of the largest oil refining and midstream companies in the US. A major energy corporation's global HQ drives substantial enterprise IT infrastructure, including data centers, cybersecurity operations, and energy management systems. Oklahoma Gas and Electric (OG&E) serves the county. Bartlesville's position on US-75 north of Tulsa gives it connectivity to the Tulsa metro fiber network, while the county's significant corporate IT footprint from Phillips 66 makes it a secondary Oklahoma data center market.

sources.label: Washington County OK — City of Bartlesville

restrictions.name: Kay County
restrictions.title: Kay County OK — Ponca City/Phillips 66 refinery/Continental Carbon
restrictions.description: Kay County (Ponca City) hosts the Phillips 66 Ponca City Refinery — one of the company's major inland refineries — along with Continental Carbon (carbon black manufacturing) and other petrochemical operations. Oklahoma Gas and Electric and Western Farmers Electric serve the county with Oklahoma's competitive rates. Ponca City's industrial base and position on US-60 and US-77 give it connectivity to the Oklahoma City and Tulsa networks. The refinery complex's significant industrial electrical load and IT infrastructure represent a meaningful data center context.

sources.label: Kay County OK — City of Ponca City

restrictions.name: Muscatine County
restrictions.title: Muscatine County IA — Muscatine/HNI Corporation/Mississippi River industrial
restrictions.description: Muscatine County (Muscatine) anchors SE Iowa's Mississippi River industrial corridor. HNI Corporation (formerly HON Industries), a major office furniture manufacturer, is headquartered in Muscatine. Grain Processing Corporation and Monsanto (now Bayer) operations drive substantial industrial electrical load through MidAmerican Energy. Muscatine Power and Water provides municipal power service in the city itself. The county's position on US-61 and the Mississippi River, combined with its dense manufacturing base and Muscatine Community College, create solid secondary data center fundamentals.

sources.label: Muscatine Power and Water — Muscatine IA

restrictions.name: Clinton County
restrictions.title: Clinton County IA — Clinton/DuPont-Chemours Lycra/Mississippi River industrial
restrictions.description: Clinton County (Clinton) anchors NE Iowa's Mississippi River industrial corridor. The former DuPont (now Chemours/Koch) Lycra/spandex manufacturing operations historically made Clinton County one of the most electrically intensive industrial counties in Iowa. MidAmerican Energy serves the county. Clinton's position on the Mississippi River at the Iowa-Illinois border, with I-80 proximity (20 miles south), gives it connectivity to the Quad Cities\/Davenport fiber network. Eagle Point Park and the county's riverfront location add manufacturing and logistics operational context.

sources.label: Clinton County IA — City of Clinton

restrictions.name: Meade County
restrictions.title: Meade County SD — Sturgis/Ellsworth Air Force Base/B-1B Lancer wing
restrictions.description: Meade County (Sturgis) hosts Ellsworth Air Force Base — home of the 28th Bomb Wing operating B-1B Lancer strategic bombers, one of two B-1B bases in the US. Ellsworth's aviation fuel, communications, and IT infrastructure for a strategic bomber wing represent significant federal computing presence. The county's position west of Rapid City and the Black Hills, served by Black Hills Energy and NorthWestern Energy, adds the data center-friendly cold climate and South Dakota's favorable tax environment. The annual Sturgis Motorcycle Rally drives temporary connectivity demand as well.

sources.label: Black Hills Energy — Meade County SD

restrictions.name: Yankton County
restrictions.title: Yankton County SD — Yankton/Gavins Point Dam/Missouri River/Mount Marty University
restrictions.description: Yankton County (Yankton) anchors SE South Dakota on the Missouri River, adjacent to the Nebraska border. Gavins Point Dam (operated by the US Army Corps of Engineers) is the smallest of the six Missouri River mainstem dams, providing hydroelectric generation through Western Area Power Administration (WAPA). Mount Marty University and Yankton College give the county a private higher education presence. The county's position at the I-81/US-81 junction connecting Sioux Falls to Omaha and its Missouri River hydropower access give it secondary data center relevance.

sources.label: Gavins Point Dam — US Army Corps of Engineers

restrictions.name: Miller County
restrictions.title: Miller County AR — Texarkana AR/ArkLaTex bi-state hub/I-30 & I-49
restrictions.description: Miller County (Texarkana, AR) forms the Arkansas half of the Texarkana metro, a significant bi-state market straddling the AR-TX border. Texarkana is the commercial hub of the ArkLaTex region, with I-30 (connecting Dallas to Little Rock) and US-59 (connecting the Gulf Coast to the Midwest) intersecting in the metro area. Entergy Arkansas serves the county. The county's unique dual-state position creates interesting connectivity and jurisdiction dynamics, and the Texarkana metro's role as a regional logistics and distribution hub drives enterprise IT demand.

sources.label: Miller County AR — City of Texarkana AR

restrictions.name: White County
restrictions.title: White County AR — Searcy/Harding University/Little Rock north corridor
restrictions.description: White County (Searcy) is home to Harding University — a major private liberal arts university (STEM programs, business, law) with over 5,000 students. Located 50 miles north of Little Rock on US-67/US-167, Searcy's position on the LR north corridor and the Arkansas River Valley fiber spine gives it connectivity to Little Rock's metro network. Entergy Arkansas serves the county. Harding's computing infrastructure and the county's growing distribution and healthcare economy drive steady IT demand along the I-67/US-167 growth axis.

sources.label: White County AR — City of Searcy

restrictions.name: Warren County
restrictions.title: Warren County MS — Vicksburg/US Army Corps of Engineers Waterways Experiment Station
restrictions.description: Warren County (Vicksburg) is home to the US Army Engineer Research and Development Center (ERDC) — the Army Corps of Engineers' primary research facility, including the Waterways Experiment Station and the Engineer Research Center. ERDC conducts major research in geotechnical engineering, hydraulics, and environmental science, requiring significant high-performance computing infrastructure. Mississippi River levee management and flood control computing also flows through Vicksburg. Entergy Mississippi serves the county with competitive rates.

sources.label: US Army Engineer R&D Center (ERDC) — Vicksburg MS
Warren County MS — City of Vicksburg

restrictions.name: Ravalli County
restrictions.title: Ravalli County MT — Hamilton/Bitterroot Valley/Rocky Mountain Research Station
restrictions.description: Ravalli County (Hamilton) is the Bitterroot Valley south of Missoula — one of the fastest-growing rural areas in Montana due to migration from California, Washington, and Idaho. The USDA Forest Service Rocky Mountain Research Station in Hamilton provides federal research computing presence. NorthWestern Energy serves the county with Montana's hydropower-backed competitive rates. The Bitterroot Valley's cold climate is excellent for free-air cooling, and the county's growing tech-sector professional population creates emerging enterprise IT demand against a backdrop of very low land costs.

sources.label: Rocky Mountain Research Station — Hamilton MT
Ravalli County MT — City of Hamilton

restrictions.name: Marshall County
restrictions.title: Marshall County — Marshalltown/JBS Turkey/major meatpacking hub
restrictions.description: Marshalltown hosts JBS Turkey's large processing plant and a major Swift/JBS beef facility — among the largest employers in central Iowa. The industrial electrical infrastructure supporting high-throughput food processing gives MidAmerican Energy significant substation capacity in Marshall County. Marshalltown also has an Emerson Electric division and Fisher Controls (Emerson Automation Solutions) operations with embedded process control and industrial IT.

restrictions.name: Lee County
restrictions.title: Lee County — Keokuk/Fort Madison/Iowa Army Ammunition Plant
restrictions.description: Lee County contains the Iowa Army Ammunition Plant at Middletown — a major Defense Logistics Agency ammunition manufacturing and storage installation with embedded federal IT infrastructure. Fort Madison and Keokuk are the county's urban centers. The Mississippi River corridor provides both fiber infrastructure and cooling water access for potential data center development in southeast Iowa's industrial zone.

restrictions.name: Marion County
restrictions.title: Marion County — Knoxville/Pella Corp manufacturing hub
restrictions.description: Pella Corporation — the major window and door manufacturer — is headquartered in Pella in Marion County. Pella Corp's manufacturing operations and global supply chain management systems represent a significant industrial IT presence in rural Iowa. The county is also home to Knoxville, which hosts the Iowa Speedway and a regional hospital hub. MidAmerican Energy serves the area with competitive industrial rates.

restrictions.name: Warren County
restrictions.title: Warren County — Indianola/Des Moines south suburb growth corridor
restrictions.description: Warren County is one of the fastest-growing counties in Iowa, driven by Des Moines southward suburban expansion. Indianola hosts Simpson College and is the county seat. The I-35 corridor through Warren County is a key fiber route connecting Des Moines to Kansas City. As Des Moines has become a major Midwest data center hub, Warren County's suburban growth and MidAmerican Energy infrastructure make it a natural extension zone.

restrictions.name: Crawford County
restrictions.title: Crawford County — Pittsburg/Pittsburg State University/SE Kansas
restrictions.description: Pittsburg State University in Crawford County runs one of the largest technology programs in southeast Kansas, including the Kansas Technology Center. PSU's Gorilla Geothermal Energy Project and engineering programs drive campus IT infrastructure. Crawford County is also a regional hub for southeast Kansas commerce, with Evergy providing electrical service. The area's proximity to the Joplin, Missouri metro extends the tech ecosystem.

restrictions.name: Ford County
restrictions.title: Ford County — Dodge City/Tyson/Cargill beef processing/cattle country
restrictions.description: Dodge City in Ford County hosts both Tyson Fresh Meats and Cargill Meat Solutions beef processing plants — together processing thousands of cattle daily, making Ford County one of the largest beef processing concentrations in the world. The combined industrial electrical load drives extraordinary Evergy substation capacity in this southwest Kansas city. Kansas wind energy from surrounding counties adds renewable power access. A genuinely high-load industrial county often overlooked for data center potential.

restrictions.name: McPherson County
restrictions.title: McPherson County — McPherson/oil refining hub/HollyFrontier
restrictions.description: McPherson hosts HollyFrontier's (now HF Sinclair) crude oil refinery, one of the largest in Kansas. Oil refining is among the most electrically intensive industrial processes, creating high-capacity Evergy substation infrastructure in McPherson County. The county sits at the intersection of major natural gas pipelines and has historically attracted industrial operations requiring both power and feedstock access. McPherson College adds an education IT presence.

restrictions.name: McKenzie County
restrictions.title: McKenzie County — Watford City/Bakken core SCADA/oil field digitalization
restrictions.description: McKenzie County is at the geographic center of the Williston Basin Bakken oil play. Watford City has transformed from a small agricultural town into a major oil field services hub. The SCADA systems, real-time production monitoring, and data analytics infrastructure serving Bakken operators represents a concentrated and growing digital footprint. Basin Electric and MDU serve the county with industrial power. McKenzie County's oil production digitalization is among the most data-intensive in the Great Plains.

restrictions.name: Stutsman County
restrictions.title: Stutsman County — Jamestown/University of Jamestown/ND central hub
restrictions.description: Jamestown is the largest city in central North Dakota and a regional hub between Fargo and Bismarck on the I-94 corridor. The University of Jamestown anchors academic computing in the county. Jamestown hosts the North Dakota State Hospital and multiple state government facilities, creating a concentration of public-sector IT. Basin Electric's transmission infrastructure and North Dakota's favorable regulatory environment for data centers make Stutsman County an underappreciated secondary market.

restrictions.name: Latah County
restrictions.title: Latah County — Moscow/University of Idaho/WSU cross-border academic hub
restrictions.description: Moscow, Idaho hosts the University of Idaho, the state's flagship research university and land-grant institution. Just across the Washington state line is Washington State University in Pullman — making the Moscow-Pullman region one of the most concentrated academic computing corridors in the Pacific Northwest relative to population. Idaho Power's hydropower-backed rates are among the lowest in the US, and the bi-state university corridor provides fiber infrastructure and technical workforce unusual for a rural community.

restrictions.name: Elmore County
restrictions.title: Elmore County — Mountain Home AFB/F-15E Strike Eagle/Idaho military IT
restrictions.description: Mountain Home Air Force Base in Elmore County is the home of the 366th Fighter Wing and F-15E Strike Eagle operations. Mountain Home AFB hosts the Air Force's premier composite wing, with integrated command, control, and intelligence systems requiring significant federal IT infrastructure. The base is one of Idaho's largest employers. Idaho Power provides the area with hydropower-backed electricity at competitive rates, and the high desert location provides natural cooling advantages for data center operations.

restrictions.name: Gage County
restrictions.title: Gage County — Beatrice/SE Nebraska agricultural hub/NPPD
restrictions.description: Beatrice is the largest city in southeast Nebraska and a regional commercial hub. Gage County's agricultural processing sector — including grain elevators and ethanol facilities — drives NPPD electrical infrastructure in the area. Nebraska Public Power District's low rates and cooperative structure make Gage County attractive for large-load users. The county is connected to Lincoln via US-77, providing fiber corridor access to Nebraska's primary data center market.

restrictions.name: Otoe County
restrictions.title: Otoe County — Nebraska City/Arbor Day Farm/I-29 Missouri River corridor
restrictions.description: Nebraska City sits on the Missouri River in Otoe County, a key position on the I-29 north-south fiber corridor connecting Omaha to Kansas City. The Arbor Day Foundation headquarters and Arbor Day Farm are here. Nebraska City has historically been a fruit and agricultural processing hub. NPPD and Omaha Public Power District's competitive rates serve the county. The Missouri River position gives Otoe County infrastructure advantages similar to other river-corridor data center markets.

restrictions.name: Jefferson County
restrictions.title: Jefferson County — Pine Bluff/UAPB/Dillard's HQ/AR southeast hub
restrictions.description: Pine Bluff is the largest city in southeast Arkansas and home to the University of Arkansas at Pine Bluff (UAPB), one of the state's historically Black universities with STEM programs. The Pine Bluff Arsenal — an Army chemical weapons demilitarization facility — represents federal IT infrastructure in the county. Jefferson County's position on the Arkansas River and rail network has historically supported industrial operations. Entergy Arkansas provides competitive rates in the region.

restrictions.name: Lonoke County
restrictions.title: Lonoke County — Cabot/Little Rock NE fastest-growing AR suburb
restrictions.description: Lonoke County is the fastest-growing county in Arkansas, driven by Little Rock's northeastern suburban expansion. Cabot is the county's largest city and one of Arkansas's fastest-growing communities. The I-40 and US-67/167 corridor through Lonoke County carries major fiber routes between Little Rock and Memphis. Entergy Arkansas serves the area. As the Little Rock metro expands, Lonoke County's suburban data center and technology infrastructure is growing to serve the region's residential and commercial base.

restrictions.name: Richland County
restrictions.title: Richland County — Sidney/Montana Bakken oil/eastern MT hub
restrictions.description: Sidney in Richland County sits in the heart of Montana's portion of the Williston Basin. The Montana Bakken oil play drives SCADA and production data infrastructure through MonDak (Montana-North Dakota) basin operators. Sidney is the commercial hub for eastern Montana's agricultural and energy economy. NorthWestern Energy and Basin Electric serve the area. Richland County's combination of energy industry data needs and Montana's low regulatory burden makes it a logical market for oil-field adjacent computing infrastructure.

restrictions.name: Hill County
restrictions.title: Hill County — Havre/MSU Northern/northern hi-line rail corridor
restrictions.description: Havre sits on the Hi-Line — the Burlington Northern Santa Fe main line across northern Montana — and is home to Montana State University-Northern, with programs in diesel technology, aviation, and engineering. The BNSF rail corridor through Hill County provides fiber infrastructure along one of the most important freight rail routes in North America. MSU Northern's technical programs provide workforce for industrial IT. Hill County's position as the largest city between Great Falls and the North Dakota border makes it a natural regional hub.

restrictions.name: Jones County
restrictions.title: Jones County — Laurel/Sanderson Farms/south-central MS poultry hub
restrictions.description: Laurel in Jones County is a regional hub for south-central Mississippi. Sanderson Farms (now Wayne-Sanderson) operates major poultry processing in the area, with industrial electrical loads driving Entergy Mississippi substation infrastructure. The University of Southern Mississippi's Hattiesburg campus is nearby. Jones County's position on US-59 and the Mississippi Power service territory provides connectivity to the Gulf Coast corridor. The county has seen television production interest due to the HGTV show 'Home Town,' bringing broader economic attention.

restrictions.name: Jasper County
restrictions.title: Jasper County — Joplin/4th largest MO city/tri-state hub
restrictions.description: Joplin is Missouri's fourth-largest city and the commercial hub of the Missouri-Kansas-Oklahoma tri-state area. Jasper County's central position in the Joplin metro makes it a regional center for healthcare (Mercy Hospital Joplin, Freeman Health System), education (Missouri Southern State University), and commerce. Empire District Electric (now Liberty Utilities/Algonquin) provides the area. The I-44/US-71 corridor through Joplin carries major fiber routes, and the tri-state market serves a regional population well beyond Jasper County itself.

restrictions.name: Washington County
restrictions.title: Washington County — Greenville/Mississippi Delta hub/port
restrictions.description: Greenville is the largest city in the Mississippi Delta and the commercial hub of Washington County. The Port of Greenville on the Mississippi River handles agricultural and industrial cargo, with logistics IT requirements. Mississippi Delta Community College serves the area. Entergy Mississippi provides electrical service. While the Delta faces economic challenges, Greenville's role as the region's primary city gives it concentrations of healthcare, government, and financial IT infrastructure absent from smaller Delta counties.

restrictions.name: Harrison County
restrictions.title: Harrison County — Clarksburg/FBI CJIS Division/federal IT hub
restrictions.description: Clarksburg in Harrison County is home to the FBI's Criminal Justice Information Services Division — the FBI's largest division and one of the most significant federal IT installations in the eastern United States outside Northern Virginia. CJIS manages the National Crime Information Center, fingerprint databases, and background check systems for law enforcement nationwide. The CJIS facility's data and computing infrastructure requirements are substantial, making Harrison County a genuinely high-value federal technology county in a state often underrepresented in data center analyses.

restrictions.name: Hancock County
restrictions.title: Hancock County — Weirton/Chester/northern WV panhandle steel heritage
restrictions.description: Weirton in Hancock County sits at the northernmost tip of West Virginia's panhandle, surrounded by Pennsylvania and Ohio. Weirton Steel — once one of the largest steel producers in the US — left behind extraordinary high-voltage electrical infrastructure sized for industrial smelting. Appalachian Power's transmission capacity in the panhandle is substantial. The Ohio River corridor through Hancock County provides cooling water access and fiber routes connecting to Pittsburgh's technology market just 40 miles north.

restrictions.name: Greenbrier County
restrictions.title: Greenbrier County — Lewisburg/The Greenbrier Cold War bunker/Appalachian hub
restrictions.description: The Greenbrier Resort in White Sulphur Springs was built atop a classified Cold War congressional bunker — a 112,000-square-foot underground facility maintained as a continuity-of-government site for Congress until decommissioned in 1995. The facility's extraordinary security and utility infrastructure has since been repurposed as a data center, making Greenbrier County one of the most distinctive data center locations in the US. Lewisburg is also a regional hub for southern West Virginia's tourism and healthcare economy.

restrictions.name: Logan County
restrictions.title: Logan County — Logan/southern WV coal/Appalachian Power legacy load
restrictions.description: Logan County is at the heart of southern West Virginia's coal mining economy. The legacy coal mining and processing operations in the Guyandotte and Man Creek watersheds created Appalachian Power's most intensive load zones in rural WV. As coal employment has declined, Logan County's fiber and power infrastructure — substantially overbuilt for mining-era demand — creates latent capacity at favorable economics. Logan Regional Medical Center is the county's largest employer and IT anchor today.

restrictions.name: Butler County
restrictions.title: Butler County — Poplar Bluff/SE Missouri regional hub/3 Rivers College
restrictions.description: Poplar Bluff is the largest city in southeast Missouri and the commercial and medical hub of the Missouri Bootheel region. Poplar Bluff Regional Medical Center and the VA Medical Center are major employers. 3 Rivers College provides a technology pipeline. US-60 and US-67 make Poplar Bluff the crossroads of a large rural region. Empire Electric (now Liberty Utilities) and Missouri's competitive rural electric cooperative rates serve the county. The city's role as a regional healthcare and retail destination creates concentrated IT demand for a sub-50,000 population center.

restrictions.name: New Madrid County
restrictions.title: New Madrid County — New Madrid/Mississippi River/Ameren nuclear plant
restrictions.description: New Madrid County sits on the Mississippi River in Missouri's Bootheel, adjacent to the New Madrid Seismic Zone — the most significant earthquake risk area in the central US. Ameren Missouri's New Madrid Power Plant (coal, now transitioning) and the area's river port infrastructure created a substantial Ameren transmission footprint in rural Missouri. The county's position at the confluence of major barge routes means fiber runs along the river corridor. New Madrid's agricultural processing and river port activity drive commercial IT needs beyond what its population suggests.

restrictions.name: Calloway County
restrictions.title: Calloway County — Murray/Murray State University/western KY university hub
restrictions.description: Murray State University in Calloway County is one of Kentucky's regional comprehensive universities, with approximately 10,000 students and a notable telecommunications and computer science program. Murray is the largest city in western Kentucky between Paducah and Tennessee. The county's rural fiber infrastructure has been enhanced by TVA's Kentucky Lake corridor and West Kentucky Rural Electric Cooperative. Murray State's STEM programs and the Kentucky Innovation Network presence provide a technical workforce pipeline for western Kentucky's growing technology economy.

restrictions.name: Muhlenberg County
restrictions.title: Muhlenberg County — Greenville/Western KY coal belt/Green River power legacy
restrictions.description: Muhlenberg County is in the heart of western Kentucky's coal country — a region made famous by John Prine's 'Paradise' and by one of the most intensive coal mining and power generation landscapes in America. The Paradise Fossil Plant (TVA) on the Green River was the largest coal power plant in the US for decades. Legacy transmission infrastructure from mining and power generation eras creates substantial electrical grid capacity in Muhlenberg County at favorable TVA power cost rates. Reduced mining activity has left excess capacity potentially available for industrial computing uses.

restrictions.name: Rowan County
restrictions.title: Rowan County — Morehead/Morehead State University/eastern KY university
restrictions.description: Morehead State University in Rowan County serves as the primary economic anchor for northeastern Kentucky. MSU's telecommunications and computer science programs have been enhanced by the Kentucky Appalachian Technology Center. Morehead's position on US-60 and I-64 provides connectivity between Lexington and Huntington, WV. East Kentucky Power Cooperative serves the area with rates competitive for eastern Kentucky. As coal-dependent counties seek economic diversification, Morehead State's tech programs make Rowan County one of the better-positioned counties in the Appalachian transition.

restrictions.name: Barren County
restrictions.title: Barren County — Glasgow/Mammoth Cave region/south-central KY hub
restrictions.description: Glasgow in Barren County is the commercial hub of south-central Kentucky, near Mammoth Cave National Park. The county has historically attracted manufacturing — Fruit of the Loom, L&N Federal Credit Union, and medical device companies. South Central Kentucky Community and Technical College serves the area. Glasgow's position on the US-31E/US-68 corridor gives it regional centrality in a part of Kentucky otherwise lacking urban anchors. Kentucky Utilities provides competitive power rates. The Mammoth Cave-area tourism economy adds hospitality IT demand beyond the county's manufacturing base.

restrictions.name: Grant County
restrictions.title: Grant County — Williamstown/Answers in Genesis/Cincinnati exurb growth
restrictions.description: Grant County in northern Kentucky is notable for hosting the Ark Encounter theme park and the nearby Creation Museum — Answers in Genesis attractions that collectively draw hundreds of thousands of visitors annually and require substantial IT infrastructure for ticketing, media, and visitor management. The county's position on I-75 between Cincinnati and Lexington makes it one of the faster-growing northern Kentucky exurban counties. Duke Energy Kentucky serves the area with transmission access from the Cincinnati metro's grid.

restrictions.name: Hughes County
restrictions.title: Hughes County — Pierre/South Dakota state capital/Bureau of Indian Affairs
restrictions.description: Pierre is South Dakota's state capital and Hughes County's major city. State government IT consolidation concentrates data centers, disaster recovery systems, and agency IT in Pierre. The Bureau of Indian Affairs and other federal agencies serving the large Native American population in the Dakotas maintain significant operations in Pierre. East River Electric and Northwestern Energy serve the area. As the smallest state capital by population in the continental US, Pierre has an outsized government IT footprint relative to its size.

restrictions.name: Beadle County
restrictions.title: Beadle County — Huron/South Dakota State Fair/turkey processing
restrictions.description: Huron is the host city of the South Dakota State Fair and one of the state's largest turkey processing centers. Dakota Provisions turkey processing plant is a major electrical load in Beadle County. East River Electric Cooperative serves the area with Missouri River hydropower access through Basin Electric. Huron's position as the geographic center of South Dakota gives it logistical centrality for a state with dispersed population. Huron University provides a technology and business education presence.

restrictions.name: Ramsey County
restrictions.title: Ramsey County — Devils Lake/flooding management/USACE hydrological IT
restrictions.description: Devils Lake in Ramsey County sits at the edge of the Devils Lake Basin — one of the most actively managed hydrological systems in the US. The lake has risen dramatically since the 1990s, requiring ongoing Army Corps of Engineers management, flood monitoring, and outlet control operations. The USACE's hydrological data infrastructure, gauging networks, and real-time flood management systems represent a unique federal computing footprint in a rural North Dakota county. Lake Region State College provides a workforce education presence in the county.

restrictions.name: Barnes County
restrictions.title: Barnes County — Valley City/Valley City State University/BNSF mainline
restrictions.description: Valley City is home to Valley City State University and sits on the BNSF main line across North Dakota. VCSU has developed a notable technology education program. The Interstate 94 corridor through Barnes County carries major fiber routes connecting Fargo to Bismarck. Basin Electric and Otter Tail Power serve the area. Valley City's position between Fargo and Jamestown on ND's primary east-west corridor gives it infrastructure access unusual for a county of its size. VCSU's technology and teacher education programs represent intellectual infrastructure beyond what the rural setting suggests.

restrictions.name: Angelina County
restrictions.title: Angelina County — Lufkin/Angelina College/deep East Texas timber/Stephen F Austin
restrictions.description: Lufkin in Angelina County is the commercial hub of deep East Texas, serving a large rural region in the Piney Woods. Angelina College and the Stephen F. Austin State University campus extension serve the area. Lufkin Industries (now NOV) was historically a major manufacturer of oilfield pumping units and power transmission products — creating an industrial IT base in the county. Entergy Texas provides service to the area. Lufkin's position on US-59 and SH-69 makes it the retail and healthcare center for a multi-county rural region, generating concentrated IT demand.

restrictions.name: Brown County
restrictions.title: Brown County — Brownwood/Howard Payne University/central Texas crossroads
restrictions.description: Brownwood in Brown County is a regional hub in the Texas Hill Country/Cross Timbers transition zone. Howard Payne University anchors the county's education sector. Brownwood has historically served as the commercial and medical center for a large rural region of west-central Texas. The Brownwood Medical Center and multiple state facilities create a public-sector IT concentration. AEP West Texas provides electrical service. US-377 and US-183 corridors make Brownwood a waypoint for regional fiber routing through central Texas.

restrictions.name: Rusk County
restrictions.title: Rusk County — Henderson/East Texas oil heritage/SWB legacy infrastructure
restrictions.description: Rusk County is the birthplace of the East Texas Oil Field — the largest oil reservoir ever discovered in the lower 48 states at the time of its 1930 discovery. The oil field's legacy of pipeline infrastructure, gathering systems, and production data management created early industrial IT infrastructure in Henderson and Kilgore. SWB (now AT&T) historically ran major telecommunications trunk routes through East Texas. Entergy Texas serves the area. While the supergiant field's production has declined, the pipeline and industrial infrastructure legacy gives Rusk County energy-sector data roots older than most Texas tech markets.

restrictions.name: Wilson County
restrictions.title: Wilson County — Floresville/San Antonio SE suburb/fastest-growing SA exurb
restrictions.description: Wilson County is one of the fastest-growing counties in Texas, driven by San Antonio's southeastern suburban expansion. Floresville, the county seat, is increasingly connected to the San Antonio metro via US-181 and SH-97. As San Antonio has grown into a major military and healthcare data center market (Joint Base San Antonio, South Texas Medical Center), Wilson County's suburban position on the edge of that metro makes it an emerging edge-data-center market. CPS Energy's service territory extends into the county, providing San Antonio's competitive electricity pricing.

restrictions.name: Wood County
restrictions.title: Wood County — Quitman/Mineola/East Texas piney woods/SWB fiber corridor
restrictions.description: Wood County in East Texas includes Mineola — a historic Texas railroad junction town at the intersection of major BNSF lines and, critically, historic AT&T/SWB fiber trunk routes through East Texas. Mineola's railroad heritage means it sits on east-west and north-south infrastructure corridors. Oncor serves the county with transmission access from the ERCOT grid. The Sabine River Authority and Lake Fork Reservoir represent water resources that could support data center cooling. Wood County's rural character with legacy fiber infrastructure is a combination that has attracted technology investment in similar East Texas counties.

restrictions.name: Houston County
restrictions.title: Houston County — Dothan/Wiregrass hub/SE Alabama commerce and healthcare
restrictions.description: Dothan is the largest city in southeast Alabama and the undisputed commercial and healthcare hub of the Wiregrass region — a multi-state trade area spanning southeast Alabama, southwest Georgia, and northwest Florida. Southeast Health (formerly Southeast Alabama Medical Center) and other healthcare systems make Dothan a regional medical center. The National Peanut Festival, agricultural processing operations, and retail concentration drive IT demand. Alabama Power's substation infrastructure serves significant industrial and commercial load in Houston County.

restrictions.name: Russell County
restrictions.title: Russell County — Phenix City/Fort Benning metro/Columbus GA cross-border
restrictions.description: Russell County sits across the Chattahoochee River from Columbus, Georgia, forming a bi-state metro with Fort Benning (now Fort Moore) — home of the US Army Infantry School and one of the largest Army bases in the country. Phenix City is the Alabama side of this metro. The Columbus/Fort Moore military digital ecosystem — Army Cyber Center of Excellence, National Infantry Museum, massive simulation and training systems — creates substantial bi-state federal IT infrastructure in which Russell County participates. Alabama Power serves the area.

restrictions.name: DeKalb County
restrictions.title: DeKalb County — Fort Payne/sock capital/NE Alabama textile manufacturing
restrictions.description: Fort Payne in DeKalb County is the hosiery and sock manufacturing capital of the world — with over 100 sock mills concentrated in the county. This highly specialized manufacturing sector created industrial electrical infrastructure sized for large-scale textile operations. DeKalb County's position on Lookout Mountain in northeast Alabama puts it on the I-59 corridor connecting Birmingham to Chattanooga. Northeast Alabama Electric Cooperative serves the area. The county's unique industrial concentration makes it an atypical but genuine industrial power market in a rural Alabama county.

restrictions.name: Coffee County
restrictions.title: Coffee County — Enterprise/Fort Rucker/US Army Aviation Center
restrictions.description: Coffee County hosts Fort Rucker (now Fort Novosel) — the US Army Aviation Center of Excellence and the home of Army helicopter training. Nearly every Army aviator learns to fly at Fort Rucker/Novosel, and the base's flight simulation, maintenance, and training IT systems represent a major federal computing concentration in rural Alabama. Enterprise State Community College complements the military workforce. Alabama Power and Central Alabama Electric Cooperative serve the county. The Army Aviation Center's digital infrastructure for flight training simulation is among the most sophisticated in the military education system.

restrictions.name: Sandoval County
restrictions.title: Sandoval County — Rio Rancho/Intel fab/NM's 3rd largest city
restrictions.description: Rio Rancho in Sandoval County is New Mexico's third-largest city and home to Intel's massive semiconductor fabrication campus — one of the largest chip manufacturing facilities in the US outside Intel's Oregon and Arizona sites. Intel's Rio Rancho operations employ thousands and represent some of the most power-intensive industrial operations in the state, creating extraordinary PNM (Public Service New Mexico) substation capacity in Sandoval County. The adjacent Sandia National Laboratories and Kirtland AFB in Bernalillo County extend the high-tech ecosystem north into Rio Rancho. A major omission from the database now corrected.

restrictions.name: McKinley County
restrictions.title: McKinley County — Gallup/Route 66/coal power legacy/Navajo Nation hub
restrictions.description: Gallup in McKinley County is the largest city on Route 66 in New Mexico and the commercial hub for the Navajo Nation and surrounding tribal lands. McKinley County has historically hosted major coal mining operations (Navajo Mine) and coal-fired power generation (Four Corners Power Plant and Navajo Generating Station nearby). Legacy transmission infrastructure from the coal power era created high-capacity Western Area Power Administration grid infrastructure in the region. The Navajo Nation's digital infrastructure needs — tribal government IT, healthcare, education — are centered in Gallup as the nearest large commercial city.

restrictions.name: San Juan County
restrictions.title: San Juan County — Farmington/Four Corners energy/NM natural gas hub
restrictions.description: San Juan County in northwest New Mexico is the center of the San Juan Basin — one of the most productive natural gas fields in the US. Farmington serves as the oil and gas industry's regional hub, with pipeline gathering, compression, and processing operations requiring extensive SCADA and digital monitoring infrastructure. The area's energy sector has driven Farmington Municipal Utilities and New Mexico Gas Company infrastructure well beyond what the population requires. As coal (San Juan Generating Station) transitions and natural gas remains critical, Farmington's energy IT sector continues to be the most data-intensive in rural New Mexico.

restrictions.name: Taos County
restrictions.title: Taos County — Taos/arts colony/Los Alamos National Lab spillover/NM northern hub
restrictions.description: Taos County sits in the northern New Mexico mountains and hosts one of the most concentrated artist and creative communities in the US — with attendant high-speed internet and technology infrastructure to support remote workers, galleries, and tourism. The proximity to Los Alamos National Laboratory (35028) — just 60 miles south — means that LANL employees, contractors, and researchers live in Taos, creating a technically sophisticated residential population. Kit Carson Electric Cooperative has invested significantly in fiber infrastructure for the county. Taos Pueblo is a UNESCO World Heritage Site with tribal government IT requirements.

restrictions.name: Pittsburg County
restrictions.title: Pittsburg County — McAlester/Army Ammunition Plant/naval munitions
restrictions.description: McAlester Army Ammunition Plant (MCAAP) in Pittsburg County is the US Army's primary facility for manufacturing and storing conventional ammunition and bombs. MCAAP produces the majority of the bombs dropped by US forces globally, and its production, inventory, and quality control systems represent a critical and highly specialized federal IT infrastructure concentration. East Central University serves the county academically. PSO (Public Service Oklahoma) provides electrical service. Pittsburg County's status as the center of the US Army's conventional ordnance production chain makes it a genuinely unique federal computing county often overlooked in commercial analyses.

restrictions.name: Bryan County
restrictions.title: Bryan County — Durant/Choctaw Nation HQ/Southeastern Oklahoma State
restrictions.description: Durant in Bryan County is the headquarters of the Choctaw Nation of Oklahoma — the third-largest federally recognized tribe in the US, with over 200,000 enrolled members. The Choctaw Nation's tribal government IT, healthcare (Choctaw Nation Health Services), and expanding gaming and hospitality enterprise (Durant's WinStar World Casino is the world's largest casino by gambling floor area) creates substantial data center demand. Southeastern Oklahoma State University adds an academic IT presence. AEP/PSO serves the county. The Choctaw Nation's IT infrastructure investment in Durant makes Bryan County an unusual and underappreciated computing market.

restrictions.name: Carter County
restrictions.title: Carter County — Ardmore/southern Oklahoma oil and gas hub
restrictions.description: Ardmore in Carter County is southern Oklahoma's largest city and a regional hub for the oil and gas industry in the Ardmore Basin. The Greater Seminole Oil Field and Healdton Oil Field legacy in Carter County created significant refinery and pipeline infrastructure. Chickasaw Nation has a major economic presence in Ardmore, with tribal healthcare and enterprise operations. The US Army Ammunition Depot (Lone Star Army Ammunition Plant) is just across the Texas border in the neighboring market. AEP/PSO and Carter County rural co-ops provide electrical service at Oklahoma's competitive rates.

restrictions.name: Kandiyohi County
restrictions.title: Kandiyohi County — Willmar/Jennie-O Turkey/CentraCare west MN hub
restrictions.description: Willmar in Kandiyohi County is the commercial and healthcare hub of west-central Minnesota. Jennie-O Turkey Store (Hormel subsidiary) operates one of its largest turkey processing facilities in Willmar, driving industrial electrical load for Xcel Energy. CentraCare's Rice Memorial Hospital serves as the regional medical center. Ridgewater College provides a technical education pipeline. Willmar's position on US-12 and US-71 makes it the largest city between Minneapolis and Sioux Falls in the cross-state corridor. Minnesota's favorable data center tax exemptions apply to the county.

restrictions.name: Crow Wing County
restrictions.title: Crow Wing County — Brainerd/lakes region/tourism IT/Minnesota National Guard
restrictions.description: Brainerd in Crow Wing County is the gateway to Minnesota's lakes region — one of the most tourism-intensive areas in the Midwest, with hundreds of resorts, cabins, and lakes. The tourism economy drives significant hospitality IT, reservation systems, and broadband infrastructure. Camp Ripley (Morrison County adjacent) hosts Minnesota's largest National Guard base and joint training center, with embedded military IT infrastructure. Crow Wing County Electric Cooperative and Minnesota Power serve the area. Brainerd's position as the lakes region hub makes it a significant secondary data market for a state with strong data center tax incentives.

restrictions.name: Chisago County
restrictions.title: Chisago County — North Branch/Twin Cities NE exurban growth corridor
restrictions.description: Chisago County is one of Minnesota's fastest-growing counties, driven by the Twin Cities' northeastern suburban expansion along the I-35 corridor. North Branch and Chisago City are the major growth centers. The county's position northeast of the Twin Cities metro on US-8 and I-35 makes it a natural edge-data-center market as the metro expands. Xcel Energy extends transmission coverage from the metro. Minnesota's favorable data center exemption from sales tax on electricity and equipment makes the TC exurban ring — including Chisago County — a cost-competitive environment for secondary facilities serving the Minneapolis-St. Paul market.

restrictions.name: Dakota County
restrictions.title: Dakota County — South Sioux City/Tyson beef/MidAmerican/Sioux City metro NE
restrictions.description: Dakota County is the Nebraska side of the Sioux City metro — the commercial hub of the Siouxland tri-state area (Iowa, Nebraska, South Dakota). South Sioux City hosts a major Tyson Fresh Meats beef processing facility and other food processing operations, creating substantial MidAmerican Energy substation capacity in the county. The Sioux City metro's bi-state fiber infrastructure runs through Dakota County. Nebraska's favorable tax and regulatory environment for data centers, combined with MidAmerican Energy's competitive industrial rates, makes Dakota County a natural industrial data center market in an already-documented metro.

restrictions.name: Colfax County
restrictions.title: Colfax County — Schuyler/Tyson pork processing/Columbus NE adjacent
restrictions.description: Schuyler in Colfax County hosts a major Tyson Fresh Meats pork processing facility — one of the largest pork plants in Nebraska, employing over 2,000 workers. The industrial electrical load from this facility drives NPPD substation infrastructure in Colfax County beyond what the rural population requires. Schuyler is adjacent to Columbus (Platte County, already in DB) and shares the Platte River corridor's infrastructure advantages. Nebraska Public Power District's competitive rates and Nebraska's favorable data center regulatory environment extend to Colfax County. The county's food processing industrial load creates a favorable co-location environment.

restrictions.name: Harrison County
restrictions.title: Harrison County — Missouri Valley/Loess Hills/I-29 corridor Iowa
restrictions.description: Harrison County contains Missouri Valley — a significant I-29 waypoint between Omaha and Sioux City on the Missouri River corridor. The I-29 corridor through western Iowa is a major fiber route connecting the Omaha and Kansas City metros. MidAmerican Energy's infrastructure in Harrison County benefits from the county's position between two of the Midwest's major data center markets. The Loess Hills terrain provides topographic diversity for infrastructure placement. Harrison County's position on the I-29 corridor makes it a natural intermediate point for the Omaha-to-Sioux City fiber route.

restrictions.name: Floyd County
restrictions.title: Floyd County — Charles City/north-central Iowa agricultural processing
restrictions.description: Charles City in Floyd County is a north-central Iowa hub with Decker Truck Line headquarters and significant agricultural processing operations. The Floyd County area has industrial manufacturing history including Floyd River valley agriculture. MidAmerican Energy and Alliant Energy serve portions of the county. Floyd County's position on US-218 and Iowa 346 connects it to the Mason City (Cerro Gordo County, already in DB) market to the north. The county's agricultural processing base creates an industrial power profile suitable for data center co-location.

restrictions.name: Bremer County
restrictions.title: Bremer County — Waverly/Wartburg College/northeast Iowa hub
restrictions.description: Waverly in Bremer County hosts Wartburg College — a liberal arts institution with strong computer science and technology programs. The county sits on the Cedar River and connects to the Waterloo/Cedar Falls metro (Black Hawk County, already in DB) 20 miles to the south. Alliant Energy serves the county. Bremer County's position in the Cedar River agricultural corridor supports grain processing and turkey processing operations that drive regional industrial electrical infrastructure. Waverly's college presence and proximity to the Waterloo market make Bremer County a natural secondary data market for northeastern Iowa.

restrictions.name: Webster County
restrictions.title: Webster County — Marshfield/I-44 fiber corridor/Springfield east suburb
restrictions.description: Webster County sits on I-44 immediately east of Springfield (Greene County, already in DB) — Missouri's third-largest city. Marshfield is the county seat. The I-44 fiber corridor through Webster County carries major communications routes between Springfield and St. Louis. As Springfield's economy and population expand, Webster County has become an active exurban growth market. Empire Electric (now Liberty Utilities) provides service. Marshfield's proximity to Missouri State University and the Springfield healthcare cluster in adjacent Greene County makes Webster County part of the functional Springfield metro data market.

restrictions.name: Montgomery County
restrictions.title: Montgomery County — Independence/Coffeyville/SE Kansas refining corridor
restrictions.description: Montgomery County in southeast Kansas contains both Independence (county seat) and Coffeyville, where Coffeyville Resources operates a crude oil refinery — one of the larger independent refineries in the Mid-Continent region. Oil refining creates extraordinary Evergy electrical infrastructure requirements in rural Kansas. Coffeyville Community College provides a technical education pipeline. The county's position on US-160 and the Verdigris River valley connects it to Tulsa's oil and gas market to the south. The Coffeyville Resources refinery's electrical load gives Montgomery County industrial power infrastructure well beyond its population.

restrictions.name: Cowley County
restrictions.title: Cowley County — Arkansas City/Winfield/south-central Kansas oil and education
restrictions.description: Cowley County contains Arkansas City (the county's largest city and a historic oil boom town near the Oklahoma border) and Winfield (county seat and home of Southwestern College). The area sits at the north end of the Osage oil fields that extend into Oklahoma. Winfield Correctional Facility and Winfield's historic Flint Hills area attract state agency IT. Evergy and the Arkansas City municipal utility serve the county. Cowley College in Arkansas City provides a technical education base for the south-central Kansas workforce.

restrictions.name: Barton County
restrictions.title: Barton County — Great Bend/Quivira/central Kansas oil production
restrictions.description: Great Bend in Barton County sits at the geographic center of Kansas and at the heart of the Central Kansas oil fields. Barton County Community College provides a petroleum technology and workforce education program serving the energy sector. The county's position on US-56 and the Arkansas River valley gives it infrastructure connectivity to Wichita. Evergy serves the county with access to Kansas wind energy from the surrounding Great Plains belt. Great Bend's role as a regional hub for central Kansas agriculture and energy industries creates concentrated IT demand for a city of its size.

restrictions.name: Franklin County
restrictions.title: Franklin County — Ottawa/Ottawa University/I-35 KC metro south corridor
restrictions.description: Ottawa in Franklin County sits on I-35 between Kansas City and Wichita — a position on one of the country's major north-south fiber corridors. Ottawa University is a Baptist-affiliated liberal arts institution with a growing online education division. The county's proximity to the Kansas City metro (~60 miles south on I-35) makes it a potential exurban edge-data-center location within the KC market's reach. Evergy serves Franklin County with transmission access from the KC metro grid. I-35 fiber runs through the county connecting KCK to the Wichita market.

restrictions.name: Grundy County
restrictions.title: Grundy County — Morris/Dresden Nuclear Station/I-80 corridor Illinois
restrictions.description: Morris in Grundy County is home to Exelon's Dresden Nuclear Generating Station — the first US nuclear plant built entirely with private funding and one of the oldest operating reactors in the US. Dresden's two boiling water reactors represent significant electrical generation infrastructure in Grundy County. The county also sits on I-80, Illinois's primary east-west freight corridor carrying major fiber routes. Commonwealth Edison (ComEd) transmission infrastructure in Grundy County is sized to transport nuclear generation, creating grid capacity substantially in excess of local demand.

restrictions.name: Vermilion County
restrictions.title: Vermilion County — Danville/east-central Illinois/Lakeview College of Nursing
restrictions.description: Danville in Vermilion County sits at the Illinois-Indiana border on the I-74 corridor connecting Indianapolis to Champaign-Urbana. The county is served by Ameren Illinois. Danville Area Community College and Lakeview College of Nursing anchor local educational IT. Danville's historic manufacturing base — Hyatt bearings, Quaker Oats, Fischer Carbon — created industrial electrical infrastructure that outlasts the original facilities. As an I-74 waypoint between two major university markets (Champaign-Urbana to the west, Indianapolis to the east), Vermilion County has fiber corridor access beyond what its standalone market would justify.

restrictions.name: Tazewell County
restrictions.title: Tazewell County — Pekin/Morton/Illinois River ag processing/Caterpillar south
restrictions.description: Tazewell County borders Peoria County (already in DB) to the north and contains Pekin (a major Illinois River port city) and Morton (the 'pumpkin capital of the world' and a significant agricultural processing center). Tazewell County's Illinois River position connects it to barge transport and the agricultural commodity infrastructure of the river corridor. Ameren Illinois serves the county. Morton Foods' pumpkin processing and other agricultural operations drive seasonal but substantial industrial electrical loads. The county's adjacency to Peoria's industrial base and Caterpillar's supply chain gives it stronger data infrastructure than its standalone size suggests.

restrictions.name: Stephenson County
restrictions.title: Stephenson County — Freeport/northwest Illinois manufacturing/Lincoln-Douglas
restrictions.description: Freeport in Stephenson County is a northwest Illinois manufacturing hub with historic ties to the Honeywell/Burgess Battery industrial legacy and Newell Brands (formerly Newell Rubbermaid). The city hosted one of the Lincoln-Douglas debates. Highland Community College provides a technical education pipeline. Illinois Route 20 and US-20 run through Freeport connecting it to Rockford (Winnebago County, already in DB) to the east and Galena to the west. ComEd and MidAmerican Energy's northwest Illinois grid serves the county. Freeport's manufacturing history created industrial electrical infrastructure that supports modern industrial computing loads.

restrictions.name: Mississippi County
restrictions.title: Mississippi County — Blytheville/Big River Steel/Osceola steel mills/AR northeast
restrictions.description: Mississippi County in northeast Arkansas hosts one of the most dramatic industrial transformations in the US South. Blytheville Air Force Base (closed 1991) was redeveloped into a major steel manufacturing cluster: Steel Technologies, Nucor Steel, and most significantly Big River Steel (now US Steel) — a 3.3-million-square-foot advanced electric arc furnace mill representing the most technologically advanced flat-rolled steel facility in North America at opening. The industrial electrical load from steel EAF operations is extraordinary, driving Entergy Arkansas transmission infrastructure in Mississippi County that rivals major metropolitan areas. A major industrial AI/IT market.

restrictions.name: Mesa County
restrictions.title: Mesa County — Grand Junction/western slope largest city/energy/federal lands
restrictions.description: Grand Junction is the largest city on Colorado's western slope and the commercial hub for a vast region covering western Colorado, eastern Utah, and northwest New Mexico. Mesa County's economy is driven by oil and gas extraction in the Piceance Basin, agriculture (Colorado's largest peach-producing county), and federal land management (BLM's Grand Junction field office is one of the largest in the US). Xcel Energy and Grand Junction municipal utility serve the area. The county's regional centrality — sitting at the junction of I-70 and US-50 — makes it the most significant secondary data center market between Denver and Salt Lake City.

restrictions.name: La Plata County
restrictions.title: La Plata County — Durango/Fort Lewis College/Four Corners energy/Southwest CO
restrictions.description: Durango in La Plata County is the commercial and cultural hub of southwest Colorado's Four Corners region. Fort Lewis College provides a technology and engineering education base. La Plata County sits atop the San Juan Basin natural gas fields shared with New Mexico's San Juan County. The county's position on US-160 and US-550 (the 'Million Dollar Highway') gives it connectivity to the broader Four Corners energy market. La Plata Electric Association serves the county with access to the region's renewable energy resources. Durango's growing technology and remote-worker economy has expanded broadband infrastructure beyond what the traditional resort-and-energy base required.

restrictions.name: Montrose County
restrictions.title: Montrose County — Montrose/western slope service hub/Uncompahgre valley
restrictions.description: Montrose serves as the service hub for the western slope's Uncompahgre Plateau region. The county's agricultural operations — particularly fruit orchards in the Uncompahgre Valley — are significant economic drivers. Montrose Regional Airport provides connectivity. The Black Canyon of the Gunnison National Park nearby drives tourism IT. Uncompahgre Valley Water Users Association and Tri-State Generation's transmission infrastructure serve the county. As Grand Junction grows and western Colorado's remote-worker population increases, Montrose has become a secondary market for broadband and technology services in the region.

restrictions.name: Logan County
restrictions.title: Logan County — Sterling/NE Colorado hub/I-76 fiber corridor/feedlots
restrictions.description: Sterling in Logan County is the largest city in northeast Colorado and the commercial hub of the 'Overland Trail' region along the South Platte River. Logan County's massive cattle feedlot operations — among the largest concentrations in Colorado — drive significant electrical infrastructure from Public Service Company of Colorado (Xcel). The I-76 corridor through Logan County carries fiber routes connecting Denver to the Nebraska panhandle. Colorado State University's extension presence and the agricultural commodity infrastructure of the South Platte corridor give Logan County more technical IT engagement than its remote rural character suggests.

restrictions.name: Coryell County
restrictions.title: Coryell County — Copperas Cove/Fort Cavazos (Hood)/Army's largest base
restrictions.description: Coryell County is home to Copperas Cove — the primary civilian community serving Fort Cavazos, formerly Fort Hood, the largest US Army installation in the world by area and one of the largest by active-duty population. Fort Cavazos is the home of III Corps, the 1st Cavalry Division, and major Army command and control infrastructure. The Army's computing, simulation, and C4ISR systems at Fort Cavazos represent an extraordinary federal IT concentration. Oncor and Copperas Cove municipal utility provide service. The civilian and military combined technology footprint of the Killeen-Fort Cavazos metro — including Bell County (already in DB) and Coryell County — is among the most significant in Texas outside major metros.

restrictions.name: Kaufman County
restrictions.title: Kaufman County — Terrell/DFW SE exurban growth corridor
restrictions.description: Kaufman County is one of the fastest-growing counties in Texas, driven by Dallas-Fort Worth's southeastern suburban expansion along the US-80 and I-20 corridors. Terrell is the county seat, and the county has attracted manufacturing and distribution operations from DFW's overflow growth. Oncor serves the county with transmission access from the DFW metro grid. As the Dallas data center market has become one of the largest in the world, Kaufman County's exurban position on the eastern edge of the DFW metro makes it a natural secondary data center and edge computing market for the region.

restrictions.name: Henderson County
restrictions.title: Henderson County — Athens/East Texas lakes/regional healthcare hub
restrictions.description: Athens in Henderson County is a regional hub in East Texas's lake district, serving the communities around Cedar Creek Reservoir, Lake Palestine, and other impoundments. Henderson County's position on US-175 — connecting Dallas to deep East Texas — makes it a significant fiber corridor waypoint. East Texas Medical Center in Athens serves as a regional hospital drawing patients from multiple counties. Oncor's transmission infrastructure through Henderson County connects DFW to the East Texas market. The county's lake district economy drives telecommunications infrastructure for remote workers and resort communities.

restrictions.name: Custer County
restrictions.title: Custer County — Miles City/eastern Montana commercial hub/livestock auction
restrictions.description: Miles City in Custer County is the commercial hub of eastern Montana and home to one of the largest livestock auctions in the US. The Eastern Montana livestock economy drives IT infrastructure for auction management, USDA reporting, and market data systems. Montana's Department of Transportation regional headquarters in Miles City adds state government IT. NorthWestern Energy and Montana-Dakota Utilities serve the area. Miles City's position at the I-94/US-12 junction makes it the largest city in a 200-mile radius of eastern Montana, creating regional retail, healthcare, and government IT concentration.

restrictions.name: Fergus County
restrictions.title: Fergus County — Lewistown/geographic center of Montana/ag and ranching hub
restrictions.description: Lewistown in Fergus County is located at the exact geographic center of Montana, making it the ultimate 'middle of nowhere' hub county. Central Montana Electric Power Cooperative serves the area with access to NorthWestern Energy's hydropower-backed grid. Lewistown serves as the commercial and medical center for a vast region of central Montana with no other significant population center within 100 miles. While the county's population (~11K) is small, its geographic centrality means that agricultural and ranching operations across a massive area route their IT through Lewistown's infrastructure.

restrictions.name: Adams County
restrictions.title: Adams County — Natchez/historic antebellum/Southwest MS regional hub
restrictions.description: Natchez in Adams County is one of the oldest cities in the US and the commercial hub of southwest Mississippi. The Natchez Trace Parkway headquarters and the region's antebellum tourism economy drive hospitality IT. Alcorn State University — Mississippi's first land-grant HBCU — is nearby. Adams County's position on the Mississippi River gives it barge terminal infrastructure and river-corridor fiber access. Entergy Mississippi serves the county. While Natchez's economy has diversified from its antebellum plantation origins, it remains southwest Mississippi's largest city and regional IT center.

restrictions.name: Grenada County
restrictions.title: Grenada County — Grenada/north MS regional hub/Grenada Lake/manufacturing
restrictions.description: Grenada in Grenada County is a north Mississippi regional hub at the I-55/US-51 junction. General Electric's appliance manufacturing plant was historically the county's largest employer. Grenada Lake provides water resources and hydroelectric coordination through the Army Corps. The I-55 corridor through Grenada County connects Memphis to Jackson, making it a significant fiber route waypoint in Mississippi. Entergy Mississippi and cooperative utilities serve the area. Grenada's position as the most significant city between Grenada and Batesville on I-55 makes it a fiber corridor anchor in north-central Mississippi.

restrictions.name: Dunklin County
restrictions.title: Dunklin County — Kennett/Missouri Bootheel poultry/cotton hub
restrictions.description: Kennett in Dunklin County is the commercial hub of the Missouri Bootheel's agricultural economy. Wayne Farms (poultry), cotton gin operations, and soybean processing create industrial electrical demand in this rural Missouri county. The Bootheel's flat delta geography and Delta blues cultural heritage tie it to the Mississippi Alluvial Plain's larger agricultural economy. Empire Electric (now Liberty Utilities) serves the county. Dunklin County's agricultural processing concentration gives it above-average industrial power infrastructure for a county of its population size.

restrictions.name: Scott County
restrictions.title: Scott County — Sikeston/Southeast Missouri hub/MO Bootheel commercial center
restrictions.description: Sikeston in Scott County is the commercial hub of southeast Missouri and the largest city in the Missouri Bootheel. Sikeston hosts a significant Lambert-St. Louis International Airport overflow cargo operation and a regional medical center. The Missouri Department of Transportation's district headquarters is here, as are agricultural commodity trading and processing operations. Ameren Missouri serves the county. Sikeston's position on I-55 and US-60/62 makes it the retail and commercial center for a large multi-county rural region with few alternatives.

restrictions.name: Pulaski County
restrictions.title: Pulaski County — Waynesville/Fort Leonard Wood/Army Engineer School
restrictions.description: Pulaski County is home to Fort Leonard Wood — the US Army's Maneuver Support Center of Excellence and the home of the Army Engineer School, Military Police School, and Chemical School. Fort Leonard Wood trains over 85,000 soldiers annually in engineering, MP, and CBRN disciplines, making it one of the Army's most active training installations. The base's extensive simulation, training management, and command IT systems represent a major federal computing concentration in the Missouri Ozarks. The county's training volume creates civilian tech workforce demand beyond what the rural setting suggests.

restrictions.name: Seward County
restrictions.title: Seward County — Seward/Concordia University/Lincoln NW exurb
restrictions.description: Seward in Seward County is located 30 miles west of Lincoln on I-80 — making it part of the functional Lincoln market for data center purposes. Concordia University Nebraska (Lutheran) provides an academic IT presence. Seward County's agricultural processing operations — particularly grain elevators and ethanol — drive NPPD substation capacity beyond residential demand. The I-80 fiber corridor running through Seward County connects Lincoln to Grand Island and beyond. Nebraska Public Power District's competitive rates make Seward County attractive for large load users priced out of the Lincoln metro.

restrictions.name: Saunders County
restrictions.title: Saunders County — Wahoo/Omaha NW exurban growth/Lincoln-Omaha corridor
restrictions.description: Saunders County sits between Omaha and Lincoln on US-77 — one of Nebraska's most significant fiber corridors connecting the two major markets. Wahoo is the county seat. As Omaha has grown into a significant Midwest data center market, Saunders County has become an increasingly attractive exurban location for facilities serving the Omaha metro at lower land costs.
