OPPD (Omaha Public Power District) extends service into portions of Saunders County. The agricultural county's position on the US-77 corridor makes it a natural intermediate point between Nebraska's two largest data center markets.

restrictions.name: Cass County
restrictions.title: Cass County — Plattsmouth/Omaha south suburb/Missouri River corridor
restrictions.description: Cass County is immediately south of Omaha and Douglas County, containing Plattsmouth — a Missouri River city historically important as a railroad crossing point. Plattsmouth Bridge carries fiber routes across the Missouri River on US-34. OPPD extends service into portions of Cass County as Omaha's southern suburban development expands. The county's river position gives it water resource access and historical infrastructure investment from the Burlington Northern rail era. As Omaha's data center market expands southward from its Douglas County core, Cass County's suburban adjacency makes it part of the broader metro market.

restrictions.name: Nez Perce County
restrictions.title: Nez Perce County — Lewiston/Clarkston bi-state/port/inland river terminus
restrictions.description: Lewiston in Nez Perce County forms a bi-state metropolitan area with Clarkston, Washington. Lewiston is the terminus of the Snake River navigation system — the farthest inland port on the US West Coast, accessible to oceangoing barges via the Columbia-Snake River system. This port position created significant logistics and commodity-trading IT infrastructure in Lewiston. Lewis-Clark State College and St. Joseph Regional Medical Center anchor the local economy. Idaho Power's hydro-backed rates are among the lowest in the US. Lewiston's grain, pulp/paper, and manufacturing base created industrial electrical infrastructure serving a regional economy larger than the city's population suggests.

restrictions.name: Blaine County
restrictions.title: Blaine County — Sun Valley/Hailey/Wood River tech community/affluent remote hub
restrictions.description: Blaine County hosts Sun Valley — one of America's premier ski and summer resorts — and Hailey, the largest city in the Wood River Valley. The county has attracted an unusually high concentration of technology executives, venture capitalists, and entrepreneurs as permanent or part-time residents, creating a technically sophisticated community with exceptional broadband requirements. Sun Valley's annual economics conference (the Allen & Company Sun Valley Conference) brings major technology industry leaders annually. Idaho Power's hydropower-backed rates and the valley's growing fiber infrastructure serve a tech-wealthy residential base with above-average per-capita IT spend.

restrictions.name: Grand Traverse County
restrictions.title: Grand Traverse County — Traverse City/northern Michigan hub/cherry capital/tech economy
restrictions.description: Traverse City in Grand Traverse County is northern Michigan's largest city and the economic hub of the upper Lower Peninsula. The area has developed a significant technology economy — StartUp TC and 20Fathoms tech incubator — alongside its cherry agriculture and tourism base. Northwestern Michigan College provides a technology workforce pipeline. Cherryland Electric Cooperative and Consumers Energy serve the county. Traverse City's growing tech sector, healthcare hub (Munson Medical Center), and year-round economy make Grand Traverse County the most important secondary data center market in Michigan outside the metropolitan Detroit and Grand Rapids areas.

restrictions.name: Jackson County
restrictions.title: Jackson County — Jackson/mid-Michigan automotive/Michigan prison complex
restrictions.description: Jackson in Jackson County is a mid-Michigan city with deep automotive supply chain roots — historically a center for auto parts, springs, and components manufacturing serving Detroit. The Michigan Department of Corrections' largest campus, including the former State Prison of Southern Michigan (once the world's largest walled prison), is in Jackson. The county sits on I-94 between Detroit and Chicago, a position on one of the highest-fiber-density corridors in the US. Consumers Energy serves the county from its headquarters in Jackson — making Jackson the home of Michigan's largest electric utility, with corresponding grid infrastructure.

restrictions.name: Maury County
restrictions.title: Maury County — Columbia/Spring Hill/GM Spring Hill Assembly/auto IT hub
restrictions.description: Maury County is home to the GM Spring Hill Assembly Plant — originally built as Saturn Corporation's dedicated facility and now one of GM's most advanced manufacturing complexes. The plant's Industry 4.0 digital manufacturing systems, supply chain management IT, and connected vehicle R&D infrastructure represent a major automotive technology concentration in central Tennessee. Columbia is the county seat and a fast-growing Nashville exurb. Maury County is among the fastest-growing counties in Tennessee, driven by both GM's expanding operations and Nashville's southward residential expansion. Tennessee Valley Authority serves the county with competitive industrial power rates.

restrictions.name: Wilson County
restrictions.title: Wilson County — Lebanon/Nashville NE fastest-growing suburb/Music Row overflow
restrictions.description: Wilson County is one of Tennessee's fastest-growing counties, driven by Nashville's northeastern suburban expansion along US-70N and the I-40 corridor. Lebanon is the county seat. The county's proximity to Nashville — home of a growing technology and entertainment industry — has attracted data center and technology campus development seeking Nashville connectivity at lower land costs. Tennessee Tech University is nearby in Cookeville. Nashville Electric Service's transmission infrastructure extends into Wilson County. The county's explosive population growth reflects Nashville's broader metro expansion making it one of the hottest real estate and business markets in the South.

restrictions.name: Putnam County
restrictions.title: Putnam County — Cookeville/Tennessee Tech/Upper Cumberland regional hub
restrictions.description: Cookeville in Putnam County is home to Tennessee Technological University — a STEM-focused public university with strong engineering and computer science programs. TTU provides a technical workforce pipeline that has attracted technology and manufacturing operations to the Upper Cumberland region. Cookeville serves as the commercial hub for a 10-county Upper Cumberland region with no other major city. The I-40 corridor through Putnam County is a primary east-west fiber route between Nashville and Knoxville. Tennessee Valley Authority provides competitive industrial power rates. Putnam County's combination of TTU's STEM output and regional hub status makes it one of Middle Tennessee's more significant secondary markets.

restrictions.name: Columbia County
restrictions.title: Columbia County — Evans/Augusta NW suburb/Cyber Center of Excellence adjacent
restrictions.description: Columbia County is the most affluent and fastest-growing suburb of Augusta, Georgia — and adjacent to Fort Eisenhower (formerly Fort Gordon), the US Army Cyber Center of Excellence and home of the Army Cyber Command. The Cyber Command's concentration of Army cybersecurity and signals intelligence operations drives extraordinary federal IT infrastructure adjacent to Columbia County. Augusta University Health System (Medical College of Georgia) extends its suburban footprint into Columbia County. Georgia Power's transmission infrastructure connects the county to the Augusta nuclear and coal power base. Columbia County's position in the Augusta metro's cyber cluster makes it one of Georgia's most significant federal-IT-adjacent markets.

restrictions.name: Burke County
restrictions.title: Burke County — Waynesboro/Vogtle Nuclear Plant/largest nuclear construction in US
restrictions.description: Burke County is home to Plant Vogtle — the first new nuclear reactor licensed and built in the United States in over three decades. Units 3 and 4 at Vogtle represent the most significant nuclear construction project in US history since the 1980s, with Georgia Power's $35+ billion investment creating extraordinary electrical generation and transmission infrastructure in rural Burke County. Vogtle's four operational reactors make the site one of the largest nuclear generating stations in the US. The electrical grid infrastructure built to carry Vogtle's output creates substantial Southern Company transmission capacity in a county otherwise characterized by agricultural and forestry land use.

restrictions.name: Floyd County
restrictions.title: Floyd County — Rome GA/Shorter University/Berry College/northwest GA hub
restrictions.description: Rome in Floyd County is northwest Georgia's largest city and the commercial hub for a multi-county region. The county hosts Shorter University, Berry College (one of the largest college campuses in the world by acreage), and Redmond Regional Medical Center. Rome's position at the junction of three rivers (Coosa, Etowah, Oostanaula) and US-27, US-411, and GA-20 makes it a regional fiber convergence point in the Appalachian foothills. Georgia Power serves the county. Floyd County's role as northwest Georgia's primary healthcare and retail market creates IT concentration beyond what the county's population alone would justify.

restrictions.name: Glynn County
restrictions.title: Glynn County — Brunswick/Golden Isles/port/SE Georgia coastal hub
restrictions.description: Brunswick in Glynn County is southeast Georgia's major port city and the gateway to the Golden Isles (St. Simons Island, Sea Island, Jekyll Island). The Port of Brunswick is a major vehicle import terminal — one of the largest automobile-processing ports in North America — with logistics IT requirements for vehicle tracking, customs, and distribution. College of Coastal Georgia serves the county. Brunswick's port position on the Atlantic coast gives it fiber connectivity from submarine cable landing stations in the region. Georgia Power serves the county. Glynn County's port infrastructure and coastal tourism economy create a distinctive mix of logistics IT and hospitality IT concentration.

restrictions.name: Pontotoc County
restrictions.title: Pontotoc County — Ada/East Central University/Chickasaw Nation hub/south-central OK
restrictions.description: Ada in Pontotoc County is the headquarters city of the Chickasaw Nation — the second-largest Indian nation in Oklahoma by tribal land area and a significant economic force in south-central Oklahoma. The Chickasaw Nation's tribal government operations, healthcare (Chickasaw Nation Medical Center), and gaming enterprise (multiple casinos in Ada) create concentrated IT demand. East Central University serves Ada's academic sector. Oklahoma Gas and Electric serves the county. Ada's unique role as both a Chickasaw Nation institutional center and a south-central Oklahoma regional hub gives Pontotoc County an IT profile significantly above its general population metrics.

restrictions.name: Ottawa County
restrictions.title: Ottawa County — Miami OK/Cherokee Nation/Quapaw Nation/Tar Creek legacy
restrictions.description: Ottawa County in northeast Oklahoma is a tribal territory hub — home to both Cherokee Nation operations and the Quapaw Nation tribal headquarters. Miami (pronounced My-AM-uh locally) is the county seat. Ottawa County is also the site of the Tar Creek Superfund Site — a massive lead and zinc mining pollution legacy that has driven significant EPA and Army Corps monitoring and remediation IT infrastructure in the county. Northeastern Oklahoma A&M College serves the academic sector. PSO serves the county. Ottawa County's combination of tribal IT infrastructure and federal environmental monitoring programs creates public-sector technology demand in a rural northeast Oklahoma county.

restrictions.name: Clark County
restrictions.title: Clark County — Winchester/Kentucky horse country/Bluegrass east exurb
restrictions.description: Clark County sits in the heart of Kentucky's Bluegrass horse country, adjacent to Fayette County (Lexington, already in DB) to the west. Winchester is the county seat. The county's position on US-60 and the Mountain Parkway connecting Lexington to eastern Kentucky makes it a fiber corridor waypoint. Clark County's horse farm economy and proximity to Lexington have attracted manufacturing operations (American Standard, Sargent & Greenleaf). Kentucky Utilities provides service. As Lexington's tech and healthcare economy expands, Clark County has become a natural exurban extension of the Lexington market, particularly along the US-60 corridor toward Winchester.

restrictions.name: North Slope Borough
restrictions.title: North Slope Borough — Prudhoe Bay/Trans-Alaska Pipeline origin/oil field SCADA
restrictions.description: North Slope Borough is the northernmost county-equivalent in the United States and the home of Prudhoe Bay — the largest oil field ever discovered in North America. The oil field's production monitoring, SCADA systems, and pipeline control infrastructure represent one of the most extensive industrial digital monitoring networks in the US. Alyeska Pipeline Service Company's control systems for the 800-mile Trans-Alaska Pipeline originate at Prudhoe Bay pump stations. BP, ConocoPhillips, and ExxonMobil operate extensive digital operations centers in the borough. North Slope Electric Service provides power to the borough's scattered communities and oil field facilities.

restrictions.name: Valdez-Cordova Census Area
restrictions.title: Valdez-Cordova — Trans-Alaska Pipeline terminus/Alyeska marine terminal
restrictions.description: Valdez is the southern terminus of the Trans-Alaska Pipeline System — where Alaska North Slope crude arrives after traveling 800 miles and is loaded onto tankers for West Coast and Asian markets. Alyeska Pipeline's Valdez Marine Terminal is one of the largest crude oil export facilities in the US. The control systems, metering infrastructure, and operational IT for managing the terminal and coordinating tanker operations represent a concentrated industrial computing environment. The Prince William Sound area also supports Coast Guard marine operations. Matanuska Electric Association and local generation serve the Valdez area.

restrictions.name: Kodiak Island Borough
restrictions.title: Kodiak Island Borough — Coast Guard Air Station Kodiak/largest USCG air station
restrictions.description: Kodiak Island Borough hosts US Coast Guard Air Station Kodiak — the largest Coast Guard air station in the US and a primary hub for Alaskan rescue, fisheries law enforcement, and maritime domain awareness operations. The Coast Guard's C4ISR systems, aerial surveillance data processing, and rescue coordination IT represent a major federal computing presence in Kodiak. The city of Kodiak is also the hub of Alaska's commercial fishing industry — home to the largest fishing fleet on the US West Coast. Kodiak Electric Association provides power from hydro and wind sources.

restrictions.name: Ketchikan Gateway Borough
restrictions.title: Ketchikan Gateway Borough — SE Alaska hub/pulp mill legacy/gateway to Alaska
restrictions.description: Ketchikan is the first major Alaskan community encountered by ships traveling the Inside Passage — making it a significant Southeast Alaska hub for cruise tourism, commercial fishing, and regional government. The Ketchikan Pulp Mill (closed 1997) left behind substantial industrial electrical infrastructure on the Ketchikan Public Utilities system. University of Alaska Southeast's Ketchikan campus provides education infrastructure. Ketchikan's strategic position as a Southeast Alaska hub city for healthcare, government, and commerce creates IT concentration in a remote coastal environment served by fiber cables running through the Inside Passage corridor.

restrictions.name: Custer County
restrictions.title: Custer County — Custer/Crazy Horse Memorial/Black Hills National Forest hub
restrictions.description: Custer County in the Black Hills hosts both the Custer State Park — South Dakota's largest state park — and the ongoing Crazy Horse Memorial carving project, the world's largest mountain carving under construction. The Black Hills National Forest headquarters in Custer generates significant federal IT infrastructure. The county's position in the Black Hills provides connectivity to the Lawrence County (Lead/SURF, already in DB) data and computing infrastructure. The Tourism and recreation economy, plus Forest Service administration, drive broadband and IT investment in Custer County beyond what the permanent population alone requires.

restrictions.name: Fall River County
restrictions.title: Fall River County — Hot Springs/VA Black Hills Health Care/SW South Dakota hub
restrictions.description: Hot Springs in Fall River County is home to the VA Black Hills Health Care System — one of the VA's rural tertiary care centers serving veterans across a vast region of South Dakota, Wyoming, and Nebraska. The mammoth fossil discovery at Hot Springs (Mammoth Site) drives paleontological research computing. Fall River County Electric and Black Hills Electric Cooperative serve the area. Hot Springs' VA hospital creates federal IT concentration in a rural county, and the county's position on US-385 connecting to the Black Hills corridor gives it infrastructure connectivity to the Lawrence-Custer cluster.

restrictions.name: Butte County
restrictions.title: Butte County — Belle Fourche/geographic center of nation (with AK)/NW SD energy hub
restrictions.description: Belle Fourche in Butte County is the center of the US geographic center of the nation (when Alaska is included). The county sits at the northern edge of the Black Hills and connects the Hills to the Bakken oil region to the north. Belle Fourche is a historic cattle shipping hub and livestock marketing center. Montana-Dakota Utilities and Black Hills Electric serve the area. The county's position on US-212 and US-85 connecting the Black Hills to Wyoming gives it infrastructure corridor access. Belle Fourche's livestock auction and agricultural commodity infrastructure create above-average industrial IT for its size.

restrictions.name: Jasper County
restrictions.title: Jasper County — Jasper/East Texas timber/Toledo Bend Reservoir recreation
restrictions.description: Jasper in Jasper County is a hub for the East Texas timber industry and the Toledo Bend Reservoir recreation economy — Toledo Bend is one of the largest lakes in Texas. Entergy Texas serves the county. Jasper County's timber operations (Champion Paper, various forestry companies) created industrial electrical infrastructure along the Neches and Angelina river corridors. The county's US-96 and US-190 corridors connect it to the Beaumont-Port Arthur petrochemical market to the south and Lufkin to the northwest. East Texas timber management and recreation economies drive IT demand for a rural county.

restrictions.name: Gaines County
restrictions.title: Gaines County — Seminole/Permian Basin edge/cotton and oil/wind development
restrictions.description: Gaines County sits at the southwestern edge of the Texas Permian Basin in the Llano Estacado flatlands. Seminole is the county seat. The county has both significant oil production and major cotton farming operations, with the oil industry driving SCADA and production monitoring IT. Additionally, Gaines County has seen major wind energy development — multiple wind farms have been built in the county's flatlands, creating electrical grid infrastructure through Oncor and ERCOT. The combination of Permian oil, cotton agriculture, and wind energy makes Gaines County an unusually multi-sector industrial county for its size.

restrictions.name: Deaf Smith County
restrictions.title: Deaf Smith County — Hereford/feedlot capital/Texas Panhandle agriculture/nuclear waste
restrictions.description: Hereford in Deaf Smith County is the 'beef cattle capital of the world' — the county contains some of the largest cattle feedlot operations in Texas, with hundreds of thousands of cattle on feed at any given time. The industrial electrical load from feedlot operations, feed mills, and meat packing plants drives substantial Xcel Energy (Southwestern Public Service) infrastructure in the county. Deaf Smith County has also been proposed as a site for consolidated interim storage of nuclear waste, which if approved would bring additional federal infrastructure. The county's agricultural and energy potential is among the highest in the Texas Panhandle.

restrictions.name: Liberty County
restrictions.title: Liberty County — Liberty/Houston NE petrochemical corridor/I-10 fiber
restrictions.description: Liberty County sits northeast of Houston on the I-10 corridor, adjacent to the Houston Ship Channel's eastern petrochemical complex. Chevron Phillips Chemical, ExxonMobil, and other major petrochemical operators' facilities downstream of Liberty County create a dense industrial IT and process control environment in the Houston-Beaumont corridor. The county's position on I-10 — one of the highest-fiber-density interstate corridors in the US — provides outstanding connectivity. Entergy Texas and CenterPoint Energy serve the area. As Houston's industrial market expands northeast, Liberty County's industrial corridor position has gained importance.

restrictions.name: Beltrami County
restrictions.title: Beltrami County — Bemidji/BSU/source of Mississippi River/north MN hub
restrictions.description: Bemidji in Beltrami County is the largest city in northern Minnesota between the Twin Cities and Fargo, serving as the commercial hub for a vast forested region. Bemidji State University provides a technology and education foundation. The Mississippi River begins at Lake Itasca in nearby Clearwater County, making Bemidji the northernmost major city on the Mississippi watershed. GreatRiver Energy and Minnesota Power serve the county. The county's timber economy, Leech Lake Tribal Band gaming operations, and regional healthcare hub (Sanford Bemidji) create IT demand for a remote northwoods city.

restrictions.name: Morrison County
restrictions.title: Morrison County — Little Falls/Camp Ripley/MN National Guard's largest installation
restrictions.description: Camp Ripley in Morrison County is the largest National Guard installation in the United States and the primary training center for the Minnesota Army National Guard. The installation's training management systems, range monitoring, and command IT infrastructure represent a substantial federal computing footprint in central Minnesota. Charles A. Lindbergh State Park (birthplace of the aviator) is nearby. Stearns Electric Association serves the county. Camp Ripley's joint training mission — hosting multiple state Guard units and international partner training — creates digital training management requirements well above those of comparable facilities.

restrictions.name: Otter Tail County
restrictions.title: Otter Tail County — Fergus Falls/west-central MN hub/lakes tourism/Otter Tail Power
restrictions.description: Fergus Falls in Otter Tail County is the largest city in west-central Minnesota and home to Otter Tail Corporation — a diversified utilities company whose Otter Tail Power subsidiary serves a large rural service territory in Minnesota, North Dakota, and South Dakota. Otter Tail's utility headquarters in Fergus Falls gives the county above-average electrical grid management and IT infrastructure for its size. Lake Region Electric Cooperative and Otter Tail Power serve the county. The county's 1,000+ lakes drive a major tourism economy and make Otter Tail County the most populous rural county in west-central Minnesota.

restrictions.name: Alcorn County
restrictions.title: Alcorn County — Corinth/northeast MS hub/historic Civil War site/Tennessee Valley
restrictions.description: Corinth in Alcorn County is a historic northeast Mississippi city — site of the Civil War Battle of Corinth and a strategic railroad junction. Today Corinth is a regional manufacturing and distribution hub, with automotive and industrial suppliers attracted by TVA power and Mississippi's business environment. Alcorn County Electric Power Association provides service through TVA's grid. The US-45 corridor through Alcorn County connects Memphis to northeast Mississippi's manufacturing base. Corinth's position as a key TVA service county in Mississippi's 'golden crescent' manufacturing region gives it competitive industrial power rates and grid infrastructure.

restrictions.name: Lafayette County
restrictions.title: Lafayette County — Oxford/University of Mississippi/Ole Miss research hub
restrictions.description: Oxford in Lafayette County is home to the University of Mississippi — Ole Miss — one of the South's flagship public universities. Ole Miss's research programs, including the National Center for Natural Products Research (the nation's largest university laboratory for natural products research) and a growing computer science department, drive substantial research computing needs. Yokohama Tire's major North American manufacturing plant is in the county. Mississippi Power and Entergy Mississippi serve the area. Oxford's status as a SEC college town with growing research ambitions and manufacturing diversity makes Lafayette County one of Mississippi's more dynamic secondary counties.

restrictions.name: Pearl River County
restrictions.title: Pearl River County — Poplarville/south MS hub/Pearl River Valley corridor
restrictions.description: Pearl River County in south Mississippi connects the Hattiesburg market (Forrest County, already in DB) to the Gulf Coast market (Harrison County, already in DB) along the US-11/I-59 corridor. Poplarville is the county seat. Pearl River Community College serves as the county's education anchor. The Pearl River water authority and its reservoir system provide regional water management IT. Mississippi Power serves the county. Pearl River County's position between two already-documented markets on the primary north-south interstate corridor through south Mississippi makes it a fiber route waypoint county.

restrictions.name: Coahoma County
restrictions.title: Coahoma County — Clarksdale/Mississippi Delta blues capital/Coahoma Community College
restrictions.description: Clarksdale in Coahoma County is the birthplace of the Delta blues — Robert Johnson, Muddy Waters, Sam Cooke, and Ike Turner all have connections here. The Delta Blues Museum and the historic crossroads at Routes 61 and 49 make Clarksdale a significant cultural and tourism IT hub. Coahoma Community College serves the county's education needs. The county's position in the Mississippi Delta gives it access to Mississippi's agricultural processing and river transportation IT infrastructure. Entergy Mississippi serves the county. While primarily rural, Clarksdale's tourism IT, community college, and Delta-wide healthcare center operations create a notable technology footprint.

restrictions.name: Etowah County
restrictions.title: Etowah County — Gadsden/NE Alabama industrial hub/Goodyear/Coosa River
restrictions.description: Gadsden in Etowah County is northeast Alabama's largest city and a historic industrial hub on the Coosa River. Goodyear Tire & Rubber, International Paper, and automotive supplier operations have historically made Gadsden one of Alabama's most significant industrial cities outside Birmingham. Alabama Power's hydroelectric stations on the Coosa River provide competitive industrial rates. Gadsden State Community College provides a technical workforce pipeline. Gadsden is the commercial hub for northeast Alabama, serving as the major healthcare and retail center between Birmingham and Chattanooga. The city's manufacturing legacy created industrial electrical infrastructure still supporting significant load.

restrictions.name: Dale County
restrictions.title: Dale County — Ozark/Fort Novosel flight corridor/SE Alabama regional hub
restrictions.description: Dale County surrounds Coffee County's Fort Novosel (formerly Fort Rucker) aviation training area, with Ozark serving as the county seat and a primary civilian community for Army aviation families. The county's position adjacent to the Army Aviation Center creates a substantial military-adjacent civilian economy — aviation parts suppliers, simulation contractors, and defense services companies serve Fort Novosel from Ozark. Alabama Power and Southern Company serve the area. Dale County's proximity to the Fort Novosel federal IT ecosystem makes it a natural data center and technology services market for the aviation training cluster.

restrictions.name: Boone County
restrictions.title: Boone County — Harrison/NW Arkansas gateway/Ozarks hub
restrictions.description: Harrison in Boone County is the northern gateway to the Arkansas Ozarks and the commercial hub for northern Arkansas between the booming Bentonville/Fayetteville metro to the west and the Springfield MO market to the north. The Boone County area serves as a regional healthcare center (North Arkansas Regional Medical Center) and tourism hub for Buffalo National River — the first national river in the US. US-65 connects Harrison to Springfield MO, creating a corridor for freight and IT infrastructure. Boone County's proximity to the Walmart/JB Hunt/Tyson technology ecosystem in NW Arkansas creates spillover demand for rural broadband and tech services.

restrictions.name: Carroll County
restrictions.title: Carroll County — Eureka Springs/Berryville/NW Arkansas Ozarks
restrictions.description: Carroll County in northwestern Arkansas contains Eureka Springs — a historic Victorian resort town built around natural springs, now a major Ozarks tourism destination with hundreds of thousands of visitors annually. The county borders Missouri and is positioned at the convergence of the NW Arkansas metro's growth corridor with the White River resort lake economy (Table Rock, Beaver Lake, Bull Shoals). Berryville, the county seat, serves the agricultural and timber economy of the upper White River watershed. Eureka Springs' arts and tourism IT infrastructure, combined with the county's proximity to the Bentonville tech ecosystem, drives above-average rural broadband investment.

restrictions.name: Crawford County
restrictions.title: Crawford County — Van Buren/I-40 corridor/Fort Smith metro east
restrictions.description: Crawford County and its city Van Buren form the eastern anchor of the Fort Smith metro area (Sebastian County, already in DB). Van Buren sits directly on I-40 — one of the most important east-west freight corridors in the US — at the Arkansas River crossing. The county's position on the I-40 fiber backbone connecting Little Rock and Fort Smith to Oklahoma City makes it a natural fiber waypoint. Arkansas River Valley industries include poultry processing, manufacturing, and distribution. The historic Rudy area in Crawford County has been targeted for industrial development leveraging I-40 access and the Fort Smith metro's labor pool.

restrictions.name: Bell County
restrictions.title: Bell County — Middlesboro/Cumberland Gap/SE Kentucky hub
restrictions.description: Bell County in southeastern Kentucky contains Middlesboro — the only US city built inside a meteor impact crater — and the Cumberland Gap National Historical Park, where Virginia, Tennessee, and Kentucky meet. The Gap was the primary passage for westward migration through the Appalachians. Bell County is served by Kentucky Power (AEP) and sits in a region of deep coal heritage with the infrastructure legacy of mountain electricity generation. Middlesboro's TVA-adjacent grid access (through Tennessee connections) and its position as the commercial hub for a tri-state area create IT concentration beyond its permanent population metrics.

restrictions.name: Bourbon County
restrictions.title: Bourbon County — Paris/Kentucky bourbon heartland/thoroughbred country
restrictions.description: Bourbon County is the original home of bourbon whiskey — the spirit takes its name from this county. Paris, the county seat, sits in the heart of the Kentucky Bluegrass region, surrounded by historic distilleries and thoroughbred horse farms. Buffalo Trace Distillery (the nation's oldest continuously operating distillery) and numerous craft distilleries in the region drive significant food and beverage IT, inventory management, and controlled process systems. The Kentucky bourbon boom has made the Bluegrass corridor one of the most active beverage-tech markets in the mid-South. Kentucky Power and Kentucky Utilities serve the county.

restrictions.name: Boyle County
restrictions.title: Boyle County — Danville/Centre College/Kentucky's technology corridor
restrictions.description: Boyle County and Danville, Kentucky are home to Centre College — one of the most selective small liberal arts colleges in the US, and a host site for the 2000 Vice Presidential debate. Danville has become one of Kentucky's more notable economic development successes in the interior — attracting Toyota (adjacent in Garrard County), Ephraim McDowell Health, and distribution operations. The county's position on US-150 connecting Lexington to the Appalachian coalfields gives it corridor fiber access. Danville's historic role as a legal and government hub (Kentucky's first court, first constitution, first college) continues as a regional administrative center. Kentucky Utilities (LG&E/KU Energy) serves the county.

restrictions.name: Camden County
restrictions.title: Camden County — Lake of the Ozarks/Camdenton/Missouri's premier lake resort economy
restrictions.description: Camden County contains the primary developed shoreline of Lake of the Ozarks — one of the largest reservoirs in the US by shoreline length (~1,150 miles) and Missouri's premier resort destination. Camdenton is the county seat and lake services hub. The lake economy supports a year-round tourism and second-home technology demand far beyond what permanent population alone suggests. Ameren Missouri's Lake of the Ozarks facilities (Bagnell Dam created the lake in 1931) provide hydroelectric generation that shaped the entire central Missouri electricity market. The lake's communications infrastructure — to support marina operations, vacation rentals, and second-home connectivity — has driven above-average rural broadband investment in Camden County.

restrictions.name: Adair County
restrictions.title: Adair County — Kirksville/Truman State University/osteopathic medicine origin
restrictions.description: Kirksville in Adair County is home to Truman State University — Missouri's only highly selective public liberal arts university — and A.T. Still University, which founded the first school of osteopathic medicine in the world in 1892. Kirksville Regional Medical Center and ATSU's medical and health sciences programs create a significant research and healthcare IT footprint for a small northern Missouri city. Truman State's technology infrastructure, including a high-performance computing environment for its STEM programs, anchors IT in a rural context. Ameren Missouri and local electric cooperatives serve the county.

restrictions.name: Audrain County
restrictions.title: Audrain County — Mexico MO/American Saddlebred horse capital/US-54 corridor
restrictions.description: Mexico in Audrain County is known as the 'Saddle Horse Capital of the World' — the American Saddlebred horse industry has deep roots here, with major breeding farms and the Missouri Fox Trotting Horse Breed Association driving agricultural technology demand. The county sits on US-54 — the primary highway connecting Kansas City to the I-70 corridor through central Missouri. Audrain County's position as a midpoint in the US-54 corridor makes it a natural waypoint for fiber infrastructure. Ameren Missouri's transmission lines through the county connect the Columbia (Boone County) market to the Mexico/Hannibal corridor. Aluminium rolling operations (Novelis, historically) contributed to Mexico's industrial electrical heritage.

restrictions.name: Clay County
restrictions.title: Clay County — Clay Center/north-central Kansas/Smoky Hill Trail corridor
restrictions.description: Clay Center in Clay County sits in the heart of north-central Kansas on the Republican River. The Smoky Hill Trail — used by settlers heading to Colorado gold fields — passed through the area. Clay County's agricultural economy centers on wheat, corn, and cattle, with major grain elevator infrastructure and SCADA monitoring for irrigation systems. Evergy (formerly Westar Energy/Great Plains Energy) serves the county. Clay County's position on US-24 and US-81 connecting Salina to Nebraska gives it highway infrastructure corridor fiber access. The county's Milford Lake reservoir (Kansas's largest) creates recreation and water management IT.

restrictions.name: Cherokee County
restrictions.title: Cherokee County — Columbus KS/Tri-State Mining District/Historic Route 66
restrictions.description: Cherokee County in the extreme southeastern corner of Kansas was part of the historic Tri-State Mining District — one of the world's most prolific sources of lead and zinc in the early 20th century, shared with Jasper County MO and Ottawa County OK. Historic Route 66 runs through the county, and the area has become a heritage tourism destination. The region's industrial electrical grid, originally built to power mining operations, created infrastructure density unusual for its current population. The EPA Superfund cleanup operations in the tri-state mining area continue to require environmental monitoring and SCADA systems. SEK Electric Cooperative serves the county.

restrictions.name: Jewell County
restrictions.title: Jewell County — Mankato/north-central Kansas/geographic and agricultural corridor
restrictions.description: Jewell County in north-central Kansas borders Nebraska and sits along the Republican River valley. Mankato is the county seat. The county exemplifies the high-plains agricultural economy: wheat, milo, and cattle operations with substantial center-pivot irrigation requiring SCADA and remote monitoring systems. North Central Electric Cooperative serves the county. Jewell County sits on US-36 — the primary east-west highway through north-central Kansas that parallels the Nebraska border, providing fiber corridor access for rural agricultural technology. The county's wind energy potential has attracted development interest, adding electrical grid infrastructure to the county's renewable energy profile.

restrictions.name: Allamakee County
restrictions.title: Allamakee County — Waukon/NE Iowa driftless bluffs/Mississippi River corner
restrictions.description: Allamakee County occupies the extreme northeastern corner of Iowa, where the Mississippi River forms the boundary with Wisconsin and Minnesota. Waukon is the county seat. The county is part of the Driftless Area — unglaciated terrain of dramatic bluffs and valleys that makes the area uniquely picturesque in the otherwise flat Midwest. The county's position on the Iowa-Wisconsin border, with the Mississippi River corridor's fiber infrastructure passing through, gives it connectivity assets beyond its size. Iowa's rural broadband expansion has prioritized driftless counties. Allamakee Electric Cooperative and Alliant Energy serve the area.

restrictions.name: Appanoose County
restrictions.title: Appanoose County — Centerville/SE Iowa coal heritage/Rathbun Lake energy hub
restrictions.description: Appanoose County in southeastern Iowa contains Rathbun Lake — one of Iowa's largest lakes, built by the Army Corps of Engineers and now managed by the Iowa DNR. Rathbun Electric Cooperative (serving a multi-county area from the Rathbun reservoir) operates one of Iowa's most innovative rural electric cooperatives. Centerville is the county seat and retains its historic character as a coal country hub — the county's deep coal history created a dense electrical grid infrastructure for the region. Iowa's rural renewable energy expansion has targeted the Appanoose area for wind and solar development, adding new grid investment. The county's data center-friendly power costs reflect the Rathbun Co-op's aggressive rural electrification model.

restrictions.name: Cooke County
restrictions.title: Cooke County — Gainesville/North Texas Oklahoma border/I-35 corridor
restrictions.description: Cooke County and Gainesville sit at the I-35 corridor crossing point between Texas and Oklahoma — one of the highest-traffic north-south freight routes in the US. The county's position on the Red River border makes it a natural hub for cross-border commerce and logistics. Lake Texoma (the largest reservoir on the Texas-Oklahoma border) creates water management and recreation IT demand in the area. Gainesville is served by Oncor and CoSer (Cooke-Denton Electric Cooperative). North Texas's manufacturing base — including proximity to the Denton County tech corridor and Gainesville's own industrial park — creates supply chain IT demand. The I-35W NTTA toll infrastructure creates additional monitoring and data management requirements.

restrictions.name: Robertson County
restrictions.title: Robertson County — Franklin/Brazos Valley/Bryan-College Station corridor
restrictions.description: Robertson County sits in the Brazos River valley between Waco and the Bryan-College Station market (Brazos County, home of Texas A&M, likely already in DB). Franklin, the county seat, is a market town serving the agricultural zone between these two major markets. The county's position on the US-79 corridor connecting Bryan-College Station to the DFW market gives it fiber corridor access. Luminant (Vistra Energy) has significant coal generation and natural gas assets in the Brazos Valley region. Robertson County's agricultural economy — cotton, cattle, sorghum — drives precision agriculture monitoring and irrigation SCADA demand. The proximity to Texas A&M's research and extension network creates agricultural IT spillover into Robertson County.

restrictions.name: Glacier County
restrictions.title: Glacier County — Cut Bank/Blackfeet Nation/Glacier National Park gateway
restrictions.description: Glacier County in northwestern Montana is the home of the Blackfeet Nation — one of Montana's largest Native American tribes — and sits on the eastern doorstep of Glacier National Park. Cut Bank is the county seat and a regional oil and natural gas hub for north-central Montana's Sweetgrass Arch petroleum geology. The county hosts the Glacier International Airport and is served by the Blackfeet telecommunications cooperative, which has been an innovator in tribal broadband development using federal universal service funding. The USDA's tribal broadband programs and FCC's E-Rate program have made Glacier County a beneficiary of significant connectivity investment. Montana-Dakota Utilities and local generation serve the area.

restrictions.name: Big Horn County
restrictions.title: Big Horn County — Hardin/Little Bighorn Battlefield/Crow Nation energy hub
restrictions.description: Big Horn County is the homeland of the Crow Nation — one of Montana's largest tribes — and contains Little Bighorn Battlefield National Monument, site of the 1876 battle known as Custer's Last Stand. Hardin is the county seat. The county is a significant energy producer: the Crow Nation's coal reserves on the Crow Indian Reservation are among the largest single tribal coal holdings in the US. The Bighorn River creates agricultural and hydropower potential. Crow Tribal Utility Authority and Big Horn County Electric Cooperative serve the area. The Crow Nation's energy sovereignty initiatives — including wind and solar development on reservation lands — create new grid and IT infrastructure investment in Big Horn County beyond what legacy coal operations provided.

restrictions.name: Rolette County
restrictions.title: Rolette County — Rolla/Turtle Mountain Band of Chippewa/Canada border
restrictions.description: Rolette County in north-central North Dakota is the homeland of the Turtle Mountain Band of Chippewa — one of North Dakota's largest tribal communities centered in and around Belcourt. Rolla serves as the county seat and a regional commercial hub near the Canadian border. The Turtle Mountain Community College has been a participant in federal tribal broadband and digital equity initiatives. The county's position near the Bottineau/Rolla border crossing with Canada creates cross-border logistics IT. MDU Resources and North Central Electric Cooperative serve the area. Federal tribal technology programs have made Rolette County a focus of rural broadband investment disproportionate to its size.

restrictions.name: Fayette County
restrictions.title: Fayette County — Fayetteville/New River Gorge National Park/adventure tourism hub
restrictions.description: Fayette County is home to New River Gorge National Park — one of the newest national parks in the US (upgraded from national river status in 2020), containing the deepest and longest river gorge in the Appalachians. The New River Gorge Bridge was the world's longest steel span arch bridge at its completion and hosts Bridge Day, the largest single-day festival in West Virginia attracting 80,000+ visitors. The county's adventure tourism economy (whitewater rafting, rock climbing, mountain biking) drives significant hospitality and recreation IT. West Virginia's outdoor tourism growth has prioritized Fayette County for rural broadband. Appalachian Power (AEP) serves the county, with legacy coal mining infrastructure creating existing grid capacity in this deeply rural mountain environment.

restrictions.name: Polk County
restrictions.title: Polk County — Livingston/East Texas piney woods/Lake Livingston
restrictions.description: Polk County in the East Texas piney woods contains Lake Livingston — one of the largest lakes in Texas by area, built on the Trinity River and serving as the primary water supply for the Houston metropolitan area. The dam and water management infrastructure create significant operational IT and SCADA systems. Livingston, the county seat, is the commercial hub for a large rural East Texas region. Entergy Texas serves the county. Alabama-Coushatta Indian Reservation — one of the few federally recognized tribes in Texas — is in Polk County, creating tribal broadband and government IT demand. The county's timber and wood products industry creates industrial IT beyond what its population alone suggests.

restrictions.name: Anderson County
restrictions.title: Anderson County — Palestine/East Texas/Dogwood Trail/TXU Energy legacy
restrictions.description: Palestine in Anderson County is the eastern Texas hub between the Tyler and Lufkin markets, known for the Dogwood Trails Festival and its historic downtown. The county sits on the US-79 and US-287 corridors connecting east Texas to the Dallas-Fort Worth market. Anderson County's oil and gas production history created industrial electrical infrastructure through TXU Energy (Luminant/Vistra). The Texas State Railroad — a heritage railroad connecting Palestine to Rusk — creates tourism IT. Palestine is the county seat for a significant East Texas agricultural and natural resource economy, with lumber, cattle, and oil all present in the county.

restrictions.name: Randall County
restrictions.title: Randall County — Canyon/Amarillo adjacent/West Texas A&M/Palo Duro Canyon
restrictions.description: Randall County contains Canyon, TX — the home of West Texas A&M University and adjacent to the Amarillo metropolitan area (Potter County, already in DB). Palo Duro Canyon State Park — the 'Grand Canyon of Texas' — is in Randall County, creating a major tourism IT hub. West Texas A&M's engineering and technology programs serve the Panhandle's agricultural and energy sectors. Xcel Energy (Southwestern Public Service) serves the county. Randall County's position as the residential suburban counterpart to commercial/industrial Potter County (Amarillo) means it participates directly in the Amarillo metro's data center and tech infrastructure, including the expanding Texas Panhandle renewable energy market.

restrictions.name: Knox County
restrictions.title: Knox County — Galesburg/BNSF Santa Fe line hub/Abraham Lincoln debate site
restrictions.description: Galesburg in Knox County is a historic Illinois railroad city — the intersection of BNSF's main line (former Santa Fe) and former Chicago, Burlington & Quincy routes creates one of the most significant rail junctions in central Illinois. The fifth Lincoln-Douglas debate was held in Galesburg (1858). Knox College, one of Illinois's oldest liberal arts colleges, provides education infrastructure. The county's position as a BNSF intermodal hub generates freight management and logistics IT. Ameren Illinois and local utilities serve the county. Galesburg's industrial heritage (Maytag/Whirlpool factory, now closed) left behind substantial commercial electrical infrastructure that supports modern business development.

restrictions.name: Williamson County
restrictions.title: Williamson County — Marion/SE Illinois hub/I-57 corridor/Herrin
restrictions.description: Williamson County in southeastern Illinois contains Marion — one of the largest cities in Illinois south of Springfield — at the junction of I-57 and I-24, two of the most important transportation arteries in southern Illinois. The county is served by Ameren Illinois's southern transmission network. Marion's position at the I-57/I-24 junction, connecting Chicago to Memphis/Nashville and St. Louis to the south, makes it a natural distribution and logistics hub. Southern Illinois University Carbondale is adjacent in Jackson County. The county's coal heritage created industrial grid infrastructure, and the John A. Logan College provides workforce development. Marion has attracted national retailers, medical centers, and logistics operations as a regional hub.

restrictions.name: Jefferson County
restrictions.title: Jefferson County — Mount Vernon/I-57 I-64 junction/SE Illinois crossroads
restrictions.description: Jefferson County and Mount Vernon sit at the precise junction of I-57 (Chicago-Memphis) and I-64 (St. Louis-Louisville), making it one of the most strategic highway crossroads in southern Illinois. Mount Vernon is the county seat and a regional hub for the Shawnee Hills region. The I-57/I-64 junction creates a natural fiber infrastructure waypoint and attracts distribution operations from both Chicago-facing and St. Louis-facing supply chains. Ameren Illinois serves the county. Mount Vernon's regional hospital and court infrastructure make it a government and healthcare center for a wide rural area. The county's position midway between St. Louis and Evansville/Nashville adds to its corridor significance.

restrictions.name: Saline County
restrictions.title: Saline County — Wilber/Czech capital of USA/SE Nebraska agriculture
restrictions.description: Saline County in southeastern Nebraska contains Wilber — the self-proclaimed Czech Capital of the USA, recognized by the US Congress. The county's Czech heritage tourism creates hospitality IT. Wilber is served by the Nebraska Public Power District. Saline County's agricultural economy — corn, soybeans, and wheat — drives precision agriculture and irrigation SCADA demand. The county sits on US-77 and US-6, giving it road corridor fiber access between Lincoln and Beatrice. Saline County's position in the Lincoln agricultural exurb zone has made it a recipient of Nebraska's rural broadband expansion. The county's food processing operations (Crete — the county's largest city — has industrial facilities) create industrial electrical demand.

restrictions.name: Thayer County
restrictions.title: Thayer County — Hebron/south-central Nebraska/Republican River Valley
restrictions.description: Thayer County in south-central Nebraska along the Republican River is a high-plains agricultural county anchored by Hebron, the county seat. The county's position along US-81 — one of the primary north-south highways through the central Great Plains connecting Kansas to North Dakota — gives it corridor fiber access. Nebraska Public Power District serves the county. Thayer County's Republican River water rights are part of the multi-state Republican River Compact among Nebraska, Kansas, and Colorado, creating water management and monitoring IT requirements. The county's center-pivot irrigation systems create above-average SCADA and remote monitoring demand for precision agriculture.

restrictions.name: Tama County
restrictions.title: Tama County — Toledo/Tama/Meskwaki Nation/Amana Colonies corridor
restrictions.description: Tama County in central Iowa is home to the Meskwaki Settlement — the largest Native American settlement in Iowa and one of the few where a tribe purchased its own land rather than receiving a reservation. The Meskwaki Nation's tribal government, casino operations (Meskwaki Bingo Casino Hotel), and tribal broadband programs create federal IT investment in the county. Tama County sits on US-30 — the former Lincoln Highway — connecting Ames to Cedar Rapids. MidAmerican Energy serves the county. The Meskwaki's sovereignty over their Settlement creates a unique data governance and digital equity framework that has attracted federal broadband and technology grants to Tama County.

restrictions.name: Jasper County
restrictions.title: Jasper County — Newton/Iowa's wind energy capital/Maytag heritage
restrictions.description: Newton in Jasper County was the world headquarters of Maytag Corporation until its 2006 acquisition by Whirlpool — a loss that devastated the local economy but prompted a remarkable pivot. Newton is now considered 'Iowa's Wind Energy Capital' — TPI Composites, Trinity Structural Towers, and other wind energy component manufacturers established major production facilities in the former Maytag industrial complex. Iowa's wind energy supply chain concentrated in Newton/Jasper County creates advanced manufacturing IT and industrial electrical load. MidAmerican Energy serves the county and operates extensive wind generation in the region. Jasper County is a national model for Rust Belt community reinvention through renewable energy manufacturing.

restrictions.name: Hopkins County
restrictions.title: Hopkins County — Madisonville/western Kentucky coal hub/TVA grid intersection
restrictions.description: Hopkins County and Madisonville are at the heart of western Kentucky's coal country — historically one of the most productive coal-producing regions in the US. The TVA grid serves western Kentucky with transmission lines built to deliver coal-fired generation from the Paradise and other Kentucky plants. Madisonville is the commercial hub for the Pennyrile region of western Kentucky, with regional healthcare (Regional Medical Center of Hopkins County), government, and commerce serving a multi-county area. WK&T Technology — a rural telecommunications cooperative — serves the county. Hopkins County's industrial heritage created electrical infrastructure density unusual for its current population, and the region has become a target for data center development given TVA power costs.

restrictions.name: Nelson County
restrictions.title: Nelson County — Bardstown/Bourbon Capital of the World/Heaven Hill/Barton 1792
restrictions.description: Bardstown in Nelson County is officially designated the 'Bourbon Capital of the World' by the Kentucky General Assembly — the county contains more bourbon distillery capacity than any other in the state. Heaven Hill Distilleries, Barton 1792, and dozens of craft distilleries operate here. The Kentucky Bourbon Trail draws hundreds of thousands of visitors annually. Distillery operations — temperature-controlled warehousing, barrel tracking, bottling line automation, and compliance IT — create significant technology demand per employee relative to most manufacturing sectors. Kentucky Utilities (LG&E/KU Energy) serves the county. Nelson County's bourbon economy makes it one of the most technologically intensive rural counties in Kentucky.

restrictions.name: Taney County
restrictions.title: Taney County — Branson/Table Rock Lake/Missouri's entertainment capital
restrictions.description: Taney County contains Branson — one of the most visited entertainment destinations in the US with over 10 million annual visitors, more than Disneyland. Branson's live music theaters, theme parks, and resort hotels create one of the highest concentrations of hospitality IT in the Midwest. Table Rock Lake, created by the Army Corps of Engineers, supports an enormous water recreation economy. Empire Electric Association and White River Valley Electric Cooperative serve the area. Branson's entertainment infrastructure — ticketing systems, hotel management, streaming/broadcast operations for its music shows, and the College of the Ozarks — creates technology demand far exceeding what the permanent population (120,000) suggests. The Silver Dollar City theme park alone has significant operational technology requirements.

restrictions.name: Randolph County
restrictions.title: Randolph County — Moberly/north-central Missouri hub/Rothwell Park
restrictions.description: Randolph County in north-central Missouri contains Moberly — the 'Magic City' of Missouri, known for its mid-19th century railroad boom growth. Moberly sits on US-24 and the former Wabash Railroad mainline connecting Kansas City to St. Louis. Moberly Area Community College provides technical education infrastructure for north-central Missouri. Ameren Missouri serves the county. The county's historic railroad infrastructure — Moberly was a major railroad crew change and maintenance point — created industrial electrical and telecommunications infrastructure in what is now a rural community. Randolph County's position between the Columbia market (already in DB) and the Kirksville market (Adair County, added in Round 20) makes it a fiber waypoint in north-central Missouri.

restrictions.name: Mitchell County
restrictions.title: Mitchell County — Beloit/Solomon River Valley/north-central Kansas hub
restrictions.description: Beloit in Mitchell County is the commercial hub for north-central Kansas in the Solomon River watershed. The county sits on US-24, the primary east-west highway through north-central Kansas, and US-281 connecting Salina to Nebraska. North Central Electric Cooperative serves the county. Mitchell County's agricultural economy drives irrigation SCADA for center-pivot systems on the Solomon River Aquifer. The county's position at the junction of two significant US highways gives it natural fiber corridor access for the region. Beloit's regional hospital and K-12 education infrastructure serve a multi-county area, creating IT concentration proportionate to its hub role rather than its own population.

restrictions.name: Jackson County
restrictions.title: Jackson County — Holton/Potawatomi Nation/northeast Kansas tribal economy
restrictions.description: Jackson County in northeast Kansas is the home of the Prairie Band Potawatomi Nation — one of Kansas's largest federally recognized tribes. The Potawatomi Casino Hotel (Prairie Band Casino) and tribal government operations create significant IT infrastructure in a rural county. Holton is the county seat and a regional agricultural and service hub. Evergy (formerly Westar Energy) serves the county. The Prairie Band Potawatomi's tribal broadband initiative has been a beneficiary of FCC and USDA rural broadband programs, and their data sovereignty policies make the tribe an active participant in federal tribal technology discussions. The county's position on US-75 connecting Topeka to Sabetha/Nebraska gives it north-south corridor infrastructure.

restrictions.name: Cleburne County
restrictions.title: Cleburne County — Heber Springs/Greers Ferry Lake/Little Red River
restrictions.description: Cleburne County in north-central Arkansas contains Heber Springs and Greers Ferry Lake — a major Army Corps of Engineers reservoir on the Little Red River. Greers Ferry Lake is one of the cleanest lakes in Arkansas and a significant recreation and second-home economy driver. The Little Red River below Greers Ferry Dam is a world-class trout fishery, creating year-round recreation IT demand. Entergy Arkansas serves the county. Cleburne County's second-home and vacation economy drives broadband demand disproportionate to permanent population. The county's proximity to the Little Rock market (30 minutes from the Quitman area) makes it a natural exurban growth zone for Arkansas's capital.

restrictions.name: Conway County
restrictions.title: Conway County — Morrilton/I-40 Arkansas River Valley/Acxiom heritage
restrictions.description: Conway County and Morrilton sit on I-40 in the Arkansas River Valley between Little Rock and Fort Smith, making the county a natural logistics and distribution waypoint. Morrilton is home to University of Arkansas Community College at Morrilton. The county's position on I-40 — carrying enormous east-west freight volume between the southeastern US and the Southwest — places it on the same fiber backbone as the major Arkansas commercial markets. Entergy Arkansas serves the county. The Arkansas River Valley's industrial base — including poultry processing and light manufacturing — creates supply chain IT demand. Conway County's river valley position with I-40 access represents a consistent industrial corridor asset.

restrictions.name: Day County
restrictions.title: Day County — Webster/NE South Dakota/Coteau des Prairies/Minnesota border
restrictions.description: Day County in northeastern South Dakota borders Minnesota and sits on the Coteau des Prairies — the highland plateau between the James River and Minnesota lake country. Webster is the county seat and the commercial hub for a large rural northeastern South Dakota area. The county has significant prairie pothole wetlands important for waterfowl production. Lake Region Electric Cooperative serves the area. Day County's position on US-12 — the primary east-west route through northern South Dakota and into Minnesota — provides fiber corridor access for the region. Agricultural IT for grain farming operations (wheat, corn, soybeans) and livestock is central to the county's technology footprint.

restrictions.name: Grant County
restrictions.title: Grant County — Milbank/quartzite capital/NE South Dakota border hub
restrictions.description: Grant County in northeastern South Dakota is known as the 'Quartzite Capital of the World' — Milbank's Sioux quartzite deposits have been quarried for construction stone since the 19th century. Milbank is the county seat and a significant commercial hub for northeastern South Dakota, serving as the market town for the granite and quartzite mining operations. Big Stone Power Plant (a coal plant now being repurposed for renewable energy) sits nearby at Big Stone City, at the South Dakota/Minnesota border. Otter Tail Power and East River Electric Cooperative serve the area. Grant County's border position, energy history, and quarrying operations create industrial IT requirements for a sparsely populated Great Plains county.

restrictions.name: Washington County
restrictions.title: Washington County — Brenham/Blue Bell Creameries/Blinn College/Austin-Houston corridor
restrictions.description: Washington County and Brenham sit midway on US-290 between Austin and Houston — one of the most traveled routes in Texas. Brenham is the home of Blue Bell Creameries, one of the most beloved regional ice cream brands in the US, with a manufacturing facility that is the county's largest employer. Blinn College's Brenham campus provides technical education. Washington County was the birthplace of Texas — Washington-on-the-Brazos State Historic Site is where the Texas Declaration of Independence was signed in 1836. The county's US-290 corridor position attracts logistics and distribution operations serving both the Austin and Houston markets. Oncor and Bluebonnet Electric Cooperative serve the county.

restrictions.name: Eastland County
restrictions.title: Eastland County — Cisco/I-20 West Texas entry/Ranger oil heritage
restrictions.description: Eastland County sits on I-20 at the gateway between the Dallas-Fort Worth market and West Texas — the county marks the transition from the Cross Timbers to the western Texas plains. Cisco is the county seat area's main commercial city and was historically significant as a 1919 oil boom town (the Ranger-Eastland oil field transformed northwest Texas). The county's position on I-20 — the primary freight route between Dallas and Midland-Odessa — makes it a natural fiber corridor waypoint. Oncor and West Texas Utilities (AEP) both operate transmission infrastructure in the county. Eastland County's ranching economy and its I-20 position give it above-average infrastructure for its small population.

restrictions.name: Loving County
restrictions.title: Loving County — least populous US county/Permian Basin core/Pecos River
restrictions.description: Loving County is the least populous county in the United States — with typically fewer than 100 permanent residents — yet it sits in the heart of the Permian Basin, one of the most productive oil-producing regions in the world. The Delaware Basin sub-basin underlying Loving County has been among the most aggressively drilled zones of the shale revolution. Despite its tiny population, the county's oil and gas infrastructure includes pipelines, SCADA systems, production monitoring networks, and fracking operation management technology from major operators including Cimarex (now Coterra Energy). Loving County is a stark illustration of how industrial energy production creates technology infrastructure completely decoupled from population metrics. The county's electricity comes from ERCOT via West Texas Utilities.

restrictions.name: San Augustine County
restrictions.title: San Augustine County — San Augustine/Deep East Texas/Angelina NF/Sam Rayburn Reservoir
restrictions.description: San Augustine County in deep East Texas contains part of the Sam Rayburn Reservoir — one of the largest lakes in Texas — created on the Angelina River. The Angelina National Forest covers much of the county, with the US Forest Service maintaining significant administrative and field IT systems. San Augustine is one of the oldest cities in Texas (settled 1716, predating US independence) and serves as the county seat of a historically timber-producing area. Entergy Texas serves the county. The Sam Rayburn/Toledo Bend reservoir complex on the Louisiana border creates a recreation and water management technology corridor through deep East Texas that is separate from the petrochemical and urban Texas markets.

restrictions.name: Menominee County
restrictions.title: Menominee County — Menominee/UP Michigan-Wisconsin border/Green Bay corridor
restrictions.description: Menominee County at the southern tip of Michigan's Upper Peninsula borders Marinette, Wisconsin across the Menominee River — together forming the Marinette-Menominee twin cities market. The county's manufacturing heritage includes paper mills that created substantial industrial electrical infrastructure through Upper Michigan Power (WEC Energy Group). Menominee's position at the UP/lower Michigan border on US-41, combined with its fiber connectivity to the Green Bay-Milwaukee market via Wisconsin, gives it unusual UP connectivity assets. The county's Lake Michigan shoreline, river-based hydroelectric history, and manufacturing legacy create a grid density that exceeds residential population metrics significantly.

restrictions.name: Dickinson County
restrictions.title: Dickinson County — Iron Mountain/UP mining heritage/Kingsford/Ford legacy
restrictions.description: Dickinson County in Michigan's Upper Peninsula contains Iron Mountain and Kingsford — Kingsford was literally created by Henry Ford, who established a Ford Motor Company wood-distillation plant here in the 1920s to produce charcoal from UP timber for his automobiles. (The charcoal briquette business Ford created eventually became Kingsford Charcoal Company.) The county's industrial heritage includes iron ore mines and Ford's operation, which created substantial electrical infrastructure. Upper Michigan Power (WEC Energy Group) serves the area. Iron Mountain is a regional UP hub with Bay de Noc Community College and a VA medical facility. Dickinson County's industrial past created grid capacity well above current residential load.

restrictions.name: Allegan County
restrictions.title: Allegan County — Allegan/Holland adjacent/SW Michigan pharmaceutical/Perrigo
restrictions.description: Allegan County in southwestern Michigan sits adjacent to Ottawa County (Holland, already in DB) and participates directly in the Holland-Grand Rapids pharmaceutical and manufacturing corridor. Perrigo Company, one of the world's largest generic pharmaceutical manufacturers, has historically had major operations in the county. The pharmaceutical manufacturing IT stack — FDA-regulated clean room monitoring, batch record management, LIMS systems — creates sophisticated technology infrastructure in a largely rural SW Michigan context. Indiana Michigan Power (AEP) and Consumers Energy serve the area. The county's position between Grand Rapids and the Lake Michigan resort economy creates both industrial and tourism IT demand.

restrictions.name: Wayne County
restrictions.title: Wayne County — Jesup/SE Georgia hub/rail corridor/Altamaha River basin
restrictions.description: Wayne County in southeastern Georgia contains Jesup — the county seat and a regional hub for the coastal plain between Savannah and the Florida border. Jesup is positioned at the junction of US-84 (the primary east-west highway through south Georgia) and US-341, creating a significant freight waypoint. The county's position between the Port of Brunswick (Glynn County, now in DB) and the interior south Georgia market makes it a logistics corridor. Altamaha Electric Membership Corporation serves the county. The Altamaha River watershed's paper mill heritage (Rayonier has historical operations in the area) created substantial industrial electrical infrastructure in the south Georgia coastal plain. Wayne County's rail heritage (CSX and Norfolk Southern lines intersect near Jesup) supports freight IT.

restrictions.name: Toombs County
restrictions.title: Toombs County — Vidalia/Vidalia sweet onion capital/SE Georgia agriculture
restrictions.description: Toombs County and Vidalia are the heart of the Vidalia sweet onion appellation — one of the few US agricultural products with a geographically protected designation, covering only counties in a specific southeast Georgia region. The Vidalia onion is Georgia's official state vegetable. The area's onion farming, packing, and cold storage operations create agricultural IT and food safety systems demand. Altamaha Electric Membership Corporation serves the county. Southeastern Technical College in Vidalia provides technical workforce development. Vidalia is also a regional commercial hub for Toombs and surrounding counties in southeast Georgia, with healthcare, retail, and services creating a concentration of IT infrastructure for a rural county.

restrictions.name: Bedford County
restrictions.title: Bedford County — Smith Mountain Lake/Blue Ridge gateway/D-Day Memorial
restrictions.description: Bedford County in central Virginia is home to Smith Mountain Lake — one of Virginia's largest lakes (20,000 acres), created by the Appalachian Power Company's Leesville Dam. The lake creates a significant second-home and resort economy, driving broadband demand well above permanent population. The National D-Day Memorial is in Bedford — the county suffered the highest per-capita losses of any US community on D-Day, June 6, 1944. Appalachian Power (AEP) serves the county and maintains the Smith Mountain Lake hydroelectric complex. The lake's recreation economy, combined with the county's position as a gateway to the Blue Ridge Parkway and Smith Mountain Wine Trail, creates tourism and hospitality IT well above rural Virginia norms.

restrictions.name: McClain County
restrictions.title: McClain County — Purcell/OKC south bedroom/Garvin-McClain oil fields
restrictions.description: McClain County sits directly south of Oklahoma County (Oklahoma City, already in DB) on I-35 and is part of the rapidly growing Oklahoma City south metro. Purcell is the county seat. The county's oil production from the Garvin-McClain field creates SCADA and production monitoring IT. As OKC's southern suburbs expand — particularly Newcastle and the I-35 corridor — McClain County's residential and commercial IT demand grows in step. Oklahoma Gas & Electric (OGE) serves the county. The county's position as OKC's primary southern commuter county, combined with its oil production history and growing suburb character, mirrors the development pattern of other fast-growing metro-adjacent rural counties.

restrictions.name: Delaware County
restrictions.title: Delaware County — Jay/Grand Lake/Cherokee Nation/NE Oklahoma lake hub
restrictions.description: Delaware County in extreme northeastern Oklahoma borders Missouri and Arkansas and contains Grand Lake O' the Cherokees — one of Oklahoma's most popular lakes and a major resort economy. Jay is the county seat. The county is within the historic Cherokee Nation territory, and the Cherokee Nation tribal government and associated enterprises create significant tribal IT infrastructure in the area. Grand Lake's resort economy — second homes, marinas, fishing tournaments — drives broadband demand well above the permanent population. KAMO Power and Oklahoma Rural Electric Cooperative serve the area. The county's tri-state border position, Cherokee Nation presence, and Grand Lake recreation economy make it one of northeastern Oklahoma's more distinctive IT markets.

restrictions.name: Fillmore County
restrictions.title: Fillmore County — Geneva/SE Nebraska/Blue River/agriculture corridor
restrictions.description: Fillmore County in southeastern Nebraska is anchored by Geneva, the county seat, along the Blue River. The county sits between York (US-81 corridor) and the Lincoln market on US-6. Nebraska Public Power District serves the county. Fillmore County's agricultural economy — corn, soybeans, wheat — drives precision agriculture technology and center-pivot irrigation monitoring. The county's position within the Nebraska Sandhills groundwater recharge zone creates water management IT for protecting the Ogallala Aquifer. Fillmore County's location on the historic Lincoln Highway (US-6) and its proximity to the York-Hastings corridor of central Nebraska place it in a natural fiber infrastructure waypoint zone.

restrictions.name: Webster County
restrictions.title: Webster County — Red Cloud/Willa Cather country/Republican River Valley
restrictions.description: Webster County in south-central Nebraska is the birthplace of Willa Cather — whose novels of the Nebraska prairie are among the most important works of American literature. Red Cloud, the county seat, has preserved Cather's childhood home and numerous sites from her fiction. The Willa Cather Foundation manages significant historical and cultural IT for literary heritage tourism. The county sits on the Republican River, participating in the multi-state water compact management IT framework. Nebraska Public Power District serves the county. Red Cloud has been designated an arts community and receives cultural tourism from across the US and internationally. The county's rural Great Plains character belies its national literary significance.

restrictions.name: Boundary County
restrictions.title: Boundary County — Bonners Ferry/NW Idaho/Canada border/Kootenai River
restrictions.description: Boundary County at Idaho's northernmost tip borders both Montana and Canada, with Bonners Ferry as the county seat on the Kootenai River. The county is a hub for international trade on US-2 connecting northern Idaho to the Pacific Northwest ports via the Canadian border crossing at Porthill. The Kootenai River has hydroelectric potential through Kootenai Electric Cooperative. Boundary County's logging and timber history created industrial electrical infrastructure in a remote mountain corridor. The county's proximity to the Canadian province of British Columbia gives it access to Canadian fiber networks through the Kootenai Valley. Idaho's remote north — the 'Idaho Panhandle' — is served by the same Pacific Northwest fiber infrastructure as Spokane, WA.

restrictions.name: Valley County
restrictions.title: Valley County — Cascade/McCall/Payette Lakes resort/tech executive retreat
restrictions.description: Valley County in central Idaho contains McCall — a mountain resort community on Payette Lake that has become one of Idaho's premier second-home and vacation destinations. The area has attracted permanent residents and second-home owners from Boise and beyond, including technology workers from the Treasure Valley. McCall's winter (Snowbird ski area) and summer (Lake Payette water sports) economies create year-round recreation IT demand. Idaho Power serves the county from hydroelectric resources. Valley County's scenic mountain character and Boise proximity (about 2 hours) make it part of the growing Idaho outdoor lifestyle economy that has attracted technology industry relocation to the state.

restrictions.name: Teton County
restrictions.title: Teton County — Driggs/Teton Valley/Grand Teton adjacent/tech executive community
restrictions.description: Teton County Idaho is the Teton Valley — the western flank of the Teton Range, just over the mountains from Jackson Hole (Teton County Wyoming). Driggs is the county seat. The valley has become a second-home and permanent residence destination for tech executives and remote workers who prefer Idaho's lower taxes and land costs versus Wyoming's Jackson Hole prices, while retaining access to Grand Teton National Park. Teton Valley's broadband infrastructure has been expanded through both federal rural broadband programs and tech industry demand. Idaho Power serves the county. The county's position on the Idaho-Wyoming border, adjacent to one of the most prestigious mountain resort markets in the US, drives IT investment far above what the permanent population of 12,000 suggests.

restrictions.name: Wabash County
restrictions.title: Wabash County — Wabash/first electrically lighted city/north-central Indiana
restrictions.description: Wabash city in Wabash County holds the distinction of being the first city in the world to be electrically lighted by a public system — on March 31, 1880, four brush arc lamps atop the courthouse dome illuminated the city. The county's manufacturing economy includes automotive components, pharmaceuticals (Indiana is a major pharma manufacturing state), and food processing. Indiana Michigan Power (AEP) and Duke Energy Indiana serve the county. Wabash County's US-24 corridor connecting Fort Wayne to the US-31 south Michigan market creates freight and fiber infrastructure. The county seat Wabash's historic electrical legacy is matched by its ongoing industrial IT needs.

restrictions.name: Jay County
restrictions.title: Jay County — Portland/east Indiana/Midwest Natural Gas System
restrictions.description: Jay County in east-central Indiana borders Ohio and is the home of Portland, the county seat. The county's industrial economy includes auto components manufacturing and plastics.
