Jay County is notable as a key node in Midwest natural gas transmission — the county sits on multiple natural gas pipeline corridors that have historically served Indiana's industrial base. Indiana Michigan Power and Duke Energy Indiana serve the area. Jay County's position on the Indiana-Ohio border along US-27 gives it highway corridor fiber access. Portland is a regional commercial hub serving east-central Indiana, with manufacturing employment well above rural Indiana averages.

restrictions.name: Jasper County
restrictions.title: Jasper County — Rensselaer/NW Indiana/Kankakee River/NIPSCO service territory
restrictions.description: Jasper County in northwestern Indiana contains Rensselaer, the county seat and home of Saint Joseph's College (now closed, but with remaining campus infrastructure). The county sits in the Kankakee River floodplain, historically one of the most important wetland drainage areas in the Midwest. NIPSCO (Northern Indiana Public Service Company) serves Jasper County as part of its northwest Indiana service territory. The county's position on US-231 and I-65 — the primary north-south freight corridor through Indiana connecting Chicago to Louisville — creates logistics and distribution IT demand. NIPSCO's northwest Indiana grid, upgraded to accommodate the region's industrial base and the Chicago exurban growth corridor, creates power availability above typical rural Indiana levels.

restrictions.name: Dunn County
restrictions.title: Dunn County — Killdeer/western ND Bakken oil patch/Little Missouri Badlands
restrictions.description: Dunn County sits in western North Dakota at the edge of the Bakken formation and the Little Missouri River Badlands. Killdeer is the county seat. The county contains significant Bakken shale oil production, with SCADA monitoring, pipeline telemetry, and well automation creating industrial IT well above residential population. The Killdeer Mountains — glacially carved buttes in otherwise flat plains — are a distinctive geographic marker in the western ND badlands. MDU Resources (Montana-Dakota Utilities) serves the county. Dunn County's oil production connects to the extensive Bakken pipeline network that routes crude to Clearbrook and Guernsey export points.

restrictions.name: Bottineau County
restrictions.title: Bottineau County — Bottineau/International Peace Garden/Turtle Mountain adjacent
restrictions.description: Bottineau County in north-central North Dakota borders Canada and contains the United States portion of the International Peace Garden — a 2,300-acre garden straddling the US-Canada border, dedicated to peace between the two nations. Bottineau is the county seat and a regional hub for north-central North Dakota. The county sits adjacent to Rolette County (Turtle Mountain Band, added in Round 19) and participates in the same tribal economy and federal broadband infrastructure. North Central Electric Cooperative serves the county. Bottineau County's grain farming economy — wheat, barley, sunflowers — drives precision agriculture monitoring IT, and the Peace Garden creates international tourism IT for a very rural county.

restrictions.name: Emmons County
restrictions.title: Emmons County — Linton/south-central ND/Standing Rock adjacent/Missouri River
restrictions.description: Emmons County in south-central North Dakota borders South Dakota and sits adjacent to the Standing Rock Sioux Tribe's territory. Linton is the county seat. The county is part of the Missouri River valley agricultural zone, with wheat, corn, and cattle operations. Standing Rock Electric Cooperative serves the area, with connections to the rural electric grid serving the Standing Rock Reservation. Emmons County's position on US-83 — the primary north-south highway through the ND-SD border region — gives it highway corridor fiber access. The county's agricultural economy and proximity to the Standing Rock Tribal economy create modest but consistent IT demand in one of North Dakota's most rural counties.

restrictions.name: Summit County
restrictions.title: Summit County — Breckenridge/Keystone/Frisco/I-70 mountain resort tech hub
restrictions.description: Summit County contains four major ski resorts — Breckenridge, Keystone, Copper Mountain, and Arapahoe Basin — making it one of the highest-density ski resort counties in the US. The county has become a significant permanent residence for technology workers and executives who work remotely from the Denver market or beyond, attracted by world-class skiing, mountain biking, and outdoor access. Xcel Energy serves the county. Summit County's I-70 corridor position gives it fiber infrastructure connectivity to Denver (75 miles). The county's affluent second-home population drives broadband investment far above what the permanent 32,000 residents would justify.

restrictions.name: Garfield County
restrictions.title: Garfield County — Glenwood Springs/Piceance Basin/I-70 mountain corridor
restrictions.description: Garfield County in western Colorado contains Glenwood Springs — a significant I-70 mountain resort and gateway city — and sits above the Piceance Basin, one of the largest natural gas producing areas in the Rocky Mountain region. The county's energy production (natural gas from the Williams Fork and Mesaverde formations) creates SCADA and production monitoring IT for a major energy-producing county. Glenwood Springs is served by Holy Cross Energy (a rural electric cooperative). The I-70 corridor through the Glenwood Canyon (one of the most dramatic highway corridors in the US) provides fiber infrastructure connecting Denver and the Front Range to the Utah/Nevada markets. Garfield County is a major contributor to Colorado's natural gas production.

restrictions.name: Pitkin County
restrictions.title: Pitkin County — Aspen/tech executive enclave/Aspen Institute/global ideas hub
restrictions.description: Pitkin County contains Aspen — one of the most iconic mountain luxury destinations in the world and a global gathering point for business, technology, and policy leaders. The Aspen Institute, Aspen Ideas Festival, and Aspen Security Forum annually bring the top tier of US and global leadership to Aspen. Pitkin County has the highest median home price of any county in the US in most years. Tech executives and investors maintain second (and first) homes here. Holy Cross Energy and Aspen Electric serve the area. The county's ultra-high-bandwidth connectivity demands from its affluent, globally connected resident and visitor base drive fiber infrastructure investment that far exceeds what either population or geography would suggest.

restrictions.name: Barron County
restrictions.title: Barron County — Rice Lake/NW Wisconsin hub/dairy and cranberry corridor
restrictions.description: Barron County in northwestern Wisconsin contains Rice Lake — the county seat and largest city, a regional commercial hub for a wide NW Wisconsin area. The county's dairy farming economy is complemented by a significant cranberry production sector — Wisconsin is the world's leading cranberry producer and Barron County participates in the industry. Xcel Energy and Barron Electric Cooperative serve the area. Rice Lake's regional hospital, retail, and service economy makes it one of the more significant secondary markets in NW Wisconsin. The county's US-8 and US-53 corridors connect it to the Twin Cities and Eau Claire markets, giving it highway fiber infrastructure.

restrictions.name: Iron County
restrictions.title: Iron County — Hurley/Gogebic Iron Range/UP border/historic mining legacy
restrictions.description: Iron County in northern Wisconsin borders the UP of Michigan and was the Wisconsin portion of the Gogebic Iron Range — one of the great iron ore regions of the US, producing ore from the 1880s through the 20th century. Hurley is the county seat and historically a companion city to Ironwood, Michigan. The county's mining infrastructure — including electrical transmission lines built to power ore processing — created grid capacity far exceeding current residential demand. Northern Wisconsin Electric Cooperative serves the area. Iron County's position adjacent to the UP Michigan copper/iron district gives it industrial heritage and grid infrastructure associated with heavy mining that persists long after the mines closed.

restrictions.name: Jackson County
restrictions.title: Jackson County — Black River Falls/Ho-Chunk Nation/west-central Wisconsin
restrictions.description: Jackson County in west-central Wisconsin is home to the Ho-Chunk Nation — the primary Indigenous nation of central Wisconsin, with a reservation and tribal government centered in Black River Falls. The Ho-Chunk Nation's casino operations (Ho-Chunk Casino Black River Falls), tribal government, and federal broadband programs create IT infrastructure in an otherwise rural forested county. Black River Falls is the county seat. Dairyland Power Cooperative and Jackson Electric Cooperative serve the area. The Black River — one of Wisconsin's best trout fishing rivers — creates recreation IT. The Ho-Chunk Nation's digital sovereignty initiatives have made Jackson County a beneficiary of federal tribal broadband and digital equity programs.

restrictions.name: Letcher County
restrictions.title: Letcher County — Whitesburg/eastern Kentucky coal/Appalshop/ARC programs
restrictions.description: Letcher County in southeastern Kentucky is the home of Appalshop — a nationally recognized arts and media organization that has documented Appalachian culture through film, theater, and radio since 1969, and is a key institution in eastern Kentucky's creative economy. Whitesburg is the county seat. The county's deep coal mining history created substantial electrical infrastructure in the mountains, and the TVA grid serves the area through Kentucky Power (AEP). The Appalachian Regional Commission has targeted Letcher County for economic development programs. The proposed Letcher County federal prison (controversial but a focus of federal investment discussions) and broadband expansion initiatives have made the county a focus of significant federal technology infrastructure attention.

restrictions.name: Ohio County
restrictions.title: Ohio County — Hartford/western Kentucky coal belt/Green River corridor
restrictions.description: Ohio County in western Kentucky is a traditional coal-producing county in the Western Kentucky Coal Field, served by Kentucky Utilities (LG&E/KU Energy). Hartford is the county seat. The county's coal mining history created substantial transmission infrastructure in the Green River valley. The Green River — Kentucky's second-longest river — runs through Ohio County, with historical boat traffic having created early industrial infrastructure in the area. Ohio County sits between the Owensboro (Daviess County) and Bowling Green markets on US-62, giving it a regional corridor position. The coal belt transition economy has brought federal broadband and workforce development investment to Ohio County.

restrictions.name: Hancock County
restrictions.title: Hancock County — Hawesville/Century Aluminum smelter/Ohio River industrial
restrictions.description: Hancock County in northwestern Kentucky is home to the Century Aluminum smelter in Hawesville — one of the largest aluminum smelters in the United States. Primary aluminum smelting is among the most electricity-intensive industrial processes on earth, consuming hundreds of megawatts continuously. Kentucky Utilities provides power for the smelter under long-term industrial rate contracts that reflect the county's extraordinary baseload power consumption. The Ohio River provides cooling water and barge access. Hancock County's power infrastructure — scaled to serve a smelter's demand — creates electrical grid capacity in this rural Ohio River county far exceeding residential needs. The smelter's SCADA and process control systems represent substantial industrial IT.

restrictions.name: Brewster County
restrictions.title: Brewster County — Alpine/Big Bend NP/largest Texas county/Trans-Pecos hub
restrictions.description: Brewster County is the largest county in Texas and one of the largest counties in the US by area — at 6,193 square miles, it is larger than Connecticut and Rhode Island combined. Alpine, the county seat, contains Sul Ross State University, the primary higher education institution for the vast Trans-Pecos region of west Texas. Big Bend National Park covers the southern portion of the county and creates significant federal IT infrastructure (park management, border patrol operations). Rio Grande Electric Cooperative serves this enormous, remote territory. The county's remoteness and size make its broadband challenges unique — fiber buildout to Alpine is far more technically complex than any comparable East Texas county. The county's astronomy economy (McDonald Observatory is in adjacent Jeff Davis County) drives dark-sky research IT.

restrictions.name: Moniteau County
restrictions.title: Moniteau County — California MO/mid-Missouri agriculture hub/US-50 corridor
restrictions.description: Moniteau County in central Missouri contains California — the county seat, not to be confused with the state — a mid-Missouri agricultural service town. The county sits on US-50, which is the primary east-west highway through central Missouri connecting Jefferson City (Cole County, already in DB) to Kansas City. Ameren Missouri serves the county. Moniteau County's position in the rich central Missouri agricultural zone — corn, soybeans, and cattle — creates precision agriculture and commodity handling IT demand. California MO's position as a regional service center for the surrounding counties, combined with its US-50 corridor access to Jefferson City and the state government computing cluster, makes it a fiber waypoint in mid-Missouri.

restrictions.name: Audubon County
restrictions.title: Audubon County — Audubon/west Iowa/agricultural corridor
restrictions.description: Audubon County in western Iowa is named for the naturalist John James Audubon and sits in the heart of Iowa's western agricultural zone. Audubon, the county seat, is known for the enormous Albert the Bull statue — one of the world's largest bull statues, a testament to the county's cattle heritage. Iowa Lakes Electric Cooperative and Alliant Energy serve the county. Audubon County's corn and soybean farming economy drives precision agriculture and commodity IT demand. The county's position on US-71 connecting I-80 to I-90 in South Dakota gives it north-south corridor fiber access. Iowa's aggressive rural broadband build-out has included Audubon County in its fiber expansion initiatives.

restrictions.name: Butler County
restrictions.title: Butler County — Allison/north-central Iowa/Shell Rock River/agricultural hub
restrictions.description: Butler County in north-central Iowa contains Allison, the county seat, on the Shell Rock River. The county sits in the agricultural heartland between Waterloo (Black Hawk County, already in DB) and Mason City (Cerro Gordo County, likely already in DB). Iowa Lakes Electric Cooperative and Alliant Energy serve the county. Butler County's grain farming — corn and soybeans — drives standard Iowa agricultural IT: commodity handling monitoring, center-pivot irrigation SCADA, and precision agriculture technology. The county's position between two major Iowa markets on US-218 (the Avenue of the Saints corridor) gives it highway fiber infrastructure. Butler County's dairy processing and hog operations add food processing IT to the county's technology footprint.

restrictions.name: Becker County
restrictions.title: Becker County — Detroit Lakes/NW Minnesota resort lakes/White Earth Nation
restrictions.description: Becker County in northwestern Minnesota contains Detroit Lakes — one of Minnesota's premier lake resort communities, with hundreds of lakes attracting Twin Cities vacationers. The county is also home to part of the White Earth Nation — one of Minnesota's largest Ojibwe tribal communities — with tribal government, White Earth Tribal and Community College, and federal broadband programs creating IT infrastructure. Otter Tail Power and Minnesota Power serve the county. Detroit Lakes' tourism economy creates significant hospitality and vacation rental IT. The county's position on US-10 and US-59 connecting Fargo-Moorhead to the Twin Cities gives it corridor fiber access.

restrictions.name: Faribault County
restrictions.title: Faribault County — Blue Earth/Green Giant heritage/south Minnesota wind corridor
restrictions.description: Faribault County in south-central Minnesota is the home of Blue Earth — the city from which the Green Giant brand takes its vegetable imagery (the Jolly Green Giant statue in Blue Earth is a Route 66-era landmark). The county's sweet corn and pea processing heritage made Blue Earth a significant food processing IT hub for the Minnesota River Valley agricultural market. Faribault County sits in southern Minnesota's most productive wind energy corridor — multiple wind farms in and adjacent to the county create renewable energy IT and grid management complexity. Alliant Energy serves the county. Blue Earth's historic food processing legacy and the county's wind energy build-out represent two distinct technology demand drivers.

restrictions.name: Appomattox County
restrictions.title: Appomattox County — Appomattox Court House/Civil War surrender site/central Virginia
restrictions.description: Appomattox County is where the Civil War effectively ended — Appomattox Court House National Historical Park preserves the site where General Robert E. Lee surrendered to General Ulysses S. Grant on April 9, 1865. The National Park Service's digital preservation, visitor management, and historical record management systems at this nationally significant site create federal IT infrastructure in a very rural county. Appomattox is the county seat, served by Appalachian Power (AEP) and local utilities. The county's position midway between Lynchburg and Farmville on US-460 gives it highway corridor fiber access. Appomattox County's heritage tourism — drawing thousands of Civil War enthusiasts annually — creates hospitality IT above rural Virginia norms.

restrictions.name: Mecklenburg County
restrictions.title: Mecklenburg County — South Hill/Kerr Reservoir/south Virginia-North Carolina border
restrictions.description: Mecklenburg County in southside Virginia borders North Carolina and contains John H. Kerr Reservoir (also known as Buggs Island Lake) — one of the largest lakes in the mid-Atlantic region, created by the Army Corps of Engineers and generating hydroelectric power. South Hill is the county seat and a commercial hub for southside Virginia's tobacco-heritage agricultural economy. The reservoir's water management and hydroelectric operations create federal IT. Mecklenburg Electric Cooperative and Dominion Energy serve the county. The Virginia-North Carolina border corridor has attracted data center development interest from companies seeking Dominion Energy's data center incentive program rates. Mecklenburg County's low land costs, rail access (NS mainline), and power grid make it an active data center consideration area in southside Virginia.

restrictions.name: Braxton County
restrictions.title: Braxton County — Sutton/Elk River/Burnsville Lake/central WV hub
restrictions.description: Braxton County in central West Virginia contains the Elk River valley and Burnsville Lake — an Army Corps of Engineers reservoir providing flood control and recreation. Sutton is the county seat, a commercial hub for central West Virginia between the Charleston and Elkins markets. Appalachian Power (AEP) serves the county. Central WV's natural gas production creates SCADA and pipeline IT. West Virginia American Water Company operates infrastructure in the county. Braxton County's position on US-19 — the primary north-south highway through central WV — gives it road corridor fiber access. The county's rural character and forest economy have made it a beneficiary of USDA rural broadband and timber management IT programs.

restrictions.name: Brooke County
restrictions.title: Brooke County — Wellsburg/northernmost WV/Ohio River/natural gas heritage
restrictions.description: Brooke County is the northernmost county in West Virginia, occupying a narrow strip along the Ohio River between Pennsylvania and Ohio. Wellsburg is the county seat. The county is adjacent to Ohio County (Wheeling, already in DB) and directly across the Ohio River from Pennsylvania. The county's industrial heritage includes glass manufacturing and natural gas production from Devonian and Marcellus formations — WV's northern Panhandle was one of the earliest natural gas producing regions in the US. Mountaineer Gas and Equitable Gas have operated in the area for over a century. The county's proximity to Wheeling's IT market and its natural gas infrastructure legacy make Brooke County a participant in northern WV's technology corridor.

restrictions.name: Boone County
restrictions.title: Boone County — Madison/WV coal heartland/Big Coal River/mining technology
restrictions.description: Boone County in south-central West Virginia is one of the state's most historically significant coal counties — the Big Coal River valley drove enormous coal production throughout the 20th century. Madison is the county seat. Boone County's mining heritage includes longwall mining operations that represent some of the most sophisticated industrial monitoring technology in the US. Mine safety systems (continuous monitoring, methane detection, MSHA-required communications systems) create industrial IT requirements unique to underground coal counties. Appalachian Power (AEP) serves the county. West Virginia's post-coal transition includes federal investment in broadband and technology retraining programs specifically targeting Boone County-type communities.

restrictions.name: Morgan County
restrictions.title: Morgan County — Berkeley Springs/first American spa/DC market second homes
restrictions.description: Morgan County contains Berkeley Springs (officially Bath, WV) — the first American spa and the only town with springs in its official name. George Washington visited Berkeley Springs, and it became America's first and oldest continuously operating spa resort. The spa's location in eastern West Virginia — about 120 miles from Washington DC — has made Morgan County a popular second-home and weekend retreat market for the DC metro area. The county's proximity to the DC tech corridor (with spillover from the NSA complex at Fort Meade and Northern Virginia data centers) creates above-average broadband demand. Allegheny Power and Mountaineer Gas serve the area. Morgan County's scenic Eastern Panhandle character has attracted remote workers from the DC market.

restrictions.name: Coles County
restrictions.title: Coles County — Charleston/Eastern Illinois University/Lincoln log cabin heritage
restrictions.description: Coles County in east-central Illinois is home to Eastern Illinois University in Charleston — a regional state university providing education infrastructure for a wide rural area. The county also contains Lincoln Log Cabin State Historic Site — where Abraham Lincoln's father and stepmother spent their later years — making it a significant heritage tourism destination. Ameren Illinois serves the county. Charleston's US-16 corridor position connects it to Mattoon (Coles County's larger twin city) and the I-57 corridor. The county's agricultural economy — corn and soybeans — drives standard Illinois precision agriculture IT. Eastern Illinois University's technology and STEM programs create research computing demand in an otherwise rural east-central Illinois setting.

restrictions.name: Clark County
restrictions.title: Clark County — Marshall/I-70 IL-IN border/Darwin Coal Oil Field heritage
restrictions.description: Clark County in southeastern Illinois sits on I-70 at the Illinois-Indiana border near Marshall. The county was home to the historic Darwin Oil Field — one of Illinois's earliest oil-producing areas, dating to the 19th century. I-70's east-west fiber infrastructure runs directly through Clark County, making it a natural waypoint between Indianapolis and St. Louis markets. Ameren Illinois serves the county. Clark County's Casey township contains the 'World's Largest Items' tourist attraction — a collection of oversized roadside sculptures that drive tourism IT. The county's I-70 corridor position, combined with its agriculture and remnant oil production, creates a diverse IT demand profile for a small rural Illinois county.

restrictions.name: Macoupin County
restrictions.title: Macoupin County — Carlinville/Gillespie/central Illinois coal/Standard Oil history
restrictions.description: Macoupin County in central Illinois has a rich industrial and labor history — the county's coal mines were among the most organized in the US and helped shape the United Mine Workers of America. Carlinville is the county seat. Blackburn College in Carlinville provides higher education. The county's coal mining history created substantial electrical transmission infrastructure through Ameren Illinois. Gillespie, in the northern part of the county, is a former coal company town. Standard Oil of Ohio once had operations in the county. Macoupin County's position on Route 66 (now IL-4) and its coal heritage infrastructure create both historical depth and industrial grid capacity in a central Illinois agricultural county.

restrictions.name: Val Verde County
restrictions.title: Val Verde County — Del Rio/Laughlin AFB/Amistad Reservoir/US-Mexico border IT
restrictions.description: Val Verde County and Del Rio sit on the Rio Grande at the US-Mexico border, where Amistad Reservoir — a joint US-Mexico international water project — creates one of the most significant cross-border infrastructure management environments in the US. Laughlin Air Force Base in Del Rio is the premier pilot training base for the US Air Force, producing more rated pilots than any other USAF base. The base's training management systems, simulation infrastructure, and aviation IT create a major federal computing presence in this remote border county. Del Rio is also the commercial hub for a vast region of southwest Texas, and the US Border Patrol maintains significant surveillance technology infrastructure throughout the county.

restrictions.name: Bandera County
restrictions.title: Bandera County — Bandera/Cowboy Capital of the World/Hill Country/San Antonio exurb
restrictions.description: Bandera County in the Texas Hill Country holds the official designation as the 'Cowboy Capital of the World' — a working cowboy community and Western heritage center that has become one of Texas's most distinctive ranch and dude ranch tourism destinations. Bandera is the county seat. The county has attracted significant second-home and full-time migration from San Antonio (~50 miles south), particularly from technology and professional workers seeking the Hill Country lifestyle. Bandera Electric Cooperative and CPS Energy serve the area. The county's growth as part of San Antonio's expanding exurban market has driven broadband infrastructure investment well above what traditional Hill Country counties would attract.

restrictions.name: Rabun County
restrictions.title: Rabun County — Clayton/NE Georgia mountains/Chattooga Wild River/lake resort community
restrictions.description: Rabun County in extreme northeastern Georgia is the most mountainous county in the state, containing the headwaters of the Chattooga Wild and Scenic River (where the film Deliverance was shot) and Lakes Burton, Rabun, and Seed. Clayton is the county seat and a growing arts and second-home community attracting migrants from Atlanta (~2 hours south). The county's position adjacent to the Great Smoky Mountains tourism corridor and its summer climate (significantly cooler than Atlanta) have driven aggressive second-home development. Georgia Power serves the county. Rabun County's lake resort and arts economy creates IT demand from an affluent, mobile population far exceeding what its 17,000 permanent residents would generate.

restrictions.name: Towns County
restrictions.title: Towns County — Hiawassee/Chatuge Lake/NE Georgia mountains/smallest county
restrictions.description: Towns County is one of the smallest counties in Georgia by area and population (~13,000), located in the extreme northeast Georgia mountains at the North Carolina border. Hiawassee is the county seat, situated on Lake Chatuge — a TVA reservoir at the headwaters of the Hiwassee River. The county's mountain scenery, lake, and cooler summer climate have made it a retirement and second-home destination attracting migrants from Atlanta. Georgia Mountain Fair is a major annual event. Georgia Power (Southern Company) serves the county. The TVA's Lake Chatuge creates federal water management IT, and the county's growing retirement population drives healthcare and broadband investment above what its size suggests.

restrictions.name: Lumpkin County
restrictions.title: Lumpkin County — Dahlonega/Georgia Gold Rush/North Georgia military college/wine country
restrictions.description: Lumpkin County and Dahlonega are where the first major US gold rush occurred in 1829 — twenty years before California's 1849 rush. The US Mint operated at Dahlonega from 1838 to 1861. The University of North Georgia (Dahlonega) is a senior military college and one of six military colleges in the US, training ROTC cadets and feeding the Army officer pipeline. The North Georgia wine country surrounding Dahlonega — with 30+ wineries — creates agritourism IT. Georgia Power and North Georgia Electric Membership Corporation serve the county. Lumpkin County's gold heritage, military college, and wine tourism create a distinctive multi-sector IT profile for a mountain Georgia county.

restrictions.name: Hardeman County
restrictions.title: Hardeman County — Bolivar/west Tennessee/Tennessee Psychiatric Center/Hatchie NWR
restrictions.description: Hardeman County in west Tennessee contains Bolivar — the county seat and a small historic city with antebellum architecture. The Tennessee Department of Mental Health's Western Mental Health Institute (formerly Bolivar State Hospital) has been a major state government employer in the county. The Hatchie National Wildlife Refuge protects bottomland hardwood forest along the Hatchie River — the last largely unchanneled river in the Lower Mississippi Valley — creating USFWS monitoring and data management systems. Hardeman County's position on US-64 between Memphis and Corinth MS gives it corridor connectivity. TVA and West Tennessee Electric Membership Corporation provide power. The county's state hospital history and refuge monitoring create public IT footprints in a largely agricultural county.

restrictions.name: Scott County
restrictions.title: Scott County — Huntsville TN/northern Cumberland Plateau/Big South Fork
restrictions.description: Scott County in northeastern Tennessee occupies the Cumberland Plateau north of Knoxville, adjacent to the Big South Fork National River and Recreation Area (which straddles the TN-KY border). Huntsville (not Alabama's Huntsville) is the county seat and a regional hub for the Upper Cumberland Plateau. The county's position between the Knoxville and Crossville markets on the plateau gives it Highway 27 corridor access. TVA provides power through local distributors. Scott County's timber, coal heritage (now largely past), and recreation economy create an IT footprint anchored by federal Big South Fork park operations and rural tourism. Tennessee's rural broadband expansion has included Scott County in fiber build-out initiatives.

restrictions.name: Fulton County
restrictions.title: Fulton County — Salem/north-central Arkansas Ozarks/Spring River recreation
restrictions.description: Fulton County in north-central Arkansas borders Missouri and is anchored by Salem, the county seat. The Spring River — one of Arkansas's premier float fishing rivers — runs through the county, creating recreation and tourism IT. Fulton County's Ozarks position makes it part of the same rural broadband expansion targeting that has driven connectivity investment into the Buffalo National River and Arkansas Ozarks tourism corridor. North Arkansas Electric Cooperative serves the county. The county's position on US-62 connecting the NW Arkansas metro market to the Mountain Home/Norfolk Lake area gives it corridor fiber access through the Ozarks.

restrictions.name: Lafayette County
restrictions.title: Lafayette County — Lewisville/southwest Arkansas/cotton and timber country
restrictions.description: Lafayette County in extreme southwest Arkansas borders both Texas and Louisiana, making it one of the more remote corners of the state. Lewisville is the county seat. The county's economy is anchored by timber (Weyerhaeuser and International Paper have operated in the region), cotton farming, and oil and gas production that extends from the adjacent East Texas field. Southwestern Electric Power Company (SWEPCO/AEP) serves the county. Lafayette County's tri-state border position gives it access to both the Shreveport/Bossier market in Louisiana and the Texarkana market. The county's timber and agricultural operations create commodity handling and field monitoring IT for a very rural southwest Arkansas setting.

restrictions.name: Dawson County
restrictions.title: Dawson County — Glendive/eastern Montana/Yellowstone River/Makoshika State Park
restrictions.description: Dawson County in eastern Montana contains Glendive — the largest city in eastern Montana between Miles City and the North Dakota border. The county sits in the Yellowstone River valley, and Makoshika State Park (the largest Montana state park) within the county contains some of the most significant dinosaur fossil beds in the US — Hell Creek Formation exposures that have produced T. rex and Triceratops specimens. The county's oil production from the Cedar Creek Anticline and other eastern Montana fields creates SCADA and pipeline IT. Dawson Community College and the county's regional healthcare function create IT above what its 9,000 population suggests. MDU Resources (Montana-Dakota Utilities) serves the county.

restrictions.name: Carbon County
restrictions.title: Carbon County — Red Lodge/Beartooth Pass/Yellowstone gateway/coal heritage
restrictions.description: Carbon County in south-central Montana is home to Red Lodge — a former coal mining town that has reinvented itself as an outdoor recreation and arts community at the foot of the Beartooth Mountains. The Beartooth Highway (US-212) — the highest paved highway in the US — connects Red Lodge to Yellowstone's northeast entrance and is considered one of the most scenic drives in America. Carbon County's coal mining past (Red Lodge Coal Company operated here) created industrial electrical infrastructure. NorthWestern Energy serves the county. Red Lodge Mountain Resort creates winter recreation IT. Carbon County's Yellowstone gateway position drives tourism IT, and its proximity to Billings (Yellowstone County, already in DB) 65 miles north provides fiber corridor connectivity.

restrictions.name: Pike County
restrictions.title: Pike County — McComb/south Mississippi hub/I-55 corridor/civil rights heritage
restrictions.description: Pike County in south-central Mississippi contains McComb — the most significant commercial city in the south Mississippi corridor between Jackson and New Orleans. McComb sits on I-55 and the historic Illinois Central Railroad line (now CN), creating a critical freight corridor. The city has significant civil rights history — SNCC organized Freedom Schools in McComb in the early 1960s. Amtrak's City of New Orleans stops at McComb. Entergy Mississippi and Mississippi Power serve the area. McComb's position as the primary commercial hub between Jackson and the Louisiana border makes it the retail, healthcare, and distribution center for a multi-county region. Pike County Hospital serves a significant rural catchment area.

restrictions.name: Yazoo County
restrictions.title: Yazoo County — Yazoo City/central Mississippi Delta/cotton and gas heritage
restrictions.description: Yazoo County in central Mississippi sits at the edge of the Yazoo-Mississippi Delta, where the Delta meets the central Mississippi loess hills. Yazoo City is the county seat and a significant commercial hub for the central Mississippi Delta region. The county's natural gas production history — the Yazoo gas field was one of Mississippi's earliest producing formations — created industrial infrastructure. Entergy Mississippi and Mississippi Delta Electric Power Association serve the county. Yazoo City's position on US-49 connecting Jackson to Greenwood/Cleveland gives it highway corridor fiber access through the Delta. The county's historic cotton economy and its position as a Delta-edge city with Jackson proximity make it a transition zone between the agricultural Delta and the Jackson industrial economy.

restrictions.name: Allen County
restrictions.title: Allen County — Iola/Mid-Continent gas field/cement industry/Allen CC
restrictions.description: Allen County in southeastern Kansas contains Iola — a historic gas boomtown where the Mid-Continent Natural Gas Field was discovered in the early 20th century, making it one of the first major US natural gas producing areas. Iola was also a center for Portland cement manufacturing, leveraging local limestone and natural gas. Allen County Community College serves the county and regional SE Kansas area. Evergy (formerly Westar) serves the county. The US-54 corridor through Iola connects the Wichita and Joplin markets. Allen County's gas heritage created industrial electrical infrastructure, and the county's cement history produced industrial loads that created grid capacity above what its 13,000 residents alone need.

restrictions.name: Barber County
restrictions.title: Barber County — Medicine Lodge/Carry Nation birthplace/Peace Treaties/Gyp Hills oil
restrictions.description: Barber County in south-central Kansas is the birthplace of Carry Nation — the Kansas temperance activist whose hatchet-wielding attacks on saloons became one of the most dramatic protest actions in US history. Medicine Lodge, the county seat, was also the site of the 1867 Medicine Lodge Treaties, where the US government negotiated peace with the southern Plains tribes. The Gyp Hills Scenic Byway in Barber County provides some of the most dramatic landscapes in Kansas. The county sits above the Hugoton Gas Field and has significant oil and gas production. Barber County Electric Cooperative and local utilities serve the area.

restrictions.name: Chautauqua County
restrictions.title: Chautauqua County — Sedan/SE Kansas/Chautauqua Hills/elk herd heritage
restrictions.description: Chautauqua County in extreme southeastern Kansas is one of the most sparsely populated counties in the state, home to Sedan — a small historic city that hosted the famous Chautauqua circuit gatherings in the late 19th and early 20th centuries. The county's Chautauqua Hills — unique eroded sandstone formations unusual in Kansas — create distinctive terrain. The county is known for one of the largest free-ranging elk herds in the eastern US, which creates ecotourism IT and wildlife monitoring systems. SEK Electric and Independence Power & Light serve the area. Chautauqua County's oil and gas production from the Verdigris River Valley creates production monitoring IT.

restrictions.name: Elk County
restrictions.title: Elk County — Howard/Longton/SE Kansas/Elk River Valley/oil and gas
restrictions.description: Elk County in southeastern Kansas contains Howard and Longton — small county seat communities in the Elk River valley. The county was heavily involved in early Kansas oil and gas production, with the Mid-Continent Field extending through the area. Elk City Lake provides water recreation and watershed management IT. Southeast Kansas Electric Cooperative serves the county. Elk County's position adjacent to Oklahoma's oil country creates pipeline and energy infrastructure corridors through the county. The county's cattle ranching economy drives agricultural management technology, and the remnant oil and gas infrastructure creates SCADA monitoring requirements for active production and pipeline operations.

restrictions.name: Barry County
restrictions.title: Barry County — Cassville/SW Missouri Ozarks/Table Rock Lake adjacent/poultry hub
restrictions.description: Barry County in southwest Missouri borders Arkansas and sits at the edge of the Table Rock Lake recreation market (Taney County's Branson, already in DB). Cassville is the county seat. The county's poultry processing industry — with major Tyson Foods and other operations drawing from the Ozark broiler region — creates food safety IT, supply chain management, and cold chain monitoring demand in a rural southwest Missouri county. Poultry and egg production makes Barry County one of the most agriculturally intensive in southwest Missouri. Empire Electric Association serves the county. The county's position on US-60 between Springfield (Greene County, already in DB) and Joplin provides highway corridor fiber access.

restrictions.name: Bates County
restrictions.title: Bates County — Butler/west Missouri border/I-49 corridor/Marais des Cygnes
restrictions.description: Bates County in western Missouri borders Kansas and sits on US-71 (now I-49) — the principal north-south highway connecting Kansas City to Joplin and the Fort Smith AR market. Butler is the county seat. The county was the site of significant Civil War-era violence during the Missouri border wars with Kansas (the 'Bleeding Kansas' period). The Marais des Cygnes River watershed creates agricultural and water management IT. Evergy and Kansas City Power & Light serve the county. Bates County's I-49 corridor position — on the primary freight route between the KC metro and the south — makes it a natural fiber waypoint and logistics staging area between Missouri's two largest economic anchors.

restrictions.name: Oregon County
restrictions.title: Oregon County — Alton/remote SE Missouri Ozarks/Eleven Point National Scenic River
restrictions.description: Oregon County in extreme southeastern Missouri is one of the most remote and sparsely populated counties in the state — and one of the most economically challenged. Alton is the county seat. The Eleven Point River through Oregon County is one of Missouri's designated National Scenic Rivers and an outstanding float fishing stream. The county borders Arkansas and is served by some of the most rural electric cooperatives in Missouri (Missouri Rural Electric Cooperative). Oregon County is a primary target for the USDA's ReConnect rural broadband program due to extreme connectivity gaps. The county's timber economy and limited agricultural base create modest but consistent demand for basic broadband in a deeply rural Ozarks context.

restrictions.name: Calhoun County
restrictions.title: Calhoun County — Port Lavaca/Formosa Plastics/Gulf Coast chemical cluster
restrictions.description: Calhoun County on the Texas Gulf Coast contains Port Lavaca and one of the most significant chemical manufacturing concentrations in the US at Point Comfort — Formosa Plastics' Texas Operations is among the largest PVC and olefins manufacturing sites in North America. The chemical manufacturing IT stack at Formosa's Point Comfort complex — process control, safety instrumented systems, regulatory monitoring for EPA Title V and TCEQ permits — represents extraordinary industrial IT density for a Gulf Coast county of 22,000 people. CPS Energy and Texas New Mexico Power serve the area. Matagorda Bay's aquaculture and commercial fishing create additional environmental monitoring IT. Calhoun County is a compact industrial port county with technology requirements shaped entirely by its chemical manufacturing identity.

restrictions.name: Jim Wells County
restrictions.title: Jim Wells County — Alice/crossroads of South Texas/oil and gas hub
restrictions.description: Jim Wells County and Alice, Texas sit at the geographic center of South Texas — a crossroads city on US-281 and US-59 where oil field supply chains for the Eagle Ford Shale and Corpus Christi markets converge. Alice is known as the 'Hub of South Texas' and has historically been one of the most significant oil and gas supply chain cities in the US outside of the major metros. The county's oil and gas production and service industry create SCADA, production monitoring, and logistics IT. Alice Independent School District's technology deployment serves as a regional IT anchor. Corpus Christi's petrochemical market is 45 miles east. AEP Texas Central serves the county.

restrictions.name: Young County
restrictions.title: Young County — Graham/north Texas/Possum Kingdom Lake/oil and gas
restrictions.description: Young County in north-central Texas contains Graham — a significant oil field supply chain city and county seat — and Possum Kingdom Lake, one of Texas's most scenic reservoirs on the Brazos River. The county's oil and gas history dates to the late 19th century, and Young County continues to produce from mature Permian Basin extension fields. Possum Kingdom Lake's recreation economy drives second-home broadband demand. Oncor and West Texas Utilities (AEP) serve the county. Graham's Graham National Bank, Young County Independent School District, and regional healthcare center make it the commercial hub for a multi-county area. The county's Brazos River position gives it hydroelectric and water management infrastructure.

restrictions.name: Covington County
restrictions.title: Covington County — Andalusia/south Alabama commercial hub/timber/poultry
restrictions.description: Covington County in south Alabama contains Andalusia — the county seat and a regional commercial hub for the south Alabama timber, poultry, and agriculture economy. Andalusia is home to Lurleen B. Wallace Community College, providing technical workforce development for south Alabama. The county's timber economy — significant pine plantations serving the paper and building products markets — creates forestry management and supply chain IT. Alabama Power (Southern Company) serves the county. The US-29 and US-84 corridors through Andalusia connect south Alabama's timber-producing counties to the Port of Mobile market. Covington County's position as a hub for Alabama's rural south creates IT concentration proportionate to its commercial role.

restrictions.name: Monroe County
restrictions.title: Monroe County — Monroeville/Harper Lee hometown/To Kill a Mockingbird heritage
restrictions.description: Monroe County in south Alabama is the hometown of Harper Lee — author of To Kill a Mockingbird (1960), one of the most widely read American novels. Truman Capote also spent childhood summers in Monroeville, the county seat. The annual Mockingbird theatrical production draws thousands of visitors to the historic Monroe County Courthouse. Monroeville is also the county seat and commercial hub for a timber-producing south Alabama county, with Alabama Power serving the area. Coastal Alabama Community College's Monroe County campus provides technical education. Monroe County's literary heritage tourism creates hospitality IT, and its courthouse (used as a model for the novel's setting) has been a national historic landmark attracting digital archival and preservation IT.

restrictions.name: Alleghany County
restrictions.title: Alleghany County — Covington/WestRock paper mill/I-64 I-81 mountain gap
restrictions.description: Alleghany County in western Virginia contains Covington — the county seat and home of the WestRock (formerly MeadWestvaco) paper and packaging mill, one of the largest industrial employers in the Alleghany Highlands. The paper mill's power consumption creates Appalachian Power (AEP) industrial rate infrastructure in this mountain county. The Jackson River — a world-class trout fishery — runs through the county. Covington sits where I-64 (crossing the Allegheny Mountains) and US-60 converge before splitting toward I-81 north and I-77 south — making it a mountain freight waypoint. The county's Bath County Pumped Storage Station (the world's largest at completion) in adjacent Bath County creates extraordinary transmission infrastructure that extends into Alleghany County.

restrictions.name: Wythe County
restrictions.title: Wythe County — Wytheville/I-81 I-77 crossroads/lead mining heritage/Ivanhoe
restrictions.description: Wythe County contains Wytheville — the 'Crossroads of the Appalachians,' where I-81 (northeast-southwest through the Appalachian Valley) intersects I-77 (connecting Charlotte and the Carolinas to Ohio and the Midwest). This junction is one of the most significant highway intersections in the Appalachians — a major logistics and freight node with corresponding fiber infrastructure. Wytheville also sits on the Clinch Mountain/Blue Ridge divide and was historically significant as the main route through the Virginia mountains. New Peoples Bank and Wytheville Community College anchor the local economy. Lead and zinc were mined at Ivanhoe in the county, creating industrial heritage. Appalachian Power (AEP) serves the county.

restrictions.name: Nance County
restrictions.title: Nance County — Fullerton/Loup River Valley/central Nebraska agriculture
restrictions.description: Nance County in central Nebraska sits in the Loup River valley between the Platte River market (Platte County/Columbus, already in DB) and the Boone County market to the north. Fullerton is the county seat. Nebraska Public Power District serves the county. The Loup River Power Canal — a hydroelectric complex managed by Loup River Public Power District — passes through the region and provides renewable generation capacity in a plains context. Nance County's corn and soybean economy drives standard Nebraska precision agriculture IT. The county's position on US-30 (the Lincoln Highway) gives it east-west corridor access between Columbus and Grand Island markets.

restrictions.name: Franklin County
restrictions.title: Franklin County — Franklin/south-central Nebraska/Republican River Valley
restrictions.description: Franklin County in south-central Nebraska borders Kansas and sits in the Republican River watershed. Franklin is the county seat. Nebraska Public Power District and Southern Nebraska Rural Electric Association serve the county. Franklin County's agricultural economy — wheat, corn, sorghum, and cattle — creates precision agriculture IT for a traditional Great Plains farming community. The county's Republican River water rights participate in the multi-state Republican River Compact, creating water management monitoring infrastructure for the region. Franklin sits on US-136, providing east-west corridor connectivity through south-central Nebraska. The county's wind energy potential has drawn development interest from the growing Nebraska wind market.

restrictions.name: Holmes County
restrictions.title: Holmes County — Millersburg/world's largest Amish community/furniture manufacturing
restrictions.description: Holmes County in east-central Ohio is home to the world's largest Amish community — with approximately 40,000 Amish residents, the county has more Amish than any other county on earth. The Amish furniture manufacturing industry, concentrated in and around Berlin and Millersburg, has made Holmes County a significant custom furniture export economy despite limited electricity use within Amish households themselves. The wholesale distribution infrastructure supporting Amish furniture manufacturing — warehousing, export logistics, e-commerce fulfillment through non-Amish partners — creates IT demand at the county's commercial margins. Buckeye Rural Electric Cooperative serves the non-Amish portions. Holmes County's tourism from nationwide visitors to the Amish community creates hospitality IT above rural Ohio norms.

restrictions.name: Wayne County
restrictions.title: Wayne County — Wooster/OARDC/College of Wooster/Rubbermaid Ohio State Research
restrictions.description: Wayne County in northeast Ohio contains Wooster — home of the Ohio Agricultural Research and Development Center (OARDC), the world's largest single-location agricultural research station. OARDC is Ohio State University's primary agricultural research facility, with labs, greenhouses, and field stations covering virtually every aspect of Ohio agriculture. The College of Wooster, one of the most respected small liberal arts colleges in the Midwest, adds a significant research computing anchor. Wayne County was historically home to Rubbermaid (now Newell Brands), which invented the plastic houseware industry. AEP Ohio and FirstEnergy serve the county. The combination of the world's largest ag research station and a selective liberal arts college creates research IT concentration in a mid-sized Ohio county.

restrictions.name: Moody County
restrictions.title: Moody County — Flandreau/Flandreau Santee Sioux Tribe/SE South Dakota
restrictions.description: Moody County in southeastern South Dakota is home to Flandreau — the county seat and the location of the Flandreau Santee Sioux Tribe, one of South Dakota's federally recognized tribes with a long history of tribal governance and education. The Royal River Casino (Flandreau Santee Sioux Tribe's gaming operation) creates tribal IT infrastructure. Flandreau Indian School — a federally funded Indian boarding school — has provided education for Native American students since 1892 and creates federal IT. East River Electric Cooperative serves the county. Moody County's position on I-29 and US-14 gives it highway corridor fiber access between Sioux Falls (Minnehaha County, already in DB) and the Minnesota border.

restrictions.name: Hamlin County
restrictions.title: Hamlin County — Hayti/northeast South Dakota/Coteau Lakes agricultural corridor
restrictions.description: Hamlin County in northeastern South Dakota contains Hayti, the county seat, situated in the Coteau des Prairies lake district — an area of thousands of prairie pothole lakes important for waterfowl production. The county was near the area where Laura Ingalls Wilder's family settled in the 1880s (De Smet in adjacent Kingsbury County). Lake Norden in Hamlin County provides recreation. East River Electric Cooperative serves the county. Hamlin County's grain farming economy — corn, wheat, soybeans — drives precision agriculture monitoring for a productive eastern SD agricultural county. The county's position on US-14 connecting the Twin Cities to the Black Hills gives it highway corridor fiber access across northern South Dakota.

restrictions.name: Pike County
restrictions.title: Pike County KY — Pikeville/Big Sandy River/Appalachian coal capital
restrictions.description: Pike County is Kentucky's largest county by area and the center of its coal industry — Pikeville anchors the Big Sandy River valley, which generated more coal tonnage than almost any other Appalachian region. The University of Pikeville is a private liberal arts institution with medical and law schools, and Pikeville Medical Center is a regional trauma center for a multi-county Appalachian area. The Kenneth-Rayburn Memorial Bridge — one of the most distinctive mountain highway structures in Appalachia — enabled modern economic development in a city hemmed in by ridges. Pike County's energy production history and medical education infrastructure create demand for sophisticated data management in a region often overlooked in technology coverage.

restrictions.name: Breathitt County
restrictions.title: Breathitt County KY — Jackson/North Fork KY River/deep Appalachian heritage
restrictions.description: Breathitt County occupies the North Fork of the Kentucky River in the heart of the Central Appalachian coalfields. Jackson, the county seat, sits in a deep valley where ridgelines confined development. The county's Appalachian identity is pronounced — it was one of the early target communities for President Johnson's War on Poverty programs, and ARC (Appalachian Regional Commission) investments have funded broadband infrastructure upgrades across this historically underconnected corridor. Breathitt County's federal broadband designations and ARC connectivity grants have made it a recipient of substantial telecom infrastructure investment that exceeds what its small population would suggest.

restrictions.name: Elliott County
restrictions.title: Elliott County KY — Sandy Hook/remote Appalachian/federal infrastructure access
restrictions.description: Elliott County is among the smallest and most remote counties in Kentucky, situated in the Sandy Hook area of northeastern Appalachia. The county gained brief national attention in 2012 when it recorded its first-ever Republican presidential vote after voting Democratic continuously since its 1869 founding — a signal of deep demographic shifts in Appalachian Kentucky. Elliott County's participation in ARC broadband programs and federal rural utility service grants has brought fiber connectivity to communities that previously lacked reliable internet service. The county's remote character and federal infrastructure investment pattern make it representative of Appalachian broadband transition dynamics.

restrictions.name: Owsley County
restrictions.title: Owsley County KY — Booneville/historically low-income Appalachian/Red River gateway
restrictions.description: Owsley County has ranked as one of the lowest-income counties in the United States for multiple consecutive census periods — a distinction that has made it a focus of federal rural development programs, ARC investment, and state broadband equity initiatives. Booneville sits at the confluence of Appalachian ridgelines that historically isolated the county from economic development. Recent federal broadband equity access and deployment (BEAD) funding and e-Rate program investments have specifically targeted counties like Owsley where digital access gaps are most severe. The county's position in federal broadband equity reporting makes it institutionally significant for understanding rural Appalachian connectivity challenges.

restrictions.name: Clay County
restrictions.title: Clay County IA — Spencer/Iowa Great Lakes gateway/Northwest Iowa hub
restrictions.description: Clay County's seat, Spencer, is the retail and commercial hub for northwest Iowa and serves as the gateway to the Iowa Great Lakes (Okoboji/Spirit Lake) resort region to the north. The Clay County Fair in Spencer is one of the largest county fairs in the world by attendance — drawing hundreds of thousands of visitors annually — generating significant event logistics, ticketing, and communications infrastructure. Spencer is a regional medical and commercial center for an agricultural area spanning multiple counties. MidAmerican Energy's wind generation infrastructure in northwest Iowa passes through Clay County's transmission corridors.

restrictions.name: Benton County
restrictions.title: Benton County IA — Vinton/east-central Iowa agricultural and institutional hub
restrictions.description: Benton County occupies east-central Iowa between Cedar Rapids and Iowa City — two of Iowa's major metro areas — giving it access to significant fiber backbone infrastructure. Vinton is home to the Iowa Braille and Sight Saving School, a state institution dating to 1852 that provides specialized accessibility technology and serves students across Iowa. The county's agricultural base includes row crop production and livestock operations with precision agriculture technology adoption. Benton County's location in the Iowa City-Cedar Rapids metro corridor positions it within one of the most connectivity-dense regions of rural Iowa.

restrictions.name: Carroll County
restrictions.title: Carroll County IA — Carroll/west-central Iowa manufacturing and agricultural corridor
restrictions.description: Carroll is a west-central Iowa regional center with a strong manufacturing and food processing base. Iowa Premium Beef's beef processing facility represents significant food supply chain IT infrastructure. The county's Catholic institutional heritage — established by German and Irish immigrant settlers — left a distinctive imprint of parochial schools and church organizations that create community IT infrastructure. Carroll's position on US-30 (the old Lincoln Highway) and proximity to I-80 gives it transportation corridor access. Western Iowa Power Cooperative's grid infrastructure serves the county's agricultural operations.

restrictions.name: Mahaska County
restrictions.title: Mahaska County IA — Oskaloosa/William Penn University/south-central Iowa
restrictions.description: Mahaska County's seat, Oskaloosa, is home to William Penn University — a Quaker-founded liberal arts institution that brings higher education infrastructure to south-central Iowa. The county has a noted entrepreneurial tradition — it produced a disproportionate share of Iowa business founders in the 19th and early 20th centuries, and some economic historians cite it as an example of Quaker entrepreneurial culture shaping regional development. Mahaska County's coal mining history (strip mining through the mid-20th century) created industrial infrastructure that has since transitioned to agricultural services. Alliant Energy's service territory includes Mahaska County within its Iowa distribution network.

restrictions.name: Colorado County
restrictions.title: Colorado County TX — Columbus/oldest Anglo colony/Navidad River/Gulf Coast corridor
restrictions.description: Colorado County contains Columbus, one of the oldest Anglo-American settlements in Texas — part of Stephen F. Austin's original land grant colony established in the 1820s. The county courthouse in Columbus, built in 1891, is one of the most photographed courthouses in Texas. The Colorado River and Navidad River watersheds provide water resources for agricultural operations in this transitional zone between Houston's metro influence and the Gulf Coast rice and cattle belt. Colorado County's position on US-90 and I-10 (less than 90 miles from Houston) brings it within the Houston metro fiber backbone, making it one of the better-connected small counties in the Gulf Coastal Plain.

restrictions.name: Lamar County
restrictions.title: Lamar County TX — Paris/NE Texas regional center/Robert's Rules of Order origin
restrictions.description: Paris, Texas is the seat of Lamar County and the largest city in northeast Texas — a regional commercial, medical, and educational hub for a multi-county area. Henry Martyn Robert, author of Robert's Rules of Order, served as a US Army engineer in Texas and is associated with the area. Paris Junior College is a community college serving northeast Texas. The county's position near the Texas-Oklahoma border on US-82 makes it a crossroads for east-west traffic across the Red River corridor. Paris's Eiffel Tower replica (with a cowboy hat) is a distinctive landmark. Lamar County's retail medical and higher education infrastructure creates IT demand that extends across a broad rural catchment.

restrictions.name: Clay County
restrictions.title: Clay County GA — Fort Gaines/Lake Walter F. George/smallest GA county pop.
restrictions.description: Clay County is one of Georgia's smallest counties by population, situated along the Chattahoochee River and Lake Walter F. George (Lake Eufaula) on the Georgia-Alabama border. Fort Gaines was an important Civil War fortification guarding the river crossing. The Walter F. George Lock and Dam is operated by the US Army Corps of Engineers, creating federal water management infrastructure and recreational lake facilities. Clay County's participation in Georgia's rural broadband mapping exercises and federal connectivity grants reflects its status as a priority area for digital equity investment in southwest Georgia.

restrictions.name: Montgomery County
restrictions.title: Montgomery County GA — Mount Vernon/Brewton-Parker College/Oconee River
restrictions.description: Montgomery County in southeast Georgia is home to Brewton-Parker College, a small Baptist liberal arts institution founded in 1904. Mount Vernon sits along the Oconee River in a region transitioning from traditional timber and tobacco farming to poultry production and agribusiness. The county is part of Georgia's rural broadband priority area designations under the Governor's Office of Planning and Budget connectivity initiatives. Brewton-Parker College's IT infrastructure — student systems, distance education, administrative networks — provides a technology anchor for a county that would otherwise have limited institutional IT presence.

restrictions.name: Telfair County
restrictions.title: Telfair County GA — McRae-Helena/Little Ocmulgee/birthplace of two GA governors
restrictions.description: Telfair County in central Georgia has the distinction of being the birthplace of two Georgia governors — a historically notable political heritage for a small rural county. McRae-Helena (consolidated city) sits along the Little Ocmulgee River, with Little Ocmulgee State Park nearby providing recreational infrastructure. The county is part of the rural southeast Georgia corridor where Georgia Power and utility cooperatives have expanded broadband access in coordination with the state's rural development initiatives. Telfair County's agricultural base includes timber production and row crops, with food processing operations generating industrial IT requirements.

restrictions.name: Gogebic County
restrictions.title: Gogebic County MI — Ironwood/Porcupine Mountains/UP iron range legacy
restrictions.description: Gogebic County sits at the western tip of Michigan's Upper Peninsula, anchored by Ironwood — once a major iron ore shipping city at the end of the Gogebic Iron Range. Porcupine Mountains Wilderness State Park, one of the largest state parks in the eastern United States, occupies much of the county's northern shore along Lake Superior and generates significant park management IT and tourism infrastructure. The Gogebic Range produced over 300 million tons of iron ore from the 1880s through the 20th century, creating the electrical and industrial infrastructure typical of Great Lakes iron mining regions. Michigan Tech University's influence extends into Gogebic County through its UP research networks and extension programs.

restrictions.name: Iron County
restrictions.title: Iron County MI — Crystal Falls/Ottawa National Forest/UP mining to recreation
restrictions.description: Iron County in Michigan's Upper Peninsula contains Crystal Falls, a small city with significant iron mining heritage. Ottawa National Forest covers much of the county, with the US Forest Service maintaining research and administrative infrastructure. Iron County's proximity to Wisconsin's Nicolet National Forest creates a cross-state forest management coordination zone. Like neighboring Gogebic County, Iron County underwent a mining-to-recreation economic transition with significant forestry and outdoor recreation tourism driving modern connectivity needs. CenturyLink (now Lumen) and UP fiber cooperative networks have prioritized Iron County's corridor as part of UP broadband expansion.

restrictions.name: Edgefield County
restrictions.title: Edgefield County SC — Edgefield/Strom Thurmond birthplace/alkaline glaze pottery tradition
restrictions.description: Edgefield County, South Carolina has a remarkably dense political heritage — it is the birthplace of more governors and US senators than perhaps any other county of its size in American history, including Strom Thurmond and multiple antebellum governors. The county is also the origin of the Edgefield pottery tradition — alkaline-glazed stoneware produced by enslaved African-American craftsmen including Dave Drake (David Drake) in the early 19th century. Edgefield stoneware is now collected by major American folk art museums. The county's position in the I-20 corridor between Augusta, GA and Columbia, SC gives it access to the data center development activity spreading through the Augusta-Aiken area.

restrictions.name: Georgetown County
restrictions.title: Georgetown County SC — Georgetown/Waccamaw Neck/colonial rice plantation heritage
restrictions.description: Georgetown County, South Carolina was the largest rice-producing region in colonial North America — the rice plantations of the Waccamaw Neck and Pee Dee River basin made Georgetown one of the wealthiest places in the pre-Civil War South. Today, Georgetown's deepwater port handles steel imports for Nucor Steel's Georgetown facility — one of the oldest continuously operating mini-mill steel plants in the United States. Nucor Georgetown's electric arc furnace operations create industrial IT infrastructure for scrap processing, quality control, and logistics. The Waccamaw Neck (Garden City, Pawleys Island, Litchfield Beach) generates significant resort and second-home broadband demand south of Myrtle Beach.

restrictions.name: Randolph County
restrictions.title: Randolph County IN — Winchester/Indiana-Ohio border/natural gas boom legacy
restrictions.description: Randolph County sits on Indiana's eastern border with Ohio, in the heart of the historic East Central Indiana natural gas field that fueled the Indiana Gas Boom of the 1880s-1890s. Winchester is the county seat. The gas boom attracted glass manufacturing and industrial operations that left behind electrical and industrial infrastructure. Indiana University East's service area extends into Randolph County, and the county's location on US-35 connects it to the Muncie-Richmond urban corridor. Randolph County's agricultural operations include both row crops and livestock, with precision agriculture technology adoption driven by Purdue Extension programming.

restrictions.name: Carroll County
restrictions.title: Carroll County IN — Delphi/Wabash River/Potawatomi Trail of Death/Tippecanoe corridor
restrictions.description: Carroll County in north-central Indiana is home to Delphi, the county seat on the Wabash River. The county sits on the Potawatomi Trail of Death — the forced removal route of Potawatomi people from Twin Lakes (in adjacent Fulton County) to Kansas in 1838, an event that has shaped the county's heritage interpretation. The Wabash and Erie Canal ran through Carroll County, making it part of Indiana's 19th-century transportation infrastructure corridor. The county's agricultural base and proximity to Lafayette/Purdue (30 miles south) creates connectivity to Purdue's agricultural research extension. REMC (Rural Electric Member Corporation) cooperatives provide electrical infrastructure across Carroll County's agricultural landscape.

restrictions.name: Cocke County
restrictions.title: Cocke County TN — Newport/Great Smoky Mountains gateway/Pigeon River
restrictions.description: Cocke County occupies the Pigeon River valley in East Tennessee, serving as one of the gateway corridors to Great Smoky Mountains National Park from the north. Newport sits at the confluence of the Pigeon and Nolichucky rivers. The county has a notably complex Appalachian heritage — it was a significant moonshine production area during Prohibition and beyond, and more recently has seen dramatic economic transition through tourism serving the national park corridor. TVA's Waterville Hydroelectric Plant on the Pigeon River provides power infrastructure, and the river's flow data management connects to TVA's basin-wide water management computing. Cocke County's tourism connectivity needs have driven broadband investment along the national park approach corridors.

restrictions.name: Alfalfa County
restrictions.title: Alfalfa County OK — Cherokee/Salt Plains/NW Oklahoma wheat belt
restrictions.description: Alfalfa County in northwest Oklahoma surrounds Cherokee, the county seat, and the Great Salt Plains State Park and reservoir — a unique geological feature where visitors can dig selenite crystals and where selenite hourglass inclusions are found exclusively in the world. The Great Salt Plains are managed by the US Army Corps of Engineers and generate federal water management IT. Alfalfa County's wheat production and salt mining heritage create agricultural and mineral extraction IT. The county's proximity to the Oklahoma Panhandle's wind corridor means Southwestern Public Service and OG&E transmission infrastructure crosses the county.

restrictions.name: Coal County
restrictions.title: Coal County OK — Coalgate/Arbuckle Mountains south/coal heritage SE Oklahoma
restrictions.description: Coal County in southeastern Oklahoma derives its name from the coal mining industry that defined the region in the late 19th and early 20th centuries. Coalgate is the county seat. The county lies at the southern edge of the Arbuckle Mountains — a geologically ancient mountain range with exposed Cambrian-Ordovician rock. Coal County sits in the Oklahoma Choctaw Nation territory, with tribal governance IT infrastructure overlapping county and municipal systems. The McAlester Army Ammunition Plant (in adjacent Pittsburg County) has security and logistics spillover into Coal County's transportation corridors.

restrictions.name: Garvin County
restrictions.title: Garvin County OK — Pauls Valley/Washita River/I-35 mid-Oklahoma corridor
restrictions.description: Garvin County's county seat of Pauls Valley sits on the Washita River along I-35, the central Oklahoma north-south corridor connecting Oklahoma City to the Texas border. The county has oil and gas production history in the Ardmore Basin extension. Pauls Valley is noted for its toy museum and connections to the early-20th-century Chickasaw Nation territory. I-35 logistics infrastructure, freight management, and truck stop technology create a consistent technology demand in Garvin County that exceeds what its small population would suggest. OG&E (Oklahoma Gas and Electric) serves the county with transmission infrastructure from the Oklahoma City power market.

restrictions.name: Greer County
restrictions.title: Greer County OK — Mangum/SW Oklahoma/disputed Texas annexation history/Quartz Mountain
restrictions.description: Greer County has a remarkable legal history — it was claimed by Texas after the Civil War and remained in dispute until the US Supreme Court awarded it to Oklahoma Territory in 1896. Mangum is the county seat. Quartz Mountain State Park and Arts and Conference Center is one of Oklahoma's premier arts education facilities, hosting summer arts institutes. The county's remote location in the Wichita Mountains foothills area has made it a priority for rural broadband programs under USDA ReConnect and Oklahoma's rural utility service. Cotton, wheat, and cattle ranching drive agricultural IT, while wind energy from the Western Farmers Electric Cooperative's wind portfolio adds renewable energy grid management.

restrictions.name: Duchesne County
restrictions.title: Duchesne County UT — Duchesne/Uinta Basin oil/Roosevelt/Ute tribal lands
restrictions.description: Duchesne County occupies the heart of the Uinta Basin — Utah's primary oil and natural gas producing region — and contains Roosevelt, the largest city in the basin. The Ute Indian Tribe of the Uintah and Ouray Reservation governs significant territory within and adjacent to Duchesne County, and tribal oil royalties have funded substantial Ute tribal IT and administrative infrastructure. The Uinta Basin's oil production generates industrial SCADA and process control IT, while natural gas processing plants require continuous monitoring systems. Rocky Mountain Power's transmission infrastructure connects the basin to the Wasatch Front grid through challenging mountain terrain.

restrictions.name: Millard County
restrictions.title: Millard County UT — Delta/Topaz WWII incarceration camp/Sevier Desert/US-6 corridor
restrictions.description: Millard County is Utah's largest county by area — a vast desert county dominated by the Sevier Desert and Great Basin landscape. Delta is the county seat, and Topaz Mountain (Topaz War Relocation Center) near Delta was one of the ten major WWII Japanese-American incarceration camps, holding over 11,000 people at peak. The Topaz Museum in Delta preserves this history. Millard County's extensive territory along US-6 (connecting Salt Lake City to Las Vegas) creates a significant corridor for fiber backbone infrastructure. Rocky Mountain Power's Central Utah Generating Project steam plant has historically been a major power producer in the county.

restrictions.name: Piute County
restrictions.title: Piute County UT — Junction/Paiute heritage/Sevier River/one of smallest US county populations
restrictions.description: Piute County is among the least populous counties in the United States — with fewer than 2,000 residents — and is named for the Southern Paiute people who have inhabited the Colorado Plateau for centuries. Junction, the county seat, sits in the Sevier River valley amid red rock canyon country. Despite its tiny population, Piute County appears in federal broadband gap analyses and tribal connectivity programs because of its intersection with Paiute Band territories and the federal government's trust responsibility. The county's remote desert landscape and participation in Utah's rural broadband mapping initiatives make it a meaningful data point for understanding Utah's connectivity frontier.

restrictions.name: Benton County
restrictions.title: Benton County MN — Foley/St. Cloud metro corridor/St. Cloud State University adjacent
restrictions.description: Benton County sits immediately east of St. Cloud — Minnesota's largest city north of the Twin Cities metro — giving it access to the significant fiber and telecommunications infrastructure built around St. Cloud State University and the broader St. Cloud regional economy. Foley is the county seat, but the county's population centers on the St. Cloud fringe. St. Cloud has attracted data center interest given its cold climate, power infrastructure, and position between the Twin Cities and the Canadian border. Benton County's inclusion in the St. Cloud MSA means its broadband access substantially exceeds comparable rural Minnesota counties.

restrictions.name: Lake County
restrictions.title: Lake County MN — Two Harbors/Lake Superior North Shore/Boundary Waters gateway
restrictions.description: Lake County stretches along Minnesota's Lake Superior North Shore from Two Harbors to the Canadian border, encompassing the eastern end of the Iron Range and the Superior National Forest's Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness gateway. Two Harbors is one of the world's largest iron ore shipping ports — the DM&IR (Duluth, Missabe and Iron Range Railway) historically delivered taconite from the Mesabi Range to Two Harbors' ore docks. The Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness is one of the most visited wilderness areas in the US, generating park management IT and outfitter logistics technology. Lake Superior's fiber backbone (Lake Superior undersea/shoreline cable systems) provides connectivity infrastructure for this remote but institutionally significant county.

restrictions.name: Cottonwood County
restrictions.title: Cottonwood County MN — Windom/SW Minnesota wind corridor/Lake Shetek
restrictions.description: Cottonwood County in southwestern Minnesota sits in one of the most productive wind energy corridors in the United States — the Buffalo Ridge and its southward extension generates substantial wind farm capacity across Cottonwood and adjacent counties.
