restrictions.name: Tuscarawas County
restrictions.title: Tuscarawas County OH — New Philadelphia/Zoar/Ohio & Erie Canal/largest Amish region proximity
restrictions.description: Tuscarawas County in east-central Ohio sits at the heart of the Ohio & Erie Canal corridor — the historic waterway that connected Cleveland to Portsmouth and catalyzed Ohio's industrial development in the 1820s-1840s. New Philadelphia is the county seat. Zoar Village, founded in 1817 by German separatist immigrants who maintained a communal economy until 1898, is a state historic site and one of Ohio's most significant communal heritage communities. The county borders Holmes County (world's largest Amish community) and contains a significant Amish presence of its own. Tuscarawas County's clay and pottery industry (Roseville Pottery heritage) and its Ohio & Erie Canal National Heritage Corridor status add to its institutional technology profile.

restrictions.name: Dickinson County
restrictions.title: Dickinson County KS — Abilene/Eisenhower birthplace/Chisholm Trail/I-70 corridor
restrictions.description: Dickinson County in central Kansas is home to Abilene — the birthplace and final resting place of Dwight D. Eisenhower, 34th President and Supreme Allied Commander in World War II. The Eisenhower Presidential Library, Museum, and Boyhood Home in Abilene is one of Kansas's most significant federal heritage institutions, with archival and visitor management IT. Abilene was the northern terminus of the Chisholm Trail — the 19th-century cattle drive route that brought Texas longhorns to Kansas railheads. The Smoky Hill River valley and I-70 corridor create a logistics and agricultural processing presence. Dickinson County's position as a heritage tourism destination and I-70 commercial hub gives it infrastructure above what its population would suggest.

restrictions.name: Kearny County
restrictions.title: Kearny County KS — Lakin/Arkansas River/High Plains irrigation/Santa Fe Trail
restrictions.description: Kearny County in far southwest Kansas occupies the Arkansas River valley — one of the most intensively irrigated agricultural regions in the United States. Lakin is the county seat. The county's High Plains Aquifer irrigation system (drawing from the Ogallala Aquifer) sustains corn, sorghum, and alfalfa production that feeds the feedlot industry of nearby Finney County (Garden City). USGS aquifer monitoring systems in southwest Kansas track the Ogallala's depletion — one of the most consequential water management crises in American agriculture. The Santa Fe Trail passed through Kearny County on its way to New Mexico, and the region's freight corridor role continues with US-50 commerce.

restrictions.name: Greenwood County
restrictions.title: Greenwood County KS — Eureka/Flint Hills Tallgrass Prairie/eastern Kansas ranching
restrictions.description: Greenwood County in eastern Kansas sits at the heart of the Flint Hills — the largest remaining expanse of tallgrass prairie in North America, preserved by the flint-rich limestone bedrock that resisted plowing. Eureka is the county seat. The Flint Hills host the Flint Hills National Scenic Byway and the Tallgrass Prairie National Preserve (in adjacent Chase County). The region's cattle ranching economy and seasonal burning of prairie for pasture management create agricultural and environmental monitoring IT. Kansas's rural connectivity programs have prioritized Flint Hills counties for broadband investment under the state's broadband initiative. The Verdigris River headwaters in Greenwood County connect to the broader Kansas watershed system.

restrictions.name: Bourbon County
restrictions.title: Bourbon County KS — Fort Scott/Battle of Mine Creek/Missouri border/Osage Cession
restrictions.description: Bourbon County in southeast Kansas borders Missouri and contains Fort Scott National Historic Site — a pre-Civil War military fort that became the center of violent conflict between pro-slavery and anti-slavery forces (Bleeding Kansas) in the 1850s. The Battle of Mine Creek (1864) — fought in adjacent Linn County — was one of the largest cavalry battles of the Civil War and resulted in a Union victory that secured southeastern Kansas. Fort Scott is now a mid-sized county seat with healthcare anchored by Ascension Via Christi. Bourbon County's position on the Kansas-Missouri border with rail and highway connections makes it part of the Kansas City metropolitan trade corridor extending into rural southeast Kansas.

restrictions.name: Shackelford County
restrictions.title: Shackelford County TX — Albany/Clear Fork Brazos/Old Jail Art Center/west Texas oil
restrictions.description: Shackelford County in west Texas centers on Albany — a small oil town on the Clear Fork of the Brazos River that has developed an unlikely national reputation as an arts community. The Old Jail Art Center in Albany houses a significant collection of pre-Columbian, Asian, and European art collected by county ranchers — one of the most distinctive rural art museums in America. Fort Griffin State Historic Site preserves the ruins of an 1867 frontier fort that guarded the Butterfield Overland Mail route and later the Western Cattle Trail. Shackelford County has oil and gas production in the North Texas basin. The county's cattle ranching and oil heritage create a distinctive west Texas combination of legacy extractive industry and cultural capital.

restrictions.name: Knox County
restrictions.title: Knox County TX — Benjamin/north Texas/Wichita Falls adjacent/Red River headwaters/ranching
restrictions.description: Knox County in north Texas is a sparsely populated agricultural county on the Brazos River headwaters — ranching and dryland farming country between Wichita Falls and Abilene. Benjamin is the county seat. The county's position in the Rolling Plains of North Texas gives it the characteristic landscape of mesquite grassland and red clay soils that define the southern Great Plains transition zone. Knox County benefits from the infrastructure of the Wichita Falls metro area (roughly 40 miles north) for connectivity and services, but remains in a rural broadband gap area prioritized by Texas BEAD deployment programs. The county's agricultural economy includes cattle ranching and wheat production.

restrictions.name: Haskell County
restrictions.title: Haskell County TX — Haskell/west Texas/Double Mountain Fork Brazos/dryland farming
restrictions.description: Haskell County in west Texas centers on Haskell, a small county seat in the Double Mountain Fork of the Brazos River valley. The county's dryland farming (cotton, wheat, sorghum) and cattle ranching economy is characteristic of the Rolling Plains. Haskell County's agricultural operations rely on the Texas Department of Agriculture's Rural Economic Development infrastructure, and the county is within the USDA ReConnect priority zone for broadband expansion. The area's historical oil and gas production in the North Texas basin provides some energy sector IT. Haskell County sits between the Abilene and Wichita Falls metro areas, accessing regional services from both directions.

restrictions.name: Kent County
restrictions.title: Kent County TX — Jayton/Double Mountain Fork Brazos/sparsely populated west Texas
restrictions.description: Kent County is one of the most sparsely populated counties in Texas — Jayton (the county seat) has a population of under 500. The county's vast, treeless plains country on the Double Mountain Fork of the Brazos River supports cattle ranching and limited dryland farming. Kent County's extreme remoteness makes it one of the most challenging broadband connectivity cases in Texas — the county is among the highest-priority rural areas in the Texas BEAD broadband deployment plan. The Stonewall County State Park (adjacent) and the region's raw West Texas landscape contribute to limited recreational and agricultural IT. Kent County represents the kind of persistent connectivity gap that broadband policy addresses at the frontier of feasibility.

restrictions.name: Baxter County
restrictions.title: Baxter County AR — Mountain Home/Bull Shoals Lake/Norfork Lake/Ozark retirement
restrictions.description: Baxter County in north-central Arkansas centers on Mountain Home — one of the most popular retirement destinations in the Ozarks, anchored by the immense recreational lakes formed by two Army Corps of Engineers dams: Bull Shoals Lake (on the White River) and Norfork Lake (on the North Fork of the White River). The county's retiree demographic creates significant healthcare IT demand, anchored by Baxter Regional Medical Center. The White River trout fishery below Bull Shoals Dam is one of the most productive tailwater trout fisheries in the country, supporting a fly fishing tourism economy. Arkansas's rural broadband initiative has prioritized Mountain Home as a regional connectivity hub for the north-central Ozarks.

restrictions.name: Logan County
restrictions.title: Logan County AR — Paris/Mt. Magazine highest AR point/coal mining heritage/Arkansas River Valley
restrictions.description: Logan County in west-central Arkansas sits in the Arkansas River Valley between the Ozark and Ouachita Mountains. Paris is the county seat. Mount Magazine — the highest point in Arkansas at 2,753 feet — is in Logan County, managed as Mount Magazine State Park with lodge and recreation IT. The county has a coal mining heritage from the Sebastian and Logan County coalfields that operated from the 1880s into the 20th century. The Arkansas River and its agricultural bottomlands support row crop farming, while the surrounding Boston Mountains terrain creates a landscape more typical of the Appalachian-like highlands than the flat Delta to the east. Logan County's rural broadband program participation reflects the challenges of serving mountain communities.

restrictions.name: Ashley County
restrictions.title: Ashley County AR — Hamburg/south Arkansas timber/Felsenthal NWR/bayou country
restrictions.description: Ashley County in south Arkansas centers on Hamburg, a timber and agricultural county seat in the bayou country between the Saline and Ouachita Rivers. The county's pine timber industry (historically significant in south Arkansas) creates forestry management IT and wood products supply chain computing. Felsenthal National Wildlife Refuge — one of the largest bottomland hardwood wetland refuges in the US — borders the county on the Ouachita River, with USFWS management and wildlife monitoring IT. Ashley County's position in Arkansas's south timber belt gives it institutional connections to the forest products industry that define much of south Arkansas's economic identity. The county's agricultural production includes soybeans, cotton, and rice typical of the Gulf Coastal Plain transition.

restrictions.name: Augusta County
restrictions.title: Augusta County VA — Staunton/Shenandoah Valley/Woodrow Wilson birthplace/I-81 corridor
restrictions.description: Augusta County in the Shenandoah Valley surrounds Staunton — the birthplace of Woodrow Wilson (28th President) and home to the Woodrow Wilson Presidential Library. Staunton is also home to Mary Baldwin University (one of Virginia's oldest women's colleges, now co-educational) and the American Shakespeare Center's Blackfriars Playhouse — the world's only re-creation of Shakespeare's indoor theater. Augusta County's I-81 corridor position makes it part of Virginia's major freight and data infrastructure axis connecting Northern Virginia to the Tennessee border. The county's agricultural base in the Shenandoah Valley (poultry, beef, apple orchards) adds agricultural supply chain IT.

restrictions.name: Botetourt County
restrictions.title: Botetourt County VA — Fincastle/Blue Ridge/I-81 Roanoke metro adjacent/Appalachian Trail
restrictions.description: Botetourt County in western Virginia borders the Roanoke metropolitan area and contains significant Blue Ridge Mountain terrain. Fincastle is the county seat — a small historic town in the Shenandoah Valley at the southern end. The Appalachian Trail crosses the Blue Ridge Mountains through Botetourt County, generating trail management and recreation IT. The James River — one of Virginia's most historically significant rivers — heads nearby. Botetourt County's position between the Roanoke metro and the rural Shenandoah Valley creates an exurban growth zone. Dominion Energy's western Virginia transmission infrastructure and the I-81/I-64 interchange area near Lexington create utility and freight corridor IT.

restrictions.name: Buchanan County
restrictions.title: Buchanan County VA — Grundy/far SW Virginia/Breaks Interstate Park/Appalachian coal
restrictions.description: Buchanan County in far southwest Virginia is one of Virginia's most remote and mountainous counties — a coal-producing Appalachian county on the Kentucky border. Grundy is the county seat, on the Levisa Fork of the Big Sandy River. Breaks Interstate Park — straddling the Virginia-Kentucky border — contains the 'Grand Canyon of the South,' the deepest canyon east of the Mississippi. Buchanan County's coal production (Virginia coalfields extend through this region) has substantially declined, and the county faces significant economic transition challenges addressed by ARC and Virginia coalfield economic development programs. The county's extreme terrain and isolation create persistent broadband challenges addressed by Virginia's BEAD deployment.

restrictions.name: Cherokee County
restrictions.title: Cherokee County OK — Tahlequah/Cherokee Nation capital/Northeastern State University/Illinois River
restrictions.description: Cherokee County in northeast Oklahoma is the governmental and cultural capital of the Cherokee Nation — the most populous federally recognized tribe in the United States. Tahlequah serves as the Cherokee Nation capital, with tribal government IT serving over 400,000 tribal citizens. The Cherokee Nation operates significant economic enterprises including Cherokee Nation Entertainment (casino and hospitality IT) and tribal health, education, and social services delivery systems. Northeastern State University in Tahlequah is a regional university with significant Cherokee Nation student enrollment and partnership programs. The Illinois River — one of Oklahoma's most popular float rivers — runs through the county, generating tourism and recreation IT.

restrictions.name: Cimarron County
restrictions.title: Cimarron County OK — Boise City/Oklahoma Panhandle/Dust Bowl epicenter/Black Mesa highest OK
restrictions.description: Cimarron County occupies the western Oklahoma Panhandle — one of the most remote and isolated counties in the contiguous United States. Boise City is the county seat. Black Mesa — the highest point in Oklahoma at 4,974 feet — is in Cimarron County, atop the Raton Plateau at the corner where Oklahoma, New Mexico, and Colorado meet. Cimarron County was one of the Dust Bowl's hardest-hit areas — John Steinbeck's 'The Grapes of Wrath' documented the mass exodus of displaced Oklahomans from exactly this region. The county still has among the lowest population densities east of the Rockies. Natural gas production and cattle ranching continue, with USDA ReConnect broadband programs targeting the extreme rural connectivity gap.

restrictions.name: Creek County
restrictions.title: Creek County OK — Sapulpa/Tulsa metro west/Muscogee (Creek) Nation/petroleum heritage
restrictions.description: Creek County in northeast Oklahoma borders the Tulsa metropolitan area and is historically one of the most petroleum-rich counties in the nation — the Glenn Pool oil field discovery in 1905 (in adjacent Tulsa County but with Creek County production) launched Oklahoma's oil boom. Sapulpa is the county seat. Creek County sits within the Muscogee (Creek) Nation reservation territory affirmed in the 2020 US Supreme Court decision McGirt v. Oklahoma, which found that the Creek Nation's reservation was never disestablished — a landmark ruling affecting criminal jurisdiction over roughly half of Oklahoma's land area. The county's Tulsa metro adjacency creates suburban and exurban connectivity well above rural Oklahoma averages.

restrictions.name: McDowell County
restrictions.title: McDowell County WV — Welch/far SW WV/Hatfield-McCoy Trail/coal history/extreme depopulation
restrictions.description: McDowell County in the far southwestern corner of West Virginia is one of the most economically distressed counties in the United States — its population peaked at over 100,000 in 1950 at the height of coal production and has declined to under 19,000 today. Welch is the county seat. The Tug Fork of the Big Sandy River (the county's western border with Kentucky) was the center of the Hatfield-McCoy feud in the 1880s-1890s. McDowell County coal production fueled America's industrial expansion — and its collapse left behind one of the most extreme examples of economic transition in Appalachia. ARC, USDA, and federal broadband programs target McDowell County among the highest-priority economic development needs in the nation.

restrictions.name: Mercer County
restrictions.title: Mercer County WV — Princeton/southern WV/US 460 Coalfields Expressway/Bluefield adjacent
restrictions.description: Mercer County in southern West Virginia is centered on Princeton — a commercial hub for the southern coalfields. The US Route 460 Coalfields Expressway corridor connects Mercer County to the Virginia border and to the Beckley metro. Mercer County's position astride I-77 (the West Virginia Turnpike/interstate connecting Charleston to the Virginia border) gives it highway connectivity superior to most of southern West Virginia. Bluefield, straddling the WV-VA border in adjacent Tazewell County VA, is the regional economic center for the broader area. Mercer County's healthcare (Princeton Community Hospital) and retail functions serve a multi-county region of southern West Virginia.

restrictions.name: Grant County
restrictions.title: Grant County WV — Petersburg/Seneca Rocks/Spruce Knob highest WV point/Monongahela NF
restrictions.description: Grant County in eastern West Virginia contains two of the state's most iconic natural landmarks: Seneca Rocks — a spectacular quartzite fin rising 900 feet above the North Fork of the South Branch of the Potomac River, one of the most technical climbing destinations in the eastern US — and Spruce Knob at 4,863 feet, the highest point in West Virginia. The Monongahela National Forest covers much of Grant County, with USFS management IT across wilderness and recreation areas. Petersburg is the county seat, on the South Branch of the Potomac River. Grant County's position in the headwaters of the Potomac watershed creates water resource management IT for one of the nation's most important rivers. The area's ski and outdoor recreation economy generates tourism IT.

restrictions.name: Ballard County
restrictions.title: Ballard County KY — Wickliffe/Mississippi-Ohio confluence/Mound Builder site/far western KY
restrictions.description: Ballard County occupies the far western tip of Kentucky, where the Ohio River meets the Mississippi — one of the most historically significant river confluences in North America, at the heart of the Native American mound-building cultures of the Mississippi period. Wickliffe is the county seat and home to the Wickliffe Mounds State Historic Site — a Middle Mississippian culture village site occupied roughly 1100-1350 CE. The county's position on the Ohio and Mississippi Rivers, near the Kentucky-Missouri-Illinois tri-state junction, creates river commerce and navigation IT. Kentucky's Purchase Area region (the Jackson Purchase) of which Ballard County is part was the last portion of Kentucky acquired from the Chickasaw Nation in 1818.

restrictions.name: Breckinridge County
restrictions.title: Breckinridge County KY — Hardinsburg/Ohio River/Lincoln Homestead adjacent/Rough Creek
restrictions.description: Breckinridge County in western Kentucky sits on the Ohio River south of Owensboro, in the region where Abraham Lincoln's parents lived before moving to Indiana — the Lincoln family farm in LaRue County (to the east) reflects Lincoln's deep Kentucky roots that Breckinridge County shares through its similar Knobs and Ohio River country heritage. Hardinsburg is the county seat. The Rough Creek State Resort Park in the county provides lodge and conference IT. Breckinridge County has a modest Ohio River economy with agriculture (corn, soybeans, tobacco), and its position on the Western Kentucky Parkway corridor gives it highway connectivity. Kentucky's rural broadband programs have included Breckinridge County in western Kentucky deployment zones.

restrictions.name: Caldwell County
restrictions.title: Caldwell County KY — Princeton/Western KY coal/dark-fired tobacco/I-24 corridor
restrictions.description: Caldwell County in western Kentucky centers on Princeton — the county seat in the heart of the western Kentucky coal and dark-fired tobacco region. The county is within the Western Kentucky coalfields, with longwall mining operations that produce thermal coal for power generation. Dark-fired tobacco (used for chewing tobacco, pipe tobacco, and snuff) is a specialty crop of the Black Patch region that includes Caldwell County — the night rider violence of the Black Patch Tobacco Wars (1906-1909) was aimed at breaking up the American Tobacco Company's monopoly over dark tobacco pricing. The I-24 corridor gives Caldwell County access to regional markets between Nashville and Paducah. Kentucky Utilities serves the county's power needs with coal-fired generation.

restrictions.name: Carter County
restrictions.title: Buchanan County KY — Grayson/Carter Caves/Ashland metro adjacent/Appalachian foothills
restrictions.description: Carter County in northeastern Kentucky sits in the foothills of the Cumberland Mountains, adjacent to the Ashland-Huntington metropolitan area. Grayson is the county seat. Carter Caves State Resort Park — one of Kentucky's most distinctive parks, with a cave complex accessible for guided tours — provides tourism and hospitality IT. The county's position near the Kentucky-West Virginia-Ohio tri-state area gives it access to the Huntington-Ashland metro's infrastructure. Carter County's iron furnace heritage (the region had numerous 19th-century blast furnaces using local iron ore and charcoal) reflects the pre-coal industrial history of eastern Kentucky. AEP transmission infrastructure serves northeastern Kentucky through the Ashland corridor.

restrictions.name: Alger County
restrictions.title: Alger County MI — Munising/Pictured Rocks National Lakeshore/Lake Superior/Hiawatha NF
restrictions.description: Alger County in the Upper Peninsula of Michigan contains Pictured Rocks National Lakeshore — the first national lakeshore in the United States (established 1966), featuring multicolored sandstone cliffs rising up to 200 feet above Lake Superior and stretching 15 miles along the shoreline. Munising is the county seat and the gateway to Pictured Rocks. The NPS visitor management, boat tour operations, and winter ice cave access IT serve one of the Great Lakes region's most photographed natural wonders. Hiawatha National Forest covers much of Alger County, with USFS management computing for timber, recreation, and wilderness management. The county's proximity to Marquette (40 miles east) gives it connection to the UP's largest city.

restrictions.name: Alpena County
restrictions.title: Alpena County MI — Alpena/Thunder Bay NMS/cement capital/NE lower Michigan
restrictions.description: Alpena County on the northeastern shore of Michigan's Lower Peninsula is home to Alpena — the only city of any size on the Lake Huron shore north of Bay City. Thunder Bay National Marine Sanctuary, centered on Alpena, preserves one of the most significant freshwater shipwreck concentrations in the world — over 200 wrecks from the lake's history as a major shipping corridor. Alpena's cement industry (dating to limestone quarrying begun in the 1900s) makes the city one of the largest cement-producing cities in the US. Alpena Community College's Great Lakes Maritime Center provides marine education IT. The county's remote position on the Lake Huron shore makes broadband connectivity a persistent challenge for residential and commercial users.

restrictions.name: Charlevoix County
restrictions.title: Charlevoix County MI — Charlevoix/Beaver Island/Lake Michigan/resort and tourism
restrictions.description: Charlevoix County on Lake Michigan includes Charlevoix (a resort city connecting Lake Michigan, Lake Charlevoix, and Round Lake) and Beaver Island — the most remote inhabited island in the Great Lakes, accessible only by ferry or small aircraft. Beaver Island was the 1850s home of James Jesse Strang, a Mormon schismatic who declared himself king of the island — his brief 'Kingdom of St. James' ended with his assassination in 1856. Today Beaver Island's year-round population of about 500 requires island-specific telecom (satellite internet, Charlevoix County's wireless mesh), ferry logistics IT, and emergency services coordination. Charlevoix County's resort economy and Lake Michigan position create tourism hospitality IT throughout the county.

restrictions.name: Cherokee County
restrictions.title: Cherokee County SC — Gaffney/Peachoid water tower/BMW proximity/Limestone University
restrictions.description: Cherokee County in the upstate of South Carolina centers on Gaffney — home to the Peachoid water tower, a 135-foot peach-shaped water storage tank that has become one of I-85's most recognized landmarks and a beloved regional icon. The county's position on I-85 places it within the upstate South Carolina industrial corridor that has attracted BMW's Spartanburg manufacturing complex (25 miles south) and the auto supplier ecosystem. Limestone University in Gaffney provides higher education and workforce development IT. Cherokee County's Broad River corridor and the Revolutionary War Battle of Cowpens (fought in adjacent Cherokee County territory) add historical significance. The county participates in South Carolina's I-85 tech and manufacturing corridor development programs.

restrictions.name: Clarendon County
restrictions.title: Clarendon County SC — Manning/Santee NWR/Lake Marion/Briggs v. Elliott civil rights
restrictions.description: Clarendon County in the South Carolina Lowcountry is the site of Briggs v. Elliott (1951) — one of the five cases consolidated into Brown v. Board of Education, the first of the Brown cases to be filed. The Clarendon County school system's refusal to equalize educational opportunities for Black students sparked a legal challenge that became foundational to school desegregation law. Santee National Wildlife Refuge borders the county on Lake Marion (one of the Santee-Cooper lakes created by the South Carolina Public Service Authority's 1940s hydroelectric project). Manning is the county seat. The county's agricultural base includes tobacco, cotton, and poultry, with the Santee-Cooper power project IT (one of the South's significant rural electrification achievements) providing regional context.

restrictions.name: Dillon County
restrictions.title: Dillon County SC — Dillon/South of the Border/I-95 corridor/Pee Dee tobacco
restrictions.description: Dillon County in northeastern South Carolina is known for South of the Border — the kitschy roadside attraction at the I-95/US-301 junction that has been a landmark for mid-Atlantic travelers since the 1950s, with its distinctive Pedro mascot and dozens of billboards on I-95 counting down the miles. The county's tobacco heritage (historically among South Carolina's most significant tobacco-producing counties) has defined its agricultural economy for generations. Dillon is the county seat, served by a historically significant school district (Dillon County Schools) that was part of desegregation litigation. The I-95 corridor through Dillon County creates freight and logistics computing activity for this high-traffic East Coast route.

restrictions.name: Bulloch County
restrictions.title: Bulloch County GA — Statesboro/Georgia Southern University/SE Georgia agricultural hub
restrictions.description: Bulloch County in southeast Georgia centers on Statesboro — home to Georgia Southern University, a large regional university with over 26,000 students that serves as the economic and educational anchor of southeast Georgia. Georgia Southern's technology programs, aviation program (one of the Southeast's leading commercial flight programs), and research IT create a significant university computing environment in a rural county. Statesboro is also the economic hub for the Coastal Plain agricultural zone — with poultry processing, vegetable farming, and food supply chain IT. The county's proximity to Savannah (65 miles southeast) positions it within the Savannah port logistics corridor.

restrictions.name: Baldwin County
restrictions.title: Baldwin County GA — Milledgeville/Georgia College/antebellum state capital/Central State Hospital
restrictions.description: Baldwin County in central Georgia is home to Milledgeville — Georgia's capital from 1804 to 1868, with a remarkably preserved antebellum downtown including the Old State Capitol (a Gothic Revival building now part of Georgia Military College). Georgia College & State University in Milledgeville is one of Georgia's public liberal arts universities. Central State Hospital — once one of the largest psychiatric hospitals in the world, with a peak population of 13,000 patients — is on the outskirts of Milledgeville; its closure and transformation to the Georgia's state government campus represents a major healthcare facility IT transition. The Oconee River runs through Baldwin County providing water resources for the region.

restrictions.name: Brantley County
restrictions.title: Brantley County GA — Nahunta/SE Georgia/Okefenokee adjacent/timber/blueberries
restrictions.description: Brantley County in extreme southeast Georgia borders the Okefenokee Swamp and sits in one of Georgia's most sparsely populated coastal plain timber regions. Nahunta is the county seat. The county's longleaf pine and mixed pine-hardwood forests create active timber management IT (Rayonier and other timber REITs manage large tracts in this region). Blueberry farming has become a significant agricultural sector in the southeast Georgia coastal plain, and Brantley County's sandy soils are well-suited to blueberry production — one of Georgia's fastest-growing agricultural export crops. The county's position near the Okefenokee NWR creates USFWS ecological monitoring computing adjacent to this enormous peat-filled wilderness wetland.

restrictions.name: Cameron Parish
restrictions.title: Cameron Parish LA — Cameron/Louisiana Gulf coast/Hurricane Katrina Rita Ike impacts/LNG
restrictions.description: Cameron Parish is Louisiana's westernmost and largest coastal parish — a low-lying Gulf Coast county that has been devastated by hurricanes repeatedly: Rita (2005), Ike (2008), Laura (2020), and Delta (2020) destroyed communities and infrastructure multiple times. Cameron, the parish seat, was nearly completely destroyed by Hurricane Rita, which struck as a Category 5 storm. The parish's Sabine National Wildlife Refuge protects coastal wetlands important for migratory birds. The Sabine Pass LNG terminal (on the Texas-Louisiana border adjacent to Cameron Parish) is one of the most significant liquefied natural gas export facilities in North America, with SCADA and safety monitoring IT for the enormous petrochemical operations. The parish's coastal position makes it critical for Gulf of Mexico storm surge monitoring.

restrictions.name: Acadia Parish
restrictions.title: Acadia Parish LA — Crowley/Rice Capital of America/Cajun prairie/Evangeline corridor
restrictions.description: Acadia Parish in south-central Louisiana centers on Crowley — the self-proclaimed 'Rice Capital of America,' where the Cajun prairie's conversion to rice farming in the late 19th century created one of the most significant rice-producing regions in the US. The international Rice Festival in Crowley (held annually since 1937) celebrates a crop that defines the cultural and agricultural identity of the region. Louisiana's rice industry relies on USDA commodity programs, rice quality management IT, and irrigation infrastructure linked to the Mermentau River basin's water management systems. The Cajun cultural landscape — French-speaking Catholic communities, zydeco music, crawfish farming in the rice paddies — is centered in Acadia Parish and surrounding parishes.

restrictions.name: Claiborne Parish
restrictions.title: Claiborne Parish LA — Homer/north Louisiana/ArkLaTex oil/Louisiana Natural Gas Storage
restrictions.description: Claiborne Parish in north Louisiana centers on Homer — a historic courthouse-square town in the north Louisiana oil country. The parish is in the Haynesville Shale play — one of the most significant natural gas producing formations in the United States, extending from northwest Louisiana into east Texas and southwest Arkansas. Claiborne Parish has significant natural gas storage infrastructure (the Bistineau storage field and other underground storage caverns) that serves as a key node in the midcontinent gas distribution network. The Homer Pipeline — a major natural gas transmission line — crosses the parish. Claiborne Parish's ArkLaTex regional position connects it to the Shreveport-Texarkana energy infrastructure corridor.

restrictions.name: Cambria County
restrictions.title: Cambria County PA — Johnstown/1889 flood/Bethlehem Steel/western PA Alleghenies
restrictions.description: Cambria County in western Pennsylvania is home to Johnstown — site of the 1889 Johnstown Flood, one of the most devastating civilian disasters in American history: when the South Fork Dam failed, a wall of water killed over 2,200 people and destroyed the city. The Johnstown Flood National Memorial preserves the site and the story. Bethlehem Steel's Johnstown Works (closed 1992) was one of Pennsylvania's major steel mills, leaving behind brownfield sites that economic development programs have targeted for industrial redevelopment. Cambria County's I-99 corridor connects it to the Pennsylvania Turnpike and to the Pittsburgh metro area's economic influence. Conemaugh Valley Conservancy manages environmental monitoring IT for the watershed system where the 1889 flood occurred.

restrictions.name: Bedford County
restrictions.title: Bedford County PA — Bedford/Lincoln Highway/Allegheny Front/PA Turnpike/southern PA
restrictions.description: Bedford County in south-central Pennsylvania occupies the Allegheny Mountain section of the Ridge-and-Valley Appalachians. Bedford is the county seat — a historic town where George Washington passed through during the Whiskey Rebellion of 1794 and where the Lincoln Highway (the first transcontinental auto route, established 1913) follows one of Pennsylvania's most scenic mountain corridors. The Pennsylvania Turnpike's Bedford interchange is the primary access point for the southern mountains. Bedford County's tourism economy (resorts, ski areas, Bedford Springs Historic Hotel) and agricultural base create a mix of heritage and agritourism IT. The county's Allegheny Front position creates challenges for wireless coverage in the valleys.

restrictions.name: Armstrong County
restrictions.title: Armstrong County PA — Kittanning/Allegheny River/western PA/Pittsburgh exurb/natural gas
restrictions.description: Armstrong County in western Pennsylvania centers on Kittanning on the Allegheny River — a river town midway between Pittsburgh (40 miles south) and the Allegheny headwaters region. The county's natural gas production (part of the Appalachian Basin conventional gas production that predates the shale era — Pennsylvania's first natural gas well was completed in the region) gives it a legacy energy industry IT presence. Armstrong County's Allegheny River corridor is part of the Allegheny River Water Trail, creating recreation IT. The county's Pittsburgh exurban position creates commuter connectivity demand and suburban development patterns extending from the metro's northern fringe. Indiana University of Pennsylvania's Armstrong County campus provides community college-level education locally.

restrictions.name: Cameron County
restrictions.title: Cameron County PA — Emporium/least populous PA county/Elk State Forest/Driftwood
restrictions.description: Cameron County in north-central Pennsylvania is the state's least populous county — with fewer than 4,500 residents, it is one of the most sparsely settled counties east of the Mississippi River. Emporium is the county seat, a small lumber and railroad town on Driftwood Branch of the Sinnemahoning Creek. The county is almost entirely covered by Elk State Forest — one of Pennsylvania's largest state forests — with game lands and wilderness that provide habitat for the state's wild elk herd (one of the largest free-roaming elk herds in the eastern US). The remoteness and forested terrain create extreme broadband connectivity challenges that Pennsylvania's BEAD program addresses. Cameron County's institutional connections are through the Pennsylvania Department of Conservation and Natural Resources and DCNR recreation management IT.

restrictions.name: Cooper County
restrictions.title: Cooper County MO — Booneville/Missouri River/Katy Trail/antebellum heritage
restrictions.description: Cooper County in central Missouri sits on the Missouri River, home to Booneville — one of the oldest towns in Missouri (platted 1817), founded by Daniel Boone's sons and named for Daniel Boone. The Katy Trail State Park — built on the former Missouri-Kansas-Texas Railroad right-of-way along the Missouri River — passes through Cooper County as part of the longest rail-trail in the United States (240 miles). Cooper County's Missouri River bottomland agriculture and its position as a central MO county give it connectivity from the US-40/I-70 corridor. The county's antebellum plantation heritage and Civil War Civil War history (Booneville was the site of the first Union victory in Missouri) are documented in local historic preservation IT.

restrictions.name: Clinton County
restrictions.title: Clinton County MO — Plattsburg/NW Missouri/Truman Lake watershed/agribusiness
restrictions.description: Clinton County in northwest Missouri sits between the Kansas City metropolitan area and the Truman Lake reservoir system on the Grand River. Plattsburg is the county seat. The county's position in the Grand River watershed — which feeds Truman Lake (Harry S. Truman Reservoir, the Army Corps of Engineers' largest surface area reservoir in Missouri) — creates water management IT for the region. Northwest Missouri's row crop and livestock agriculture creates agricultural supply chain IT. Clinton County's proximity to the Kansas City metro (45 miles southwest of Liberty) gives it suburban and exurban connectivity patterns for a largely rural county.

restrictions.name: Chariton County
restrictions.title: Chariton County MO — Keytesville/Chariton River/Missouri River/General Sterling Price
restrictions.description: Chariton County in north-central Missouri sits at the confluence of the Chariton River and the Missouri River. Keytesville is the county seat and the birthplace of Sterling Price — the Confederate general who led the Army of Missouri in the most significant Confederate military campaign in Missouri history (Price's Raid, 1864). The county's agricultural economy features corn, soybeans, and cattle on the Missouri River bottomlands. Chariton County's rural position on the Little Dixie corridor (the Missouri River counties that had antebellum plantation agriculture and Southern cultural heritage) gives it a distinctive historical identity among Missouri's north-central counties. The Chariton River wildlife management areas support hunting and recreation IT.

restrictions.name: Benton County
restrictions.title: Benton County MO — Warsaw/Harry S. Truman Lake/Osage River/south-central MO
restrictions.description: Benton County in south-central Missouri contains Warsaw — the county seat on Harry S. Truman Lake (officially Harry S. Truman Reservoir), the Army Corps of Engineers' largest surface area reservoir in Missouri at over 55,000 acres. The dam and reservoir project, completed in 1979, required the relocation of Warsaw and flooded the original town site. The Army Corps of Engineers' Truman Lake management IT — including dam safety monitoring, water quality measurement, and flood control operations — represents significant federal computing infrastructure in this rural county. The Osage River arm of the reservoir extends into adjacent Hickory and St. Clair Counties. Benton County's tourism and recreation economy around the lake creates hospitality IT.

restrictions.name: Bayfield County
restrictions.title: Bayfield County WI — Washburn/Apostle Islands NL/Lake Superior/Red Cliff Ojibwe
restrictions.description: Bayfield County in northern Wisconsin contains the Apostle Islands National Lakeshore — 21 islands and 12 miles of mainland shoreline on Lake Superior, featuring sea caves, lighthouses (the highest concentration of lighthouses per square mile in the US), and old-growth forests. The NPS visitor management, lighthouse maintenance, and ice cave access IT (when Lake Superior freezes, the sea caves draw tens of thousands of winter visitors) create significant federal computing infrastructure. The Red Cliff Band of Lake Superior Chippewa's reservation is in Bayfield County — the tribe's operations include fishing rights IT (the Voigt Decision affirmed Great Lakes tribal treaty fishing rights), Red Cliff Casino, and tribal health services. Bayfield County is part of the Lake Superior shoreline broadband priority zone.

restrictions.name: Door County
restrictions.title: Door County WI — Sturgeon Bay/Door Peninsula/Lake Michigan/cherry orchards/resort
restrictions.description: Door County occupies the Door Peninsula — a narrow limestone ridge extending into Lake Michigan between Green Bay and the lake proper. Sturgeon Bay is the county seat. Door County is one of the Midwest's most popular resort destinations — its cherry orchards (producing a significant share of US tart cherry output), fish boils, lighthouses (more than any other US county), and Lake Michigan scenery attract over 2 million visitors annually to a county of 30,000 permanent residents. The county's tourism IT (hospitality management, cherry processing and shipping, ferry operations to Washington and Rock Islands) is substantial relative to its size. Door County's cellular and broadband connectivity challenges in northern portions are addressed by Wisconsin's rural broadband programs.

restrictions.name: Florence County
restrictions.title: Florence County WI — Florence/north Wisconsin/Iron Belt/Nicolet NF/Forest County adj
restrictions.description: Florence County in the far north of Wisconsin is one of the state's most remote and lightly populated counties — an iron and copper mining heritage county in the Wisconsin Iron Belt. Florence is the county seat on the Menominee River (which forms the Wisconsin-Michigan border). The Nicolet National Forest covers much of Florence County, with USFS management IT for timber, recreation, and wilderness. Florence County's mining heritage (iron ore from the Menominee Iron Range was significant in 19th-century iron production) has given way to recreation and forestry. The county's extreme northern position creates challenging broadband connectivity, addressed by Wisconsin's rural connectivity programs targeting the Iron and Forest county region.

restrictions.name: Crawford County
restrictions.title: Crawford County WI — Prairie du Chien/Wisconsin-Mississippi confluence/Effigy Mounds/Driftless
restrictions.description: Crawford County in southwest Wisconsin sits at the confluence of the Wisconsin and Mississippi Rivers at Prairie du Chien — one of the oldest European settlements in the Upper Midwest, a French fur trade post established in the late 17th century. The county is in the Driftless Area — the region of southwestern Wisconsin (and adjacent Minnesota, Iowa, and Illinois) that was never glaciated and features rugged bluffs, spring-fed streams, and exceptional topographic variety. Effigy Mounds National Monument (across the Mississippi in adjacent Iowa) and the Wisconsin mound complexes reflect the Native American Woodland cultures that dominated this landscape. The Wisconsin River's confluence with the Mississippi creates USGS monitoring IT for two of the continent's most important river systems.

restrictions.name: Box Butte County
restrictions.title: Box Butte County NE — Alliance/Nebraska Panhandle/Carhenge/Sandhills-Panhandle transition
restrictions.description: Box Butte County in the Nebraska Panhandle centers on Alliance — the largest city in the Nebraska Panhandle and home to Carhenge, a full-scale replica of Stonehenge constructed from vintage American automobiles (created by Jim Reinders in 1987). The county's position at the transition between the Nebraska Sandhills (to the east) and the High Plains Panhandle gives it agricultural diversity: cattle ranching, dryland wheat, and irrigated crops. Alliance is the hub for the Panhandle's healthcare, commercial, and governmental services, serving multiple surrounding counties. BNSF Railway's main line passes through Alliance, creating railroad maintenance and logistics IT. The county's Ogallala Aquifer irrigation monitoring and dryland agriculture extension services connect to USDA programs for the High Plains water management challenge.

restrictions.name: Cedar County
restrictions.title: Cedar County NE — Hartington/NE Nebraska/Missouri River adj/Czech heritage/Santee Sioux
restrictions.description: Cedar County in northeast Nebraska centers on Hartington, a small agricultural county seat in Nebraska's Czech heritage corridor — one of the most Czech-American regions in the United States, where towns like Crete, Wilber, and Clarkson celebrate Czech cultural traditions. The county borders South Dakota and sits adjacent to the Missouri River and the Niobrara River system. The Santee Sioux Tribe's reservation (in adjacent Knox County) extends cultural and tribal government IT into the region. Cedar County's row crop agriculture (corn, soybeans) and cattle feeding operations create agricultural supply chain IT. Nebraska's rural broadband deployment programs include Cedar County in the northeast Nebraska priority zone.

restrictions.name: Antelope County
restrictions.title: Antelope County NE — Neligh/Elkhorn River/north-central Nebraska/cattle and row crops
restrictions.description: Antelope County in north-central Nebraska centers on Neligh on the Elkhorn River — a principal tributary of the Platte River flowing through northeastern Nebraska's agricultural heartland. The county's cattle feeding, hog production, and row crop farming create agricultural processing and supply chain IT. Antelope County is part of the Northeast Nebraska Economic Development District's rural development programs. The Elkhorn River's watershed connects Antelope County to the broader Platte River system's agricultural water management network. Nebraska's rural broadband deployment through USDA ReConnect and state programs has included Antelope County in priority deployment zones for northeast Nebraska.

restrictions.name: Adams County
restrictions.title: Adams County IL — Quincy/Mississippi River/Nauvoo adjacent/Lincoln-Douglas debate/Civil War
restrictions.description: Adams County in western Illinois sits on the Mississippi River, with Quincy as the county seat — one of Illinois's most historically significant cities. The Lincoln-Douglas debate in Quincy (October 13, 1858) was the sixth of seven famous debates between Abraham Lincoln and Stephen Douglas over slavery and popular sovereignty. Nauvoo — the Mormon city established by Joseph Smith across the Illinois River in Hancock County — is adjacent, and its history is intertwined with Quincy's role as a haven for expelled Missouri Mormons in 1839. Quincy also had significant Underground Railroad activity during the antebellum era. The University of Illinois Quincy Medical District and Blessing Health System provide healthcare IT for western Illinois. Quincy's historic warehouse district and Mississippi River port create freight and logistics IT.

restrictions.name: Brown County
restrictions.title: Brown County IL — Mount Sterling/Siloam Springs State Park/Illinois River watershed
restrictions.description: Brown County in western Illinois centers on Mount Sterling, a small agricultural county seat in a region of rolling hills and small farms at the edge of the Illinois River watershed. Siloam Springs State Park in the county provides outdoor recreation IT. Brown County's agricultural base includes hog production, cattle, and row crops — typical of the western Illinois farm landscape. The county's proximity to Quincy (Adams County) and Macomb (McDonough County) gives residents access to regional services without requiring county-level infrastructure comparable to more urban areas. Illinois's rural broadband programs have included western Illinois counties in deployment priorities.

restrictions.name: Calhoun County
restrictions.title: Calhoun County IL — Hardin/Illinois-Mississippi confluence/least accessible IL county/apple orchards
restrictions.description: Calhoun County is a finger of land at the confluence of the Illinois and Mississippi Rivers, accessible only by ferry or a bridge from Missouri — making it one of the most geographically isolated counties east of the Mississippi. Hardin is the county seat. The county's peninsula position between the two rivers historically limited development and continues to challenge broadband connectivity (Calhoun County is persistently among Illinois's least-connected counties). Apple orchards have been the county's agricultural signature — Calhoun County produces a significant share of Illinois's apple crop, with orchards marketing through fall agritourism. The Pere Marquette State Park in adjacent Jersey County (accessible through Calhoun) manages visitor IT for one of Illinois's most visited state parks.

restrictions.name: Bradley County
restrictions.title: Bradley County TN — Cleveland/SE Tennessee/Ocoee River/Cherokee NF/I-75 industrial
restrictions.description: Bradley County in southeast Tennessee is home to Cleveland — a mid-sized industrial city on the I-75 corridor connecting Chattanooga (25 miles south) to Knoxville. The county's I-75 position has attracted manufacturing investment in chemicals (Cleveland is the headquarters of multiple chemical companies), automotive components, and distribution. Lee University and Cleveland State Community College provide higher education and workforce development. The Ocoee River — flowing through the Cherokee National Forest adjacent to Bradley County — was the site of the 1996 Summer Olympics whitewater canoe/kayak competition, one of the most unusual Olympic venues in US history. Bradley County's chemical manufacturing sector requires EPA environmental reporting and safety IT.

restrictions.name: Claiborne County
restrictions.title: Claiborne County TN — Tazewell/Cumberland Gap/VA-KY-TN junction/Wilderness Road
restrictions.description: Claiborne County in northeastern Tennessee contains Cumberland Gap — the natural mountain pass through the Cumberland Mountains that was the primary gateway to Kentucky and the trans-Appalachian West from 1775 until the railroads. Daniel Boone blazed the Wilderness Road through Cumberland Gap in 1775, and hundreds of thousands of settlers crossed through the gap in the following decades. Cumberland Gap National Historical Park spans the Virginia-Kentucky-Tennessee tri-state junction at the northern tip of Claiborne County. Tazewell is the county seat in the Powell Valley. The county's coal mining heritage (Virginia coalfields extend into northeast Tennessee) has transitioned as mines have closed, and ARC economic development programs address Claiborne County's persistent economic challenges.

restrictions.name: Campbell County
restrictions.title: Campbell County TN — Jacksboro/Norris Lake/Cumberland Mountain/coal mining heritage
restrictions.description: Campbell County in northeast Tennessee sits astride Cumberland Mountain — one of the most dramatic ridges in the Appalachians. Jacksboro is the county seat. Norris Lake (Norris Reservoir), created by the TVA's first dam (Norris Dam, completed 1936) — the first dam built by the TVA — provides flood control, power generation, and recreation. The New River Gorge of Tennessee (the New River flowing north through Campbell County) cuts through Cumberland Mountain in a rugged landscape that includes active and legacy coal mines. Campbell County's coal production created one of Tennessee's most characteristic Appalachian landscapes of company towns and mining communities that persist as the mines decline.

restrictions.name: Dubois County
restrictions.title: Dubois County IN — Jasper/wood furniture capital/German Catholic heritage/south-central IN
restrictions.description: Dubois County in south-central Indiana centers on Jasper — one of the most distinctive small industrial cities in Indiana. Jasper is the wood office furniture manufacturing capital of the United States — companies including MasterBrand Cabinets, Jasper Seating, and numerous wood products manufacturers have made Dubois County disproportionately important in American furniture and cabinet production relative to its population. The county's German Catholic heritage (settled by German immigrants in the 1840s-1860s) is visible in the architecture of Jasper's St. Joseph Catholic Church and the persistence of German festivals. Vincennes University's Jasper campus provides technical education for the county's manufacturing workforce. The Ohio River is 40 miles south, and Dubois County's furniture exports move through I-64.

restrictions.name: Crawford County
restrictions.title: Crawford County IN — English/Harrison Crawford State Forest/cave country/Blue River
restrictions.description: Crawford County in south-central Indiana is one of Indiana's most rugged and least-developed counties — a hilly forested landscape of limestone cave systems, the Blue River, and Harrison-Crawford State Forest (Indiana's largest state forest). English is the county seat. Wyandotte Caves — one of the largest cave systems east of the Mississippi — are within Crawford County in the state forest. The Blue River (Indiana's only federally designated Wild and Scenic River segment) flows through the county in one of Indiana's most ecologically intact stream corridors. Crawford County's extreme topography (relative to the flat Indiana agricultural landscape) creates persistent broadband connectivity challenges, making it one of Indiana's highest-priority rural broadband deployment counties.

restrictions.name: Daviess County
restrictions.title: Daviess County IN — Washington/Amish community/NW Indiana coal/White River corridor
restrictions.description: Daviess County in southwest Indiana centers on Washington — a small county seat on the White River in Indiana's coal and agricultural transition zone. The county hosts one of Indiana's significant Amish communities — the Daviess County Amish settlement is among Indiana's larger Amish populations, with distinctive agricultural practices (horse farming, no grid electricity) that create an unusual economic geography. The White River corridor provides agricultural water management IT. The Western Indiana coalfield extends into Daviess County, with legacy surface mining reclamation programs requiring environmental monitoring IT. Vincennes University's influence reaches into the county from adjacent Knox County. Indiana's rural broadband programs prioritize the White River watershed communities.

restrictions.name: Oldham County
restrictions.title: Oldham County TX — Vega/Texas Panhandle/Route 66/I-40/Alibates Flint Quarries
restrictions.description: Oldham County in the Texas Panhandle centers on Vega — a small town on I-40 (the Route 66 successor) at the heart of the Panhandle's High Plains. The county contains Alibates Flint Quarries National Monument — the only national monument in Texas, preserving quarries where Native Americans mined Alibates flint (a distinctive red, blue, and white agatized dolomite) for over 13,000 years. Flint from Alibates spread across the Great Plains trading networks and has been found at sites from Canada to Mexico. Oldham County's agricultural economy focuses on dryland and irrigated wheat, corn, and cattle. The I-40 corridor gives the county freight logistics connectivity to the Amarillo metro 40 miles east, with BNSF intermodal facilities serving the Panhandle economy.

restrictions.name: Scurry County
restrictions.title: Scurry County TX — Snyder/west Texas oil/Colorado River/Deep Rock/Permian Basin edge
restrictions.description: Scurry County in west Texas is home to Snyder and the Deep Rock oil field — one of the most productive oil fields in west Texas history. The Scurry Area Canyon Reef Operators Committee (SACROC) unit in Scurry County is one of the earliest and most significant CO2 enhanced oil recovery projects in the US, having been under continuous oil production since the 1940s and CO2 injection since 1972. The Deep Rock discovery in 1948 created an oil boom that transformed Snyder. The Colorado River (Texas headwaters, not the western Colorado) flows through the county. Scurry County's Permian Basin edge position connects it to the vast West Texas energy infrastructure corridor.

restrictions.name: McCulloch County
restrictions.title: McCulloch County TX — Brady/Heart of Texas/Brady Creek/geographic center TX
restrictions.description: McCulloch County in central Texas centers on Brady, which markets itself as the 'Heart of Texas' — Brady is the closest city to the geographic center of Texas. The county's position at the geographic heart of Texas gives it an agricultural and crossroads commercial role connecting the Hill Country (to the south), West Texas (to the west), and the Central Texas plains (to the north and east). Brady Creek flows through the county, providing water for the modest local agricultural economy (cattle, sheep, goats, pecans). McCulloch County's rural healthcare (Heart of Texas Memorial Hospital) and agricultural extension services anchor local institutional IT.

restrictions.name: Terrell County
restrictions.title: Terrell County TX — Sanderson/Trans-Pecos/most remote TX county/Amistad NRA adjacent
restrictions.description: Terrell County in the Trans-Pecos region of southwest Texas is one of the most remote and least populated counties in the United States, with fewer than 1,000 residents in a county larger than Rhode Island and Delaware combined. Sanderson is the county seat on the Southern Pacific mainline — a railroad water stop in the Chihuahuan Desert. The county borders Coahuila, Mexico, and its remote position creates significant Border Patrol operations with USBP IT infrastructure for surveillance and enforcement in one of the most difficult terrain environments on the US-Mexico border. Amistad National Recreation Area on the Rio Grande (adjacent to Val Verde County) extends its influence into the Terrell County region. The Pecos River enters the Rio Grande at the county's eastern edge.

restrictions.name: Daniels County
restrictions.title: Daniels County MT — Scobey/Hi-Line/extreme NE Montana/Saskatchewan border/dryland wheat
restrictions.description: Daniels County in extreme northeastern Montana sits on the Saskatchewan border — one of the most remote counties in the contiguous United States. Scobey is the county seat, a dryland wheat farming community on the Hi-Line (the railroad corridor following the 48th parallel). The county's Great Northern Railway heritage created a string of grain elevator towns that still define the Hi-Line's settlement pattern. Daniels County's dryland wheat production is entirely dependent on rain-fed agriculture with no irrigation, making it vulnerable to drought cycles. The county's proximity to the Canadian border creates CBP activity at the small border crossings. Montana's rural broadband programs target Hi-Line counties like Daniels as priority deployment areas for satellite and wireless connectivity.

restrictions.name: Musselshell County
restrictions.title: Musselshell County MT — Roundup/central Montana/coal mining/Musselshell River valley
restrictions.description: Musselshell County in central Montana centers on Roundup on the Musselshell River — a cattle and coal town that gave rise to the region's 'roundup' cattle gathering tradition (the county seat name reflects this heritage). The county had significant coal production from the Bull Mountain coalfield, which supplied fuel for Great Northern Railway locomotives. The Musselshell River flows eastward through the county in the Missouri River watershed, with the Musselshell River Arm of Fort Peck Reservoir creating water recreation IT. Montana's energy transition programs have targeted Musselshell County as coal mine reclamation work transitions the landscape. The county's cattle ranching heritage and agricultural water management create ongoing agricultural IT.

restrictions.name: Blaine County
restrictions.title: Blaine County MT — Chinook/Fort Belknap/Bear Paw Mountains/Chief Joseph surrender
restrictions.description: Blaine County in north-central Montana contains the Bear Paw Battlefield National Monument — the site where Chief Joseph of the Nez Perce surrendered to Colonel Nelson Miles on October 5, 1877, ending the Nez Perce War and the 1,170-mile flight toward Canada. His surrender speech ('From where the sun now stands, I will fight no more forever') is one of the most quoted statements in American history. The Fort Belknap Indian Community — comprising the Gros Ventre (Aaniiih) and Nakoda (Assiniboine) peoples — occupies a reservation in Blaine County, with tribal government, healthcare, and gaming enterprise IT. Chinook is the county seat on the Milk River, in the dryland wheat farming Hi-Line corridor.

restrictions.name: Carter County
restrictions.title: Carter County MT — Ekalaka/SE Montana badlands/least populous MT county/Custer NF
restrictions.description: Carter County in the far southeastern corner of Montana is among the least populated counties in the United States — with fewer than 1,400 residents in a county of 3,340 square miles, it has a population density of about 0.4 people per square mile. Ekalaka is the county seat, accessible only by two-lane roads in a region of badlands, buttes, and cattle ranches. The Medicine Rocks State Park's unusual sandstone formations were described by Theodore Roosevelt during his ranching years in the Badlands. Carter County's remote position has made it a persistent broadband challenge area targeted by USDA ReConnect programs. The Custer National Forest patches within the county add USFS management IT in one of Montana's most isolated landscapes.

restrictions.name: Hettinger County
restrictions.title: Hettinger County ND — Mott/SW North Dakota/Cannonball River/Slope County adj
restrictions.description: Hettinger County in southwest North Dakota centers on Mott on the Cannonball River — a wheat and cattle county in the Missouri Plateau. The county's position in southwestern North Dakota gives it a rougher terrain than the flat Red River Valley to the east — badlands topography and buttes characterize the region. The Cannonball River flows eastward to the Missouri River, and the county sits near the Standing Rock Sioux Reservation (to the west in Sioux County) that gained national attention during the Dakota Access Pipeline controversy of 2016. Basin Electric Power Cooperative's rural electric infrastructure serves Hettinger County's dispersed farms and ranches.

restrictions.name: Bowman County
restrictions.title: Bowman County ND — Bowman/SW corner ND/oil and gas/Badlands edge
restrictions.description: Bowman County occupies the extreme southwestern corner of North Dakota, bordering both South Dakota and Montana. Bowman is the county seat in a region where North Dakota's agricultural plains transition to the Badlands and the Black Hills uplift region. The county has oil and gas production from the Williston Basin, the same formation that drives North Dakota's petroleum economy further north and east in the Bakken shale. Bowman County's position at the tri-state corner creates a minor commercial crossroads for ranchers and travelers moving between the Dakotas and Montana. The county's isolation and small population (under 3,300) make it a priority in North Dakota's rural broadband connectivity efforts.

restrictions.name: Billings County
restrictions.title: Billings County ND — Medora/Theodore Roosevelt NP/North Unit Badlands/least populous ND
restrictions.description: Billings County is North Dakota's least populous county — with fewer than 1,000 residents, it contains the North Unit of Theodore Roosevelt National Park and the historic town of Medora, founded by the French aristocrat Marquis de Morès in 1883. Theodore Roosevelt ranched in the badlands immediately adjacent to what is now Billings County in the 1880s, and his time in North Dakota profoundly influenced his conservation philosophy that would shape the national park and national forest systems he created as president. Medora's Chateau de Morès Historic Site, the Badlands landscape that Roosevelt described as 'romance and adventure,' and the Theodore Roosevelt NP's visitor management IT make Billings County a significant NPS and heritage tourism computing location.

restrictions.name: Hertford County
restrictions.title: Hertford County NC — Winton/NE North Carolina/Chowan River/Roanoke-Chowan region
restrictions.description: Hertford County in northeastern North Carolina sits on the Chowan River in the Roanoke-Chowan region — one of North Carolina's most historically African American rural counties. Winton is the county seat, near the confluence of the Chowan and Wiccacon Rivers. The county's agricultural economy (soybeans, corn, tobacco, hogs) reflects the Coastal Plain agricultural heritage. Hertford County is part of the Roanoke-Chowan Area, where USDA ReConnect and FCC broadband programs have targeted persistent digital equity gaps in rural northeastern North Carolina. The county's position near the Virginia border and the Albemarle Sound drainage connects it to the coastal plain water quality monitoring systems.

restrictions.name: Pender County
restrictions.title: Pender County NC — Burgaw/NE NC/Cape Fear River/Camp Lejeune adjacent/Holly Ridge
restrictions.description: Pender County in southeastern North Carolina borders Onslow County (Camp Lejeune) and New Hanover County (Wilmington), in a zone of military influence, coastal development, and longleaf pine flatwoods. Burgaw is the county seat. The county's Holly Ridge community was the site of Camp Davis — a World War II antiaircraft artillery training base where the Women Airforce Service Pilots (WASPs) trained for towed target missions, one of the most unusual WWII military operations. The Cape Fear River forms the county's western border. Pender County's coastal proximity to Wilmington's growing metro and Camp Lejeune's military influence zone create suburban and military-adjacent connectivity demands. The county's longleaf pine flatwoods are part of important longleaf ecosystem restoration projects.

restrictions.name: Ashe County
restrictions.title: Ashe County NC — Jefferson/High Country NC/New River/oldest US river/Christmas trees
restrictions.description: Ashe County in the High Country of northwestern North Carolina is traversed by the New River — one of the oldest rivers in the world (among the few rivers that predate the Appalachian Mountains, flowing north). The New River is designated a National Scenic River through much of its Ashe County reach. Jefferson is the county seat. Ashe County is one of North Carolina's leading Christmas tree producers — the cool mountain climate and elevation (3,000-5,500 feet) is ideal for Fraser fir production, and the county's farms supply a significant share of the eastern US retail Christmas tree market. The county's High Country position (Blue Ridge Parkway nearby) and its tourism and agritourism economy create IT demand for a rural mountain county.

restrictions.name: Athens County
restrictions.title: Athens County OH — Athens/Ohio University/Hocking River/Appalachian Ohio/sustainable energy
restrictions.description: Athens County in southeastern Ohio is home to Athens and Ohio University — one of Ohio's oldest universities (founded 1804), with over 20,000 students and a significant research computing presence. Athens is considered one of Ohio's most progressive cities, with an active sustainability movement and Ohio University's Voinovich School of Leadership and Public Affairs contributing energy policy and Appalachian development research. The Hocking Hills region adjacent to Athens County includes Hocking Hills State Park (one of Ohio's most visited state parks). The Appalachian Highlands region of southeastern Ohio has persistent broadband connectivity challenges that Ohio's ConnectOhio broadband deployment program prioritizes. Athens County's connection to Appalachian Ohio's coalfield legacy creates a distinctive mix of progressive university culture and deindustrialized coal heritage.

restrictions.name: Richland County
restrictions.title: Richland County OH — Mansfield/Ohio State Reformatory/Shawshank/mid-Ohio industrial
restrictions.description: Richland County in north-central Ohio centers on Mansfield — a mid-sized industrial city historically important in steel, auto parts, and manufacturing. The Ohio State Reformatory in Mansfield was built in 1886 in Romanesque Revival style and achieved worldwide fame as the filming location for 'The Shawshank Redemption' (1994), drawing significant film tourism IT. The reformatory closed as a prison in 1990. Mansfield's manufacturing economy includes GM's Mansfield Metal Center and various auto parts suppliers. Richland County's position on the I-71 corridor between Columbus (75 miles south) and Cleveland (75 miles north) gives it strong freight and logistics connectivity. The Mid-Ohio Sports Car Course (racing venue) in adjacent Knox County extends IT demand into the region.

restrictions.name: Geauga County
restrictions.title: Geauga County OH — Chardon/Cleveland exurb/largest Ohio Amish population/maple syrup
restrictions.description: Geauga County in northeastern Ohio is home to the largest Amish population in Ohio (and one of the largest in the world) — the Geauga-Holmes Amish settlement is the oldest in Ohio (dating to 1886) and has grown to tens of thousands of Amish residents. Chardon is the county seat, a small upscale community in the Chagrin River watershed. Geauga County produces more maple syrup than any other Ohio county, with hundreds of maple syrup operations using traditional and modern tapping methods. The county's Cleveland exurban position (30 miles from downtown Cleveland) creates suburban residential IT demand while the Amish economy (no public utility electricity, horse-drawn transportation) creates a distinctive technological landscape contrast. Geauga County parks and the Headwaters Park system provide recreation IT.

restrictions.name: Noxubee County
restrictions.title: Noxubee County MS — Macon/NE Mississippi Black Belt/Noxubee NWR/Tombigbee watershed
restrictions.description: Noxubee County in northeastern Mississippi sits in the Black Belt — the dark, fertile prairie soil region that stretches from Alabama through Mississippi, historically one of the most cotton-productive and African American-majority landscapes in the US. Macon is the county seat. The Noxubee National Wildlife Refuge preserves one of the most significant bottomland hardwood wetland complexes in the Deep South, managed for wood duck, alligator, and bald eagle habitat with USFWS monitoring IT. The Tombigbee River watershed in Noxubee County connects to the Tennessee-Tombigbee Waterway system. The county's Black Belt agricultural heritage and its historic African American population majority make it part of the Deep South civil rights geography.

restrictions.name: George County
restrictions.title: George County MS — Lucedale/SE Mississippi/Red Creek NWR/timber/fastest-growing MS county
restrictions.description: George County in southeastern Mississippi is one of the state's fastest-growing counties — its position between Hattiesburg (to the northwest) and Mobile (Alabama, to the east) on I-59 has attracted residential and commercial development from both metro areas. Lucedale is the county seat. The county's pine timber industry remains significant, with paper company and timber REIT land management across much of the county's area. Red Creek is a scenic tributary of the Pascagoula River system, designated a Wild and Scenic River in the Desoto National Forest. George County's growth reflects the suburbanization dynamics of the I-59 corridor connecting the Gulf Coast metro areas, creating connectivity demand exceeding what the county's historical rural character would suggest.

restrictions.name: Jefferson Davis County
restrictions.title: Jefferson Davis County MS — Prentiss/SW-central Mississippi/longleaf pine/Bouie River
restrictions.description: Jefferson Davis County in south-central Mississippi centers on Prentiss, a small county seat in the transition zone between Mississippi's pine timber belt and the Delta. Named for the Confederate president who was born in adjacent Wilkinson County, Jefferson Davis County has an agricultural and timber economy. The Bouie River (a tributary of the Pearl River) provides water management context. The county's longleaf pine heritage — before 20th-century logging, the Piney Woods region including Jefferson Davis County was covered with longleaf pine savannas — connects to restoration programs addressing one of the most ecologically depleted forest types in the Southeast. Mississippi's rural broadband priority programs include Jefferson Davis County in south-central Mississippi deployment zones.

restrictions.name: Geneva County
restrictions.title: Geneva County AL — Geneva/Wiregrass region/Conecuh River/peanut farming/SE Alabama
restrictions.description: Geneva County in extreme southeastern Alabama sits in the Wiregrass region — named for the wiregrass plant that covered the native pine savanna before European settlement and agriculture. Geneva is the county seat on the Conecuh River. The Wiregrass region is Alabama's peanut belt, with Geneva County a significant peanut-producing county in one of the nation's top peanut-producing states. The county's position near the Florida Panhandle connects it to the northwest Florida economy and Destin-Fort Walton Beach metro area's workforce commuting zone. USDA's peanut programs, crop insurance IT, and agricultural extension services are significant institutional computing anchors for Geneva County's farming economy.

restrictions.name: Conecuh County
restrictions.title: Conecuh County AL — Evergreen/Conecuh National Forest/longleaf pine restoration/SW Alabama
restrictions.description: Conecuh County in southwestern Alabama is home to Evergreen and the Conecuh National Forest — one of the most active longleaf pine restoration areas in the Southeast. The Conecuh NF's Red Hills salamander habitat (one of the rarest salamanders in the US, found only in a 6-county strip of southwest Alabama) creates USFWS monitoring IT for a critically endangered endemic species. The Conecuh River flows through the county toward the Florida state line. The county's history includes significant African American population heritage and civil rights context. Alabama's rural broadband deployment programs include Conecuh County in southwest Alabama's priority deployment zones.

restrictions.name: Barbour County
restrictions.title: Barbour County AL — Eufaula/Lake Eufaula/Wallace birthplace/SE Alabama Black Belt
restrictions.description: Barbour County in southeastern Alabama is best known as the birthplace of George Wallace — four-term Alabama governor and 1968 presidential candidate who ran on a segregationist platform and later renounced his views. Eufaula is the county seat on the Chattahoochee River, which forms the Alabama-Georgia border; Lake Eufaula (Walter F. George Lake) is one of the most productive bass fishing lakes in the Southeast, driving a significant recreation and tournament fishing economy. Barbour County sits at the edge of the Black Belt (the dark prairie soil belt historically associated with cotton and African American population) and the Wiregrass region. The county's civil rights history, Wallace legacy, and Lake Eufaula tourism create a distinctive institutional IT profile.

restrictions.name: Butler County
restrictions.title: Butler County AL — Greenville/I-65 corridor/SW Alabama Black Belt/Camellia City
restrictions.description: Butler County in south-central Alabama centers on Greenville — located on I-65, the primary north-south corridor connecting Birmingham to Montgomery to Mobile. Greenville is known as the 'Camellia City' for its camellia gardens. The county's I-65 position creates freight logistics IT connecting the Alabama Black Belt to the Gulf Coast. Butler County's agricultural economy includes forestry, cattle, and row crops in the transition zone between the Black Belt prairie soils and the coastal plain pine forests. The county's proximity to Montgomery (40 miles north) gives it some metro influence without being a true suburb. Alabama Rural Electric Association serves the county's dispersed rural electricity customers.

restrictions.name: Grayson County
restrictions.title: Grayson County VA — Independence/Mount Rogers highest VA point/New River headwaters/Grayson Highlands
restrictions.description: Grayson County in far southwestern Virginia contains Mount Rogers — at 5,729 feet, the highest point in Virginia — and Grayson Highlands State Park, one of Virginia's most spectacular mountain parks with wild ponies grazing above timberline on bald summits. The New River (one of the world's oldest rivers) originates in Grayson County, flowing north through the county before entering North Carolina. The county's remote position in the Blue Ridge Mountains makes it one of Virginia's most geographically isolated counties, with ARC broadband and rural development programs targeting the connectivity gap. Jefferson National Forest covers extensive portions of the county. The Appalachian Trail passes through the Mount Rogers National Recreation Area in Grayson County.

restrictions.name: Dickenson County
restrictions.title: Dickenson County VA — Clintwood/far SW Virginia/Breaks Interstate Park/Appalachian coal
restrictions.description: Dickenson County in the far southwestern corner of Virginia is one of the state's most remote and mountainous counties — a coal-producing Appalachian county adjacent to Buchanan County and the Kentucky border. Clintwood is the county seat. The county shares the Breaks Interstate Park with neighboring Kentucky — the 'Grand Canyon of the South' (the deepest canyon east of the Mississippi River). Dickenson County's coal production, once a major employer, has substantially declined.
